stageDetails.watchNotes: Punchy terrain will test the sprinters. Breakaways have a chance if they build enough gap over the climbs. GC teams may use the hills to put pressure on rivals.

stages.name: Stage 2: Hilly Stage
stages.description: Punchy stage with uphill finish - favors attackers

stageDetails.courseSummary: Punchy stage with uphill finish - favors attackers
stageDetails.watchNotes: Punchy terrain will test the sprinters. Breakaways have a chance if they build enough gap over the climbs. GC teams may use the hills to put pressure on rivals.

stages.name: Stage 3: Hilly Stage
stages.description: Rolling terrain - sprint possible if fast men survive

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling terrain - sprint possible if fast men survive
stageDetails.watchNotes: Punchy terrain will test the sprinters. Breakaways have a chance if they build enough gap over the climbs. GC teams may use the hills to put pressure on rivals.

stages.name: Stage 4: Long Hilly Stage
stages.description: Longest stage of the race - attrition through distance and terrain

stageDetails.courseSummary: Longest stage of the race - attrition through distance and terrain
stageDetails.watchNotes: Punchy terrain will test the sprinters. Breakaways have a chance if they build enough gap over the climbs. GC teams may use the hills to put pressure on rivals.

stages.name: Stage 5: Mountain Stage
stages.description: Final stage through Andalusian mountains - last chance to reshape GC

stageDetails.courseSummary: Final stage through Andalusian mountains - last chance to reshape GC
stageDetails.watchNotes: Key GC day. Expect attacks on the final climb. Teams will set tempo to drop sprinters before the decisive section. Watch for long-range moves if multiple major climbs.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Vuelta a Andalucia (Ruta del Sol) is one of Spain's most historic stage races, held annually in February through the sun-baked roads of Andalusia. The challenging terrain with multiple climbs each day makes this a race for puncheurs and all-rounders rather than pure sprinters.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Expect aggressive racing throughout - there are few easy stages in this race. Spanish teams and riders are particularly motivated. Hot conditions in Andalusia add to the challenge.
narratives: Spanish teams targeting home race glory
Puncheurs and climbers have multiple opportunities
No hiding places - every stage is demanding

broadcast.notes: ProSeries race - check GCN/Eurosport for highlights coverage

races.videoNote: Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations to prevent spoilers.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Classic Var is a one-day race through the hilly terrain of the Var department in Provence, southern France. The race features a succession of short, punchy climbs through the Mediterranean hills, making it ideal for puncheurs and sprinters who can survive the terrain. It serves as early-season preparation for riders targeting the French spring classics.

puncheurs: Strong finishers who can handle the hilly terrain of Provence
sprinters: Fast finishers who can survive the climbs for a reduced bunch kick

raceDetails.historicalContext: The Classic Var is part of the early-season French racing calendar, taking place in the warm Mediterranean climate of the Var region. It attracts a mix of French continental teams and WorldTour squads using the mild February weather to sharpen early-season form.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The repeated climbs throughout the race wear down pure sprinters, often leaving a reduced group for the finale. Watch for attacks on the final hills - if the group stays together, it becomes a punchy sprint.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Grote Prijs Jean-Pierre Monsere is a 202.5km Belgian one-day race based in Roeselare, West Flanders. Named after the late Belgian world champion who died racing in 1971, the race follows a flat to mildly rolling course through the Flemish countryside. It typically favors fast finishers and sprinters, though the race's length and possible crosswinds add unpredictability.

sprinters: Fast finishers who can handle the long race distance
breakawaySpecialists: Aggressive riders who may exploit crosswinds in the flat Flemish terrain

raceDetails.historicalContext: Named in honor of Jean-Pierre Monsere, the Belgian world champion who tragically died in a racing accident in 1971 at just 22 years old. The race is held in his hometown of Roeselare and serves as a memorial to one of Belgium's most talented riders. It's part of the early Flemish racing calendar.
raceDetails.watchNotes: A flat race through Flanders that typically ends in a sprint, but crosswinds through the exposed Flemish polders can blow the race apart. Watch for echelon formation in windy conditions. The race's length at 202km can tire out pure sprinters.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Midwest Cycling Classic is a 137km women's one-day race held in Belgium. Despite its name suggesting an American event, this is a Belgian race that forms part of the early-season women's calendar. The race features a mix of flat and mildly rolling terrain typical of the Belgian Flemish region.

sprinters: Fast finishers looking for early-season wins
breakawaySpecialists: Aggressive riders who may exploit the race dynamics

raceDetails.historicalContext: The Midwest Cycling Classic is a relatively new addition to the women's Belgian racing calendar. It provides racing opportunities for the women's peloton during the early spring build-up to the major cobbled classics.
raceDetails.watchNotes: A relatively straightforward race profile that typically favors sprinters, though Belgian weather and wind can always add unpredictability. Watch for late attacks if conditions favor the aggressive riders.

stages.name: Stage 1
stages.description: Mountain stage through the Alpes-Maritimes

stages.name: Stage 2
stages.description: Closing mountain stage

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Tour des Alpes-Maritimes et du Var is a 2-stage race through the hills and mountains of the French Riviera hinterland. The race typically features challenging terrain in the Maritime Alps, with a hilly opening stage and a mountain stage with a summit or hilltop finish. The race serves as a stepping stone between early-season warm-weather racing and the spring classics.

keyClimbs.name: Col d'Eze
keyClimbs.description: Classic Riviera climb above Nice, frequently used in races along the Côte d'Azur

keyClimbs.name: Col de Vence
keyClimbs.description: Long climb behind Nice leading to the hilltop village of Vence

climbers: Lightweight climbers who excel on Mediterranean mountain roads
puncheurs: Strong riders who can handle the hilly terrain

narratives: Short stage race format means every climb matters for the overall classification
Teams use this as final preparation before bigger stage races like Paris-Nice or Tirreno-Adriatico
The French Riviera scenery provides a spectacular backdrop
raceDetails.gcDynamics: With only two stages, the GC is typically decided on the mountain stage. Bonus seconds on the first stage can be crucial. Climbers are favored overall.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The race combines the heritage of the former Tour du Haut Var and Tour des Alpes-Maritimes, consolidated into a single two-stage event. It takes place in the same region as Paris-Nice's mountain stages and features similar terrain.
raceDetails.watchNotes: With just 2 stages, the race is compact and aggressive from the start. The mountain stage is where the overall winner will emerge. Watch for attacks on the final climb.

stages.name: Prologue: BK Arena - Amahoro Stadium
stages.description: Opening prologue time trial in Kigali

stages.name: Stage 1: Rukomo - Kayonza
stages.description: Rolling stage through eastern Rwanda

stages.name: Stage 2: Kigali - Musanze
stages.description: Mountain stage toward the Virunga volcanoes

stages.name: Stage 3: Musanze - Gisenyi
stages.description: Stage from Musanze to Lake Kivu

stages.name: Stage 4: Lake Kivu
stages.description: Scenic stage along Lake Kivu

stages.name: Stage 5
stages.description: Mountain stage through the interior

stages.name: Stage 6

stages.name: Stage 7
stages.description: Penultimate mountain stage

stages.name: Stage 8: Kigali
stages.description: Final stage finishing in Kigali

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Tour du Rwanda is a prestigious 9-stage race through the 'Land of a Thousand Hills.' The race covers approximately 840km across Rwanda's dramatically hilly terrain, with stages featuring constant climbing and descending through the country's volcanic landscape. Racing at altitudes between 1,400-2,500m above sea level, the thin air and relentless terrain make this one of the most demanding races in Africa.

keyClimbs.name: Wall of Kigali
keyClimbs.description: Iconic finishing climb in the capital city - steep and decisive

keyClimbs.name: Col de Musanze
keyClimbs.description: Long mountain pass through volcanic terrain near the Virunga mountains

gcContenders: African climbing specialists and WorldTour development riders
stageHunters: Breakaway specialists and sprinters for flatter stages

narratives: Tour du Rwanda is Africa's biggest stage race and a key development event for African cycling talent
Altitude and relentless terrain test riders' endurance in ways unlike any European race
Continental and WorldTour development teams battle for supremacy
The passionate Rwandan crowds create an electric atmosphere, especially in Kigali
raceDetails.gcDynamics: The constant climbing means there are few hiding places for GC riders. Mountain stages create significant time gaps, and the altitude factor can surprise riders not acclimatized. The Wall of Kigali often features in the decisive stages.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1988, the Tour du Rwanda has grown from a regional event to a UCI 2.1 classified race attracting international teams. Rwanda's investment in cycling infrastructure and the race's growing prestige have made it a cornerstone of African cycling development. Many African riders who have gone on to WorldTour careers cut their teeth here.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Every stage features significant climbing due to Rwanda's terrain. Watch for attacks on the longer climbs where altitude takes its toll. The atmosphere is incredible, with huge crowds lining the roads. The race often features aggressive racing from start to finish.

races.name: Castellón Gravel Race
races.description: UCI Gravel World Series qualifier. Top 25% in each category earn entry to the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships (Nannup, Australia) and 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships (Houffalize, Belgium).

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gravel race in Llucena, Spain. Elite men's and elite women's classifications contest the same course.

broadcast.notes: UCI Gravel World Series events are typically not livestreamed; results and short recap clips appear on the UCI YouTube channel and event social media.

stages.name: Stage 5

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Giro di Sardegna is a multi-stage race across the Italian island of Sardinia. The race features a diverse mix of terrain, from coastal flat stages to challenging mountain stages through Sardinia's rugged interior. The island's windswept roads and punchy climbs create an unpredictable race where both sprinters and climbers can find opportunities.

keyClimbs.name: Sardinian Interior Climbs
keyClimbs.description: Short, steep climbs through the mountainous interior of the island

gcContenders: Stage race specialists and Italian riders using this as Giro d'Italia preparation
stageHunters: Sprinters for flat coastal stages, puncheurs for hilly stages

narratives: Early-season Italian stage race attracts riders preparing for the Giro d'Italia
Sardinia's varied terrain ensures no single rider type dominates
Wind exposure on coastal stages can split the peloton unexpectedly
raceDetails.gcDynamics: The mountain stages decide the GC, but wind on coastal stages can create unexpected splits. The 6-stage format allows for GC battles to develop over multiple days.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Giro di Sardegna has a long tradition as an early-season Italian stage race. It provides an important racing opportunity for Italian and continental teams while giving WorldTour riders a chance to build form in Mediterranean conditions.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch for wind-affected stages along the coast and mountain stages in the interior. The diverse terrain means different types of riders can shine on different days. The race often features aggressive racing from Italian continental teams seeking recognition.

races.name: Omloop Nieuwsblad Women
races.description: Opening Weekend classic through the Flemish Ardennes featuring iconic bergs like the Muur van Geraardsbergen and Bosberg

raceDetails.courseSummary: Omloop Het Nieuwsblad Women is the first major cobbled classic of the European season, traditionally marking the start of 'Opening Weekend' alongside the men's race and Kuurne-Brussel-Kuurne. The 130km route from Ghent to Ninove takes riders through the heart of the Flemish Ardennes, tackling the same iconic bergs (hellingen) and cobbled sectors that define the Tour of Flanders. The decisive Muur van Geraardsbergen and Bosberg climbs come inside the final 15km.

keyClimbs.name: Muur van Geraardsbergen (Kapelmuur)
keyClimbs.notes: The iconic 'Chapel Wall' - steep cobbles through woodland to the chapel at the top. Often race-deciding.

keyClimbs.name: Bosberg
keyClimbs.notes: Final climb with 9km to the finish. Long enough for attackers, close enough for chasers.

keyClimbs.name: Molenberg
keyClimbs.notes: Sharp berg that can trigger early selection

keyClimbs.name: Berendries
keyClimbs.notes: Technical berg before the final showdown

keySectors.name: Paddestraat
keySectors.description: First cobbled sector, sets the rhythm

keySectors.name: Lange Munte
keySectors.description: Long cobbled section through farmland

keySectors.name: Haaghoek
keySectors.description: Rough cobbles that can cause splits before the finale

cobbleSpecialists: Riders who thrive on the rough cobbled bergs
puncheurs: Explosive attackers who can power over the Muur
sprinters: Strong sprinters who can survive the hellingen
allRounders: Versatile riders who can attack and sprint

narratives: Opening Weekend marks the start of the 'real' European racing season
First test on the iconic Flemish climbs before Tour of Flanders
Belgian home riders seek glory on familiar roads
Cold, wet February conditions add to the challenge
Form indicator for the entire Spring Classics campaign
raceDetails.historicalContext: Omloop Het Nieuwsblad has traditionally marked the opening of the Spring Classics since 1945. Named after the Flemish newspaper Het Nieuwsblad, the race takes place in the heart of cycling country, on many of the same cobbles and bergs used in the Tour of Flanders. The women's edition has become a major WorldTour event, often producing surprise winners and dramatic racing.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The Muur van Geraardsbergen is where the race explodes - the steep cobbles shed pure sprinters and force attackers to play their cards. The Bosberg 9km from the finish is the last chance to escape. After the Bosberg, a 9km run-in to Ninove often comes down to a reduced group sprint. Cold conditions and tired legs from winter training can produce unexpected results.

primary.notes: Full live coverage and replays
Full live coverage and replays
Live coverage on TNT Sports (formerly BT Sport)
Free-to-air in Belgium on VRT/Een

broadcast.notes: Opening Weekend is a major event in Belgium with extensive local TV coverage. Women's race runs alongside the men's event.

races.videoNote: Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations to prevent spoilers.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Visit South Aegean GP is a one-day race held in the Dodecanese islands of Greece, part of the South Aegean region. The race features coastal roads and hilly terrain on the Greek islands, offering a unique Mediterranean racing experience. The combination of climbs and wind exposure on the island roads creates a challenging course.

puncheurs: Riders who excel on short Mediterranean climbs
allRounders: Versatile riders who can handle both climbs and wind

raceDetails.historicalContext: Part of a series of Greek cycling events in the Dodecanese and South Aegean islands. Greek cycling has been growing in profile with an increasing number of UCI-registered events, and the stunning island settings provide a unique backdrop for racing.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Island racing means wind exposure is a constant factor. The combination of climbs and crosswinds can split the race apart. Watch for attacks on the coastal sections where wind hits the exposed peloton.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Faun Drôme Classic is a French one-day race held in the Drôme department of southeastern France. Starting and finishing in Étoile-sur-Rhône, the 200km route traverses the rolling terrain between the Rhône Valley and the Vercors foothills. Despite its 'flat' classification, the parcours features several short climbs that can break up the peloton before the finale.

sprinters: Fast finishers capable of surviving selective climbs
puncheurs: Riders who can attack on late climbs
allRounders: Strong all-rounders using this as spring season opener

narratives: Early-season test for sprinters ahead of spring classics
French teams looking for home soil victories
Form indicator race for Paris-Nice preparation
raceDetails.historicalContext: Formerly known as the Classic Sud Ardèche, the race was rebranded as the Drôme Classic and has grown in stature as a key early-season race in France. It serves as preparation for both sprinters targeting Milan-San Remo and classics specialists.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch for breakaways on the final climbs trying to stay away from the bunch sprint. If the race comes together, expect a bunch sprint where positioning through the final kilometers is crucial.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Kuurne-Brussels-Kuurne is a classic Belgian one-day race held on the day after Omloop Het Nieuwsblad as part of the 'Opening Weekend' of Belgian cycling. The 200km race starts and finishes in Kuurne, West Flanders, traversing through Brussels and tackling multiple bergs and cobbled sectors. The race features the famous hellingen (short steep hills) of Flanders and selective cobbled sections that test spring classics fitness.

keySectors.name: Oude Kwaremont
keySectors.description: Classic Flemish cobbled climb - selective but not decisive at this point

keySectors.name: Koppenberg
keySectors.description: Short but brutal 22% gradient cobbles - often causes splits

cobbleSpecialists: Flanders specialists using this as Tour prep
puncheurs: Riders who can handle the hellingen
sprinters: Fast finishers with cobble survival skills

narratives: Second day of Opening Weekend tests tired legs from Omloop
Flandrien classics specialists fine-tuning form before Tour of Flanders
Weather often plays a major role - wind, rain, and cold add to difficulty
Can sprinters survive the cobbles for a bunch sprint?
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1901, Kuurne-Brussels-Kuurne is one of the oldest races in Belgium. The race gained prominence as the companion event to Omloop Het Nieuwsblad, together forming the unofficial 'Opening Weekend' that kicks off the Belgian spring classics season. Many riders use it as final preparation before the Spring Classics block.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The race can go multiple ways: breakaway success on the bergs, late attacks on the final climbs, or a reduced bunch sprint. Watch for the Koppenberg and Oude Kwaremont to create selection. Tired legs from Omloop the day before often leads to surprises.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Le Samyn des Dames is a 122km women's one-day race in the Hainaut province of Belgium, from Quaregnon to Dour. The race features a circuit around Dour with several short, steep cobbled climbs that test riders' classics credentials. The punchy finale with its hellingen (short cobbled hills) makes this race selective despite its modest distance.

keySectors.name: Cobbled climbs around Dour
keySectors.description: Short, steep cobbled hills that create selection in the finale

cobbleSpecialists: Women who excel on short, steep cobbled climbs
puncheurs: Strong finishers who can attack on the final hills

raceDetails.historicalContext: Le Samyn des Dames is the women's edition of Le Samyn, a Belgian semi-classic. The race is named after José Samyn, a Belgian cyclist who died in a racing accident. It serves as an important early-season Belgian cobbled race for the women's peloton, testing classics form ahead of the major spring campaigns.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The cobbled climbs around Dour are where the race is decided. Watch for attacks on the final laps of the circuit - the repeated efforts on short, steep cobbled hills wear riders down. An uphill finish adds to the selectivity.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Vuelta a Extremadura Femenina is a women's one-day race in the Extremadura region of western Spain. The race traverses the rolling terrain of this less-visited Spanish region, featuring agricultural landscapes and moderate climbs. It's part of the growing Spanish women's racing calendar.

puncheurs: Riders who handle the rolling terrain of Extremadura
sprinters: Fast finishers if the race comes together

raceDetails.historicalContext: The Vuelta a Extremadura Femenina is part of Spain's expanding women's race calendar. Extremadura, bordered by Portugal, is one of Spain's least densely populated regions and provides scenic racing through its rolling pastoral landscapes.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The rolling terrain means the race could go to either a reduced sprint or a late attack. Spanish early-March weather can be variable, adding an element of unpredictability.

stages.name: Stage 1: Herrera del Duque → Herrera del Duque
stages.description: Opening 18km prologue / short ITT in Herrera del Duque, inland Extremadura.

stageDetails.courseSummary: 18.44km opening time trial — first leader's jersey via race-against-the-clock.

stages.name: Stage 2: Pueblonuevo del Guadiana → Fuente del Maestre
stages.description: 133km across the Guadiana plains — rolling Extremadura terrain.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling 133km across central Extremadura. Breakaway or reduced bunch finish.

stages.name: Stage 3: Jerte → Jaraíz de la Vera
stages.description: Queen stage: 139km through the Valle del Jerte and La Vera with 2,633m of climbing.

stageDetails.courseSummary: GC decider — 139km, 2,633m of elevation through the Valle del Jerte and La Vera valley.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Where the race is decided — significant climbing packed into the closing kilometres.

races.name: Strade Bianche
races.videoNote: Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations to prevent spoilers.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Strade Bianche (men's) is a 215km one-day classic through the white gravel roads (strade bianche) of Tuscany. Starting from Siena, the men's course features 11 gravel sectors totaling over 60km of unpaved roads across the rolling Crete Senesi hills before the iconic uphill finish through Siena's medieval streets to Piazza del Campo. The race character demands gravel handling, sustained climbing, and tactical patience over the 5+ hours of racing.

keySectors.name: Sector 1 - Vidritta
keySectors.description: Opening gravel sector — shakes out the peloton early.

keySectors.name: Sector 2 - Bagnaia
keySectors.description: Rolling gravel with gradient changes.

keySectors.name: Sector 4 - La Piana
keySectors.description: Mid-race selection point — Tuscan hill country gravel.

keySectors.name: Sector 6 - Monte Sante Marie
keySectors.description: The most iconic Strade Bianche sector — 11.5km of rolling gravel with sustained climbing. Often the decisive launching point.

keySectors.name: Sector 9 - Colle Pinzuto
keySectors.description: Late-race climb — sets up the final shake-out before Le Tolfe.

keySectors.name: Sector 10 - Le Tolfe
keySectors.description: Final gravel sector with steep gradients — last chance for gravel specialists to attack.

keySectors.name: Via Santa Caterina
keySectors.description: Iconic final climb through Siena's narrow medieval streets — ramps up to 16% on worn brick surface into Piazza del Campo.

classicSpecialists: Riders with gravel + classics pedigree (Strade is the unofficial first Monument of the season).
Crossover gravel-and-road specialists who target this race as a season highlight.
puncheurs: Strong finishers who can power up Via Santa Caterina after 5+ hours of racing.
Pure climber-puncheur profiles handle the Monte Sante Marie attrition best.
climbers: Lighter riders who attack on the rolling gravel hills of the closing 50km.

narratives: Unofficial first Monument: the modern Strade has earned Monument-equivalent prestige since its 2007 debut.
Tuscan gravel selectivity: 60+ km of gravel rewards bold attacks on sectors 6-10 in the final 80km.
Piazza del Campo finish: the medieval shell-shaped square is among cycling's most spectacular finish-lines.
Weather wildcard: rain turns gravel into mud, makes descents treacherous, and rewards opportunistic late attacks.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Strade Bianche was inaugurated in 2007 as Eroica Pro and renamed Strade Bianche in 2008. It rapidly became one of the most prestigious one-day races on the men's calendar despite being only WT category — many riders and fans treat it as the unofficial first Monument of the season. The race showcases the unique white clay gravel roads of the Crete Senesi region of Tuscany and shares the same iconic Piazza del Campo finish with the women's event.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Decisive moves come on Sector 6 (Monte Sante Marie, 11.5km) onward — anyone hoping to win typically attacks in the final 80km. The race often comes down to a small group or solo attack on Le Tolfe before the Via Santa Caterina climb. Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations.

races.name: Strade Bianche (Women)
races.videoNote: Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations to prevent spoilers.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Strade Bianche Women is a 136km one-day classic through the stunning white gravel roads (strade bianche) of Tuscany. Starting from Siena, the race features 8 gravel sectors totaling over 30km of unpaved roads across rolling Tuscan hills before the brutal uphill finish through Siena's medieval streets to Piazza del Campo. The race character demands a combination of gravel handling, climbing ability, and tactical intelligence.

keySectors.name: Sector 1 - Vidritta
keySectors.description: Opening gravel sector, used to shake out the peloton

keySectors.name: Sector 2 - Bagnaia
keySectors.description: Rolling gravel with some gradient changes

keySectors.name: Sector 3 - Radi
keySectors.description: Long gravel sector that begins to thin the peloton

keySectors.name: Sector 5 - San Martino in Grania
keySectors.description: The longest sector - decisive terrain where attacks often stick

keySectors.name: Sector 8 - Le Tolfe
keySectors.description: Final gravel sector with steep gradients, last chance for gravel specialists

keySectors.name: Via Santa Caterina
keySectors.description: The iconic final climb through Siena's narrow streets - steep ramps of up to 16% on worn brick surface

classicSpecialists: Riders with gravel and classics pedigree who can handle the terrain
puncheurs: Strong finishers who can power up the Via Santa Caterina
climbers: Lighter riders who can attack on the gravel hills

narratives: The women's Strade Bianche has rapidly become one of the most prestigious one-day races on the WWT calendar
The unpredictable Tuscan gravel roads reward boldness - late attackers on sectors 5-8 often decide the race
The Piazza del Campo finish is among the most spectacular in cycling - narrow medieval streets into the shell-shaped square
Weather can transform the race - rain turns gravel roads into mud, making descents treacherous and attacks more rewarding
raceDetails.historicalContext: Strade Bianche Women was first held in 2015 and quickly became a marquee event on the women's calendar. The race showcases the unique white clay gravel roads of the Crete Senesi region of Tuscany. It shares the same iconic Piazza del Campo finish with the men's event and has produced some of the most dramatic finishes in women's cycling.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Key moments come on sectors 5-8 in the final 50km where the gravel gets more demanding and the hills steeper. The race often comes down to a small group or solo attack on the final gravel sectors before the Via Santa Caterina climb. Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations.

races.name: Paris-Nice

stages.name: Stage 1: Achères - Carrières-sous-Poissy
stages.description: Punchy opener with two ascents of the steep Côte de Chanteloup-les-Vignes in the final 30km, favoring puncheurs over pure sprinters.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling terrain with four category three climbs in the first 100km. The decisive feature is the double ascent of Côte de Chanteloup-les-Vignes in the final 30km. Steep gradients make this a challenging opener for the sprinters.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Chanteloup-les-Vignes
keyClimbs.notes: Ascended twice in final 30km

stages.name: Stage 2: Épône - Montargis
stages.description: Sprinters' best opportunity with three early categorized climbs and potential crosswinds through exposed Gâtinais sections.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The sprinters' stage of Paris-Nice 2026. Three categorized climbs are confined to the first 120km, providing breakaway opportunities. The exposed Gâtinais plains could create crosswind splits before a likely bunch sprint in Montargis.

stages.name: Stage 3: Cosne-Cours-sur-Loire - Pouilly-sur-Loire (TTT)
stages.description: Team time trial across rolling Nièvre terrain with intermediate time check at Saint-Laurent-L'Abbaye (14km).

stageDetails.courseSummary: A 23.5km team time trial over rolling terrain in the Nièvre region. Intermediate time check at Saint-Laurent-L'Abbaye at 14km. The road rises to Saint-Andelain before descending toward Pouilly-sur-Loire. Individual GC times taken at the finish.

stages.name: Stage 4: Bourges - Uchon
stages.description: Predominantly flat until the final 40km, finishing with the demanding category one Signal d'Uchon (8km at 4.5%, pitches to 16%).

stageDetails.courseSummary: A long day that stays flat for 155km before the road tilts upward for the decisive finale. The category two Côte de la Croix des Cerisiers leads into the category one Signal d'Uchon summit finish, a significant GC battleground.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Croix des Cerisiers
Signal d'Uchon
keyClimbs.notes: Summit finish, decisive GC climb

stages.name: Stage 5: Cormoranche-sur-Saône - Colombier-le-Vieux
stages.description: The longest stage at 206.3km with three steep climbs after the intermediate sprint, including the brutal Côte de Saint-Jean-de-Muzols (2.2km at 11%).

stageDetails.courseSummary: The race's longest stage packs its punch in the second half. After the intermediate sprint, three steep climbs will test the peloton. The Côte de Saint-Jean-de-Muzols (2.2km at 11%) and Côte de Saint-Barthélémy-le-Plain (3.2km at 7.6%) are particularly demanding, with the road climbing to the finish line.

stages.name: Stage 6: Barbentane - Apt
stages.description: Four categorized climbs with the final Côte de Saignon (4km at 5%) cresting 4.5km from the finish, rewarding skilled descenders into Apt.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A Provençal hilly stage with four categorized climbs concentrated in the second half. The first 100km are nearly flat before the Col de l'Aire Deï Masco (7.1km at 4.4%) and the final Côte de Saignon (4km at 5%) summit 4.5km from the finish. The sharp descent into Apt rewards skilled descenders.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Saignon
keyClimbs.notes: Final climb before descent to finish

stages.name: Stage 7: Nice - Auron
stages.description: Summit finish at the Auron ski station with a final ascent of 7.3km at 7.2%, the penultimate stage GC showdown.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A mountain stage finishing at the Auron ski station. Climbing begins from the outset with the Côte de Carros and Côte de Bouyon, followed by an 80km transitional section with minimal elevation. The final ascent to Auron (7.3km at 7.2%) is the major GC battleground of the race.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Carros
keyClimbs.notes: Early climb from Nice

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Bouyon
keyClimbs.notes: Second early climb

keyClimbs.name: Auron
keyClimbs.notes: Summit finish at ski station

stages.name: Stage 8: Nice - Nice
stages.description: Final stage with three categorized climbs including the punishing Côte du Linguador (3.3km at 8.8%, max 14%), the last chance to shake up GC before Nice.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The final stage loops through the hills around Nice with nearly 2,800m of climbing. Three categorized climbs define the day: Col de la Porte (7km at 7.2%), Côte de Châteauneuf-Villevieille (6.6km at 6.6%), and the fearsome Côte du Linguador (3.3km at 8.8%, max 14%). Less than 20km remains after the final summit, allowing continued attacks before crowning the overall winner.

keyClimbs.name: Côte du Linguador
keyClimbs.notes: Final categorized climb, less than 20km from finish

races.videoNote: Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations to prevent spoilers.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Paris-Nice — La Course au Soleil (Race to the Sun) — 8 stages over the second week of March from the Paris region to the Côte d'Azur. The longest-running professional stage race after the Grand Tours (since 1933), the race is traditionally the first major GC stress test of the European season. The route mixes early flat stages around Paris, hilly middle stages, a decisive mid-week ITT, and Alpine summit-finishes in the closing days. The Col de la Couillole and Col de Turini have been recent classic GC-deciders.

keyClimbs.name: Mont Brouilly
keyClimbs.notes: Classic Beaujolais climb featured on the mid-race rolling stages.

keyClimbs.name: Col de la Couillole
keyClimbs.notes: Recent Paris-Nice queen-stage summit finish — Maritime Alps climb above Nice.

keyClimbs.name: Col de Turini
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic Maritime Alps climb featured on the closing weekend stages.

gcContenders: Top GC riders use Paris-Nice as the first major Spring GC test on the road to the Grand Tours.
Climbers + TT specialists balance favoured on the mixed 8-stage profile.
stageHunters: Sprinters target the opening flat stages in the Paris basin.
Puncheurs hunt the hilly middle days.
Climbing specialists target the Maritime Alps closing weekend.

narratives: Race to the Sun: traditional opener of the European GC season — Paris in March, Nice on the Côte d'Azur.
Tour de France barometer: GC favourites use Paris-Nice as their first big Spring test.
Sister race to Tirreno-Adriatico: held the same week with the WT peloton split between the two — top GC stars typically pick one.
Closing-weekend Alpine drama: the GC is decided in the Maritime Alps with summit finishes producing race-winning attacks.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Paris-Nice has been held since 1933 — one of the oldest and most prestigious stage races outside the Grand Tours. Sean Kelly holds the record with 7 wins. The race is owned by ASO (Tour de France organisers) and serves as the Spring counterpart to Tirreno-Adriatico in the WT calendar.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Key stages: the mid-race ITT (stage 4 typically) reshapes GC; closing-weekend Maritime Alps summit stages decide the maglia jaune. Pure sprinters target stages 1-3, climbers target the final 3. Disable autoplay; avoid sidebar.

races.name: Gravel Brazil
races.description: UCI Gravel World Series qualifier. Top 25% in each category earn entry to the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships (Nannup, Australia) and 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships (Houffalize, Belgium).

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gravel race in Camboriú, Brazil. Elite men's and elite women's classifications contest the same course.

broadcast.notes: UCI Gravel World Series events are typically not livestreamed; results and short recap clips appear on the UCI YouTube channel and event social media.

races.name: Tirreno-Adriatico

stages.name: Stage 1: Lido di Camaiore (ITT)
stages.description: Opening individual time trial along the Tuscan coast, a flat out-and-back effort with a U-turn at Marina di Pietrasanta.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A flat 11.5km individual time trial at Lido di Camaiore with just 8 metres of elevation gain. An out-and-back effort along the same carriageway with a U-turn at Marina di Pietrasanta. An S-bend provides the sole technical challenge near the finish. Pure time trial specialists favored.

stages.name: Stage 2: Camaiore - San Gimignano
stages.description: Hilly stage through Tuscany featuring gravel roads and an uphill finish reaching 14% gradient in San Gimignano, suited for puncheurs.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A demanding Tuscan stage with a categorized climb to Castelnuovo Val di Cecina followed by transitional flatness. The finale features gravel roads leading to a punchy uphill finish in San Gimignano reaching 14% gradient. Puncheurs and classics specialists favored.

stages.name: Stage 3: Cortona - Magliano de' Marsi
stages.description: The longest stage at 221km with deceptive undulation in the final 50km, mainly designed for sprinters despite the distance.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The longest day of Tirreno-Adriatico 2026 at 221km with 2,436m of climbing. Deceptively undulating, particularly in the final 50km, but mainly designed for the sprinters to contest. The sheer distance and rolling terrain could cause fatigue-related splits.

stages.name: Stage 4: Tagliacozzo - Martinsicuro
stages.description: Two Apennine climbs and sharp walls including the Badetta with 20% gradients before descending to the Adriatic coast finish.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A demanding stage crossing the Apennines with 2,781m of climbing. Two major climbs at Ovindoli and Valico delle Capannelle set up the midrace action. Short, sharp walls including Badetta (20% gradients) punctuate the descent to the seafront finish in Martinsicuro.

keyClimbs.name: Ovindoli
keyClimbs.notes: First major Apennine climb

keyClimbs.name: Valico delle Capannelle
keyClimbs.notes: Second Apennine ascent

keyClimbs.name: Badetta
keyClimbs.notes: Short steep wall on descent to coast

stages.name: Stage 5: Marotta-Mondolfo - Mombaroccio
stages.description: Brutal stage with 3,900m of climbing on a two-lap circuit, finishing with the Santuario del Beato Sante climb just 1.6km from the line.

stageDetails.courseSummary: One of the toughest stages with 3,900m of climbing packed into 184km. A two-lap circuit features the Santuario del Beato Sante climb, with the final summit just 1.6km before the finish. Barely a metre of flat ground all day. A key GC battleground designed for the pure climbers.

keyClimbs.name: Santuario del Beato Sante
keyClimbs.notes: Tackled twice on circuit, summit finish virtually

stages.name: Stage 6: San Severino Marche - Camerino
stages.description: Final GC showdown with 3,900m of climbing, featuring the Madonna delle Carceri wall (18% gradients) tackled twice on a two-lap circuit.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The decisive GC stage with a massive 3,900m of elevation gain over 188km. A two-lap circuit features the fearsome Madonna delle Carceri wall with gradients reaching 18%, tackled twice. The finish atop the wall determines the overall race winner.

keyClimbs.name: Madonna delle Carceri
keyClimbs.notes: Tackled twice on two-lap circuit, summit finish

stages.name: Stage 7: Civitanova Marche - San Benedetto del Tronto
stages.description: Traditional sprinters' finale along the Adriatic coast with five flat laps and a technical final 3km featuring sweeping bends.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The traditional Tirreno-Adriatico flat finale. Five flat laps along the Adriatic coast with just 1,100m of total climbing. The final 3km includes sweeping bends and a double bend near the finish, adding technical difficulty to the sprint showdown in San Benedetto del Tronto.

races.videoNote: Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations to prevent spoilers.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Tirreno-Adriatico — Corsa dei Due Mari (Race of the Two Seas) — 7 stages running from the Tyrrhenian coast across central Italy to the Adriatic. Held the same week as Paris-Nice, Tirreno is the sister race that traditionally hosts the Italian-leaning WT GC favourites. The route mixes coastal sprint stages, hilly Apennines middle days, summit finishes on Mount Sasso Tetto or similar, and the iconic San Benedetto del Tronto closing ITT (Tridente Trophy).

keyClimbs.name: Sasso Tetto
keyClimbs.notes: Apennines summit finish — recent Tirreno queen-stage GC-decider.

keyClimbs.name: Monte Petrano
keyClimbs.notes: Italian Apennines summit finish — alternative GC-deciding day.

keyClimbs.name: San Benedetto del Tronto ITT (final stage)
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic closing-day flat ITT — the Tridente Trophy finale.

gcContenders: Italian-leaning WT GC stars who pick Tirreno over Paris-Nice — Pogačar, Roglič, Vingegaard era riders.
Climbers favoured on the Apennines summit finishes balanced by TT-strong riders for the closing Tridente.
stageHunters: Sprinters target stages 1-2 along the Tyrrhenian coast.
Puncheurs hunt the hilly Apennines transitional stages.
Pure TT specialists target the Tridente final-stage ITT.

narratives: Race of the Two Seas: Tyrrhenian Coast to Adriatic, crossing central Italy.
Sister race to Paris-Nice: held the same week with WT GC stars typically split between the two.
Italian Spring tradition: Tirreno serves as the lead-in to Milano-Sanremo for many sprinters and classics riders.
Tridente Trophy finale: the closing flat ITT in San Benedetto del Tronto produces iconic GC drama.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Tirreno-Adriatico has been held since 1966. Roger De Vlaeminck holds the record with 6 wins. The race is owned by RCS Sport (Giro d'Italia organisers) and serves as the Italian counterpart to Paris-Nice in the Spring WT calendar.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Key stages: the Apennines summit finishes (stages 5-6 typically) decide GC; the Tridente Trophy ITT finale (stage 7) can flip the standings on the closing day. Disable autoplay; avoid sidebar.

stages.name: Stage 1: Taipei City → Taipei City
stages.description: Opening Taipei circuit — 80km, low elevation. Sprinters' day in the capital.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Short opening circuit (495m elevation) through Taipei. First leader's jersey on a bunch sprint.

stages.name: Stage 2: Taoyuan City → Jiaobanshan Park
stages.description: Climb to Jiaobanshan Park — 124km, 1,195m elevation. First GC test.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Hilly transfer to the Jiaobanshan summit. Climbers and puncheurs in the mix.

stages.name: Stage 3: Fo Guang Shan Buddha Museum → Kaohsiung National Stadium
stages.description: 146km hilly day from the Fo Guang Shan Buddha Museum to Kaohsiung — 1,316m elevation.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling southern Taiwan stage finishing at Kaohsiung National Stadium.

stages.name: Stage 4: Gaoshu → Liudui Hakka Cultural Park
stages.description: Flat 130km stage through Pingtung — sprinters' day, just 286m elevation.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Flattest day of the race — bunch sprint expected at the Liudui Hakka Cultural Park.

stages.name: Stage 5: Luye Township → Liyu Lake
stages.description: Final 154km stage from Luye Township to the scenic Liyu Lake. 895m elevation — closing GC opportunities.

stageDetails.courseSummary: East-coast Taiwan finale with rolling terrain. Last chance for GC contenders to make moves.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Milano-Sanremo is the longest one-day race on the WT calendar — approximately 290km from Milano (with a Pavia start) to Sanremo on the Ligurian Riviera. The Classicissima is decided by the closing 30km, where the Cipressa (5.6km at 4.1%) and especially the Poggio di San Remo (3.7km at 4%, summit 9km from finish) thin the field. The Poggio descent into Sanremo is a treacherous technical run-in where the winning move often sticks. Sprinters who survive the climbs, puncheurs who attack on the Poggio, and breakaway specialists all have legitimate cards to play.

keyClimbs.name: Capi (Capo Mele, Capo Cervo, Capo Berta)
keyClimbs.notes: Three short Ligurian coastal climbs in the closing 50km — used to thin the bunch before the Cipressa/Poggio.

keyClimbs.name: Cipressa
keyClimbs.notes: First decisive climb — launching point for ambitious puncheurs hoping to drop sprinters early.

keyClimbs.name: Poggio di San Remo
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic decisive climb of the race — short, not steep, but the only chance for puncheurs to drop sprinters before the line. Summit 9km from the finish.

classicSpecialists: POGAČAR Tadej (SVK / UAE Team Emirates - XRG) — defending World Champion targets the only Monument he has not yet won.
VAN DER POEL Mathieu (NED / Alpecin-Premier Tech) — 2023 Sanremo winner; the Poggio descent suits his classics power.
GANNA Filippo (ITA / Ineos Grenadiers) — Italian power rider with sustained-effort engine for solo Poggio attacks.
sprinters: PHILIPSEN Jasper (BEL / Alpecin-Premier Tech) — 2024 Sanremo winner; the perfect Poggio-survivor profile.
PEDERSEN Mads (DEN / Lidl - Trek) — durable classics-sprinter who survives the climbs and contests the line.
MERLIER Tim (BEL / Soudal Quick-Step) — pure sprinter banking on a bunch finish.
puncheurs: ALAPHILIPPE Julian (FRA / Tudor Pro Cycling) — French puncheur with Poggio-attack form historically.
MOHORIČ Matej (SLO / Bahrain - Victorious) — descender-puncheur who has won Sanremo via the Poggio descent.

narratives: La Classicissima: the longest and oldest of the Monuments (first held 1907) and the season's first Monument.
The Sprinter's Classic: traditionally suits sprinters who can survive the Poggio, though Pogačar's 2024-2025 era has reshaped the calculus.
Final-10km drama: Cipressa 22km from line, Poggio 9km from line, descent into Via Roma — every tactical card plays in these closing minutes.
Weather and wind: cold March Riviera weather + mistral risk in the Capi section can transform the race.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Milano-Sanremo is the oldest of the five Monuments, first held in 1907. Known as La Classicissima or La Primavera (the Spring), it opens the Monument season every March. The race traditionally favours sprinters who can survive the Cipressa and Poggio climbs, but puncheurs and breakaway specialists with bold tactics on the Poggio have produced famous solo wins. Eddy Merckx still holds the record with 7 victories.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The race is dormant for the first 250km — tune in for the closing 50km when the Capi-Cipressa-Poggio sequence decides everything. The Poggio descent into Sanremo is technical and high-stakes. Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Milano-Sanremo Donne — the women's Classicissima, returning to the WWT calendar in 2026 after a previous hiatus. Approximately 132km along the Ligurian Riviera, finishing in Sanremo with the same iconic Cipressa-Poggio closing sequence as the men's race. The shorter distance favours classics-sprinters and puncheurs, but the closing climbs still produce the decisive selection.

keyClimbs.name: Cipressa
keyClimbs.notes: Shared with men's race — first decisive launching point.

keyClimbs.name: Poggio di San Remo
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic Poggio — same decisive climb as men's race. Summit 9km from finish.

classicSpecialists: Top WWT classics-sprinters who handle hilly finishes — Wiebes, Kopecky, Vos profiles fit.
Italian home-roads riders use this as a marquee season target.
puncheurs: Puncheurs who can attack on the Poggio to drop pure sprinters.

narratives: Returning Classic: Milano-Sanremo Donne returns to the WWT calendar after a hiatus — a milestone for women's Monument-tier racing.
Same iconic finale: the Cipressa-Poggio closing sequence is the same dramatic finale that defines the men's race.
Shorter but selective: 132km is shorter than men's 290km, but the closing climbs still determine the winning move.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The women's edition of Milano-Sanremo was previously held as a separate race (Primavera Rosa, 1999-2005) before being discontinued. Its return to the WWT calendar in 2025-2026 marks a major addition to the women's Monument-tier calendar.
raceDetails.watchNotes: As with the men's race, the action is in the closing 50km. Watch the Poggio for puncheur attacks aiming to drop the sprinters before the Via Roma finish.

races.name: Turnhout Gravel
races.description: UCI Gravel World Series qualifier. Top 25% in each category earn entry to the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships (Nannup, Australia) and 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships (Houffalize, Belgium).

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gravel race in Turnhout, Belgium. Elite men's and elite women's classifications contest the same course.

broadcast.notes: UCI Gravel World Series events are typically not livestreamed; results and short recap clips appear on the UCI YouTube channel and event social media.

races.name: Princess Maha Chakri Sirindhorn's Cup Tour of Thailand
races.description: UCI 2.1 stage race in northeastern Thailand across Nong Khai and Udon Thani provinces. 6 stages, 816.6 km. Named in honor of HRH Princess Maha Chakri Sirindhorn.

primary.notes: Official broadcaster. Free daily live streams from 08:50 AM ICT. Dual Thai-English commentary. Multi-View camera angles on VIPA.me app.

alternatives.notes: Multi-view camera angle selection via app

primary.notes: Free YouTube live stream, appears geo-unrestricted. Starts 08:50 AM Bangkok time (01:50 UTC). Dual Thai-English commentary.

alternatives.notes: Official program page with episode links

raceDetails.courseSummary: 6-stage race through Nong Khai and Udon Thani provinces in northeastern Thailand, along the Mekong River. Three flat stages bookend three hilly stages in the middle, with the queen stage to Phu Foi Lom National Park. Total distance 816.6 km.

gcContenders: Alexander Salby (defending champion)
Luke Mudgway
Norman Vahtra
Peerapol Chawchiangkwang
stageHunters: Pierre Barbier
Eduard-Michael Grosu
Oskar Nisu
Sarawut Sirironnachai

narratives: Can Alexander Salby defend his 2025 title for Li Ning Star?
Pierre Barbier arrives in form after winning GP Alaiye — can he add sprint stage wins?
20 teams from 12 nations showcase Asian continental cycling's depth
Stage 3 summit finish at Phu Foi Lom expected to decide the GC
raceDetails.historicalContext: Thailand's premier cycling race, first held in 1999 and named in honor of HRH Princess Maha Chakri Sirindhorn. Upgraded to UCI 2.1 category in 2017. Historically won by a mix of Asian and Oceanian riders, with recent champions including riders from Mongolia, Netherlands, Poland, and Denmark.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Free live streams available daily on Thai PBS YouTube channel starting 08:50 AM Bangkok time (01:50 UTC). Dual Thai-English commentary. Stages 1-2 and 6 favor sprinters; Stages 3-5 in hilly terrain decide the GC.
raceDetails.gcDynamics: The race splits into three phases: flat opening (Stages 1-2), hilly middle (Stages 3-5) where the GC is decided, and a flat finale (Stage 6). Stage 3's summit finish at Phu Foi Lom is the key selection point.

stages.name: Stage 1 - Nong Khai Circuit
stages.description: Opening circuit stage around Nong Khai via Tha Bo and Mueang districts.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Circuit stage starting and finishing in Nong Khai, looping through Tha Bo and Mueang districts. Flat terrain suited to sprinters.
stageDetails.watchNotes: First stage to establish the race hierarchy. Look for sprint teams controlling the peloton. Time bonuses at the finish important for GC.

stages.name: Stage 2 - Phon Phisai to Nong Khai
stages.description: Longest flat stage from Phon Phisai to Nong Khai.

stageDetails.courseSummary: 150 km flat stage from Phon Phisai district to Nong Khai. The longest stage of the race, expected to favor the sprinters.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Long flat stage likely ending in a bunch sprint. Watch for breakaway attempts in the heat.

stages.name: Stage 3 - Nong Khai to Phu Foi Lom
stages.description: Queen stage finishing at Phu Foi Lom National Park in Udon Thani province.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The decisive stage crossing from Nong Khai into Udon Thani province, finishing at the hilltop of Phu Foi Lom National Park. The only summit finish of the race.
stageDetails.watchNotes: This is the queen stage where the GC will be decided. The climb to Phu Foi Lom will split the field. Expect attacks from GC contenders.

stages.name: Stage 4 - Nong Khai to Si Chiang Mai
stages.description: Hilly stage from Nong Khai to Si Chiang Mai district.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Undulating stage heading to Si Chiang Mai district near the Mekong River. Rolling terrain offers opportunities for puncheurs and attackers.
stageDetails.watchNotes: GC riders must stay alert on the rolling terrain. Breakaway opportunities for those who lost time.

stages.name: Stage 5 - Si Chiang Mai Circuit
stages.description: Hilly circuit race around Si Chiang Mai.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Circuit stage around Si Chiang Mai with rolling terrain. Multiple laps offer repeated selective sections.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Penultimate stage — last chance for GC riders to make up time. Circuit format means repeated climbs could cause attrition.

stages.name: Stage 6 - Nong Khai Circuit
stages.description: Final circuit stage in Nong Khai. Traditional sprinter finale.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Closing circuit stage in Nong Khai city. Flat terrain for a sprinter's finale to close out the race.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Final stage typically sees the GC settled with the peloton riding conservatively. Sprint teams will fight for the stage win.

stages.name: Stage 1: Barbaresco → Barolo
stages.description: Wine-country opener through the Piemonte Langhe — 161km from Barbaresco to Barolo.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Hilly Langhe vineyards stage with 2,414m of climbing. La Morra-style punchy finish.
stageDetails.watchNotes: First leader's jersey via uphill sprint or late attack.

stages.name: Stage 2: Lodi → Massalengo
stages.description: 158km Po valley stage from Lodi to Massalengo — sprinters' chance.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Flat Lombardian plains stage. 1,364m elevation — minor speed bumps in an otherwise sprint day.

stages.name: Stage 3: Erbusco → Iseo
stages.description: 176km Franciacorta wine country into the Iseo lakes — 1,703m elevation.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling Lombard lakes stage finishing on Lake Iseo. Punchy late terrain.

stages.name: Stage 4: Ponte di Piave → Valdobbiadene
stages.description: 160km Veneto stage finishing in Prosecco country at Valdobbiadene.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling Prosecco hills — Strade Bianche-veterans country. 1,360m elevation.

stages.name: Stage 5: Cormons → Gemona del Friuli
stages.description: Queen stage finale — 166km from Cormons to Gemona del Friuli, 2,353m of climbing.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Friulian Alps closer — 2,353m elevation. GC settled in the closing climbs.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Race finale with substantial mountain climbing. Final GC decision.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Ronde van Brugge — Brugge-based 200km one-day Belgian classic with a mix of flat coastal sections and short Flemish kickers. The route showcases medieval Brugge's cobbled streets in the finishing circuit, with the closing kilometres typically producing a reduced bunch sprint or late breakaway finish.

keyClimbs.name: Belgian Flemish kickers
keyClimbs.notes: Series of short steep cobbled climbs typical of the Belgian one-day calendar.

classicSpecialists: Belgian and Dutch classics riders historically dominate the Ronde van Brugge.
Riders targeting the Belgian one-day classics week as a season highlight.
sprinters: Survivors of the punchy circuit contest the reduced sprint in Brugge.

narratives: Brugge city-centre finish: medieval cobbled streets through one of Europe's best-preserved medieval cities.
Belgian classics week: positions in the Holy Week leading up to Flanders.
Flat-to-rolling terrain: not as selective as Flanders but tactical positioning still decides.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Ronde van Brugge is one of the Belgian one-day classics in the springtime classics calendar, run during the lead-up to the Tour of Flanders.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch the closing 20km in the city-centre circuit for late attacks or sprint positioning. Disable autoplay; avoid sidebar.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Ronde van Brugge Women — Brugge-based one-day Belgian classic on flat-to-rolling terrain. The route showcases medieval Brugge's cobbled streets in the finishing circuit, with the closing kilometres typically producing a reduced bunch sprint or late breakaway finish.

keyClimbs.name: Belgian Flemish kickers
keyClimbs.notes: Series of short steep cobbled climbs typical of the Belgian one-day calendar.

classicSpecialists: Belgian and Dutch classics riders historically dominate the Ronde van Brugge.
Riders targeting the Belgian one-day classics week as a season highlight.
sprinters: Survivors of the punchy circuit contest the reduced sprint in Brugge.

narratives: Brugge city-centre finish: medieval cobbled streets through one of Europe's best-preserved medieval cities.
Belgian classics week: positions in the Holy Week leading up to Flanders.
Flat-to-rolling terrain: not as selective as Flanders but tactical positioning still decides.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Ronde van Brugge is one of the Belgian one-day classics in the springtime classics calendar, run during the lead-up to the Tour of Flanders.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch the closing 20km in the city-centre circuit for late attacks or sprint positioning. Disable autoplay; avoid sidebar.

raceDetails.courseSummary: E3 Saxo Classic is the dress rehearsal for the Tour of Flanders — a 200km Belgian Flemish classic from Harelbeke featuring 17+ Flemish hellingen including the iconic Oude Kwaremont, Paterberg, and Taaienberg cobbled climbs. The race typically produces the same winners and tactics that will play out at Flanders the following weekend.

keyClimbs.name: Oude Kwaremont
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic cobbled Flemish climb featured in both E3 and Flanders — opening launching point for the closing 40km.

keyClimbs.name: Paterberg
keyClimbs.notes: Brutal short cobbled wall — 20%+ ramps. Last climb before the closing run-in.

keyClimbs.name: Taaienberg
keyClimbs.notes: Cobbled Flemish climb with steep gradients — traditional move-launching point for Boonen-style attacks.

classicSpecialists: VAN DER POEL Mathieu (NED / Alpecin-Premier Tech) — multi-time E3 winner; tune-up for Flanders.
VAN AERT Wout (BEL / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — Belgian home-roads classics specialist.
PEDERSEN Mads (DEN / Lidl - Trek) — durable classics rider who survives the hellingen.
puncheurs: POGAČAR Tadej (SVK / UAE Team Emirates - XRG) — explosive climber-puncheur targeting Flemish classics.
PIDCOCK Thomas (GBR / Pinarello-Q36.5) — punchy climber for repeated short steep climbs.

narratives: Flanders rehearsal: same hellingen as Tour of Flanders, run a week before — produces the same favourites.
Cobbled-and-steep terrain: 17+ climbs in 200km wears down legs over the hellingen-heavy closing 50km.
Belgian heartland: roadside fans pack the cobbled climbs from dawn — atmosphere unmatched on the calendar.
raceDetails.historicalContext: E3 Saxo Classic (formerly E3 BinckBank Classic / Harelbeke) traces back to 1958. Long considered the most important Flanders tune-up, it shares the same Flemish hellingen and traditionally produces the same winning profiles. Tom Boonen holds the record with 5 victories.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Decisive moves typically come from the Oude Kwaremont onward (30-40km from finish). Watch for solo attacks on the Paterberg ramps and the run-in to the finish in Harelbeke. Disable autoplay; avoid sidebar.

races.name: 114 Gravel Race
races.description: UCI Gravel World Series qualifier. Top 25% in each category earn entry to the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships (Nannup, Australia) and 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships (Houffalize, Belgium).

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gravel race in Elvas – Badajoz, Portugal. Elite men's and elite women's classifications contest the same course.

races.name: Gent-Wevelgem

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gent-Wevelgem In Flanders Fields (rebranded for 2026) is a 250km Belgian classic from Middelkerke to Wevelgem along the coastal-and-Flemish-hill route. The race features multiple ascents of the iconic Kemmelberg cobbled climb plus the new Plugstreets gravel section — a mixed-terrain finale that suits sprinter-classics riders and puncheurs equally.

keyClimbs.name: Kemmelberg
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic cobbled climb with 23% ramps — featured 3x on the closing laps. The decisive launching point of the race.

keyClimbs.name: Baneberg
keyClimbs.notes: Shorter Flemish climb paired with Kemmelberg on the closing circuit.

keyClimbs.name: Plugstreets gravel sectors
keyClimbs.notes: Gravel/dirt sectors honoring WWI memorial battlefields — mixed-surface challenge in the closing 70km.

classicSpecialists: VAN DER POEL Mathieu (NED / Alpecin-Premier Tech) — multi-time Gent-Wevelgem winner.
VAN AERT Wout (BEL / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — Belgian home-roads veteran.
PEDERSEN Mads (DEN / Lidl - Trek) — survives the hellingen, contests the sprint.
sprinters: PHILIPSEN Jasper (BEL / Alpecin-Premier Tech) — top survivor-sprinter for the reduced bunch finish.
MERLIER Tim (BEL / Soudal Quick-Step) — pure sprinter for the bunch finish if the Kemmelberg fails to split.

narratives: In Flanders Fields rebrand for 2026: race now called In Flanders Fields, honoring the WWI memorial significance of the Plugstreets gravel sectors.
Wind + Kemmelberg + Plugstreets: three distinct race-shaping variables make this a tactical chess match.
Coastal echelon risk: the opening 100km along the Belgian coast can flip the race if onshore wind blows.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Gent-Wevelgem dates to 1934 — one of the oldest Belgian classics. The Plugstreets gravel sectors were introduced in 2017 to honor the WWI memorial significance of the Heuvelland region. The 2026 edition starts the In Flanders Fields rebrand emphasizing the war memorial heritage of the route.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Decisive moments: the second-to-last Kemmelberg ascent + Plugstreets gravel sections. Sprint finish in Wevelgem expected unless a solo attacker has timed it perfectly. Disable autoplay; avoid sidebar.

races.name: Dwars Door Vlaanderen

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 80th edition of Dwars door Vlaanderen ran 185km from Roeselare to Waregem on April 1, serving as the traditional Wednesday curtain-raiser to the Tour of Flanders. The 2026 course added two new climbs (12 total, two more than 2025) and seven cobblestone sections across the Flemish Ardennes. The finale featured double ascents of Berg Ten Houte, Côte de Trieu, and Hotond, plus twice over the Mariaborrestraat cobbles approaching the finish circuit. The new climb of Onderbossenaarstraat (77km to go, 1.4km climb with 10% ramps on a flank of the Taaienberg) added fresh difficulty. The familiar finishing circuit includes a double loop via Nokereberg and Herlegemstraat.

favorites.description: Dwars door Vlaanderen has evolved from a sprinter's race into a selective classics-style event with aggressive racing typical of the Belgian cobbled campaign. It favours versatile one-day riders who can survive multiple cobbled and climbing efforts while retaining power for the finishing circuit in Waregem.
rouleurs: Mathieu van der Poel (Alpecin-Deceuninck) - if starting, the overwhelming favourite
Wout van Aert (Visma-Lease a Bike) - cobbled classics staple, always a contender
Mads Pedersen (Lidl-Trek) - former world champion with the power for Dwars

narratives: The penultimate stop of 'Flemish Holy Week' - teams use Dwars to test form and tactics before the Tour of Flanders on Sunday
The 2026 course additions (Hellestraat, Onderbossenaarstraat) made the race more selective - trending away from the sprinters' finish it once was
Long-range attackers thrive when teams get caught looking at each other — a recurring pattern in modern editions
The twin-loop finishing circuit in Waregem via Nokereberg/Herlegemstraat adds a tactical wrinkle - late attacks or sprint setup
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1945, Dwars door Vlaanderen joined the UCI WorldTour in 2017 and in 2018 moved to its current Wednesday pre-Flanders slot (taking over from the Three Days of De Panne). Historically a sprinter's race, it has evolved dramatically since joining the WorldTour - Christophe Laporte's 2023 solo victory and Neilson Powless's 2025 win reflect the modern template of aggressive, selective racing. The race functions as a key form-indicator for the Monument on Sunday.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Tune in for the final 90 minutes - the race opens up on the second pass of Berg Ten Houte (71.6km to go). Critical viewing from the second Hotond (50km to go) through the Waregem circuit finale. Watch for late attacks on Nokereberg and Herlegemstraat. Spoiler caution: this race concluded on April 1 - avoid YouTube sidebar and autoplay as extended highlights often include race results and winner announcements.

races.name: Dwars Door Vlaanderen (Women)

raceDetails.courseSummary: Dwars door Vlaanderen joined the Women's WorldTour in 2026 for its inaugural WWT edition, a major milestone for the race. The course took the peloton across the Flemish Ardennes with six cobbled sectors and eight sharp climbs. The Hellestraat was the only new climb added to the 2026 route. Key climbs include Berg Ten Houte, Knokteberg-Trieu, Hotond, Eikenberg, and the Nokereberg (climbed twice in the finishing circuit). The cobbled sectors include Holleweg, Mariaborrestraat, Doorn, Huisepontweg, and the Herlegemstraat (also twice). The finish takes place in Waregem after a double loop of the Nokereberg-Herlegemstraat circuit.

favorites.description: The women's Dwars door Vlaanderen favours powerful classics specialists with the bike handling for cobbles and punch for the bergs. As the mid-week race before the Tour of Flanders, form here signals for Sunday's Monument.

Staple of the Spring Classics since 2017 - the race has been building toward WWT status for years

raceDetails.historicalContext: Dwars door Vlaanderen Women has been a staple of the women's calendar since 2017. In 2026 it was elevated to the Women's WorldTour - a significant milestone reflecting the race's growing prestige. It occupies the same mid-week pre-Flanders slot as the men's race and serves as a form indicator for the Monument on Sunday. Previous editions have produced a wide range of winners from classics specialists to punchy sprinters.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Tune in for the final hour when the Flemish Ardennes climbs begin. The race typically opens up on the second pass of key climbs and the finishing circuit in Waregem. The Nokereberg-Herlegemstraat combination in the final 20km is usually where the decisive move goes. Spoiler caution: this race concluded on April 1 - avoid YouTube sidebar, autoplay, and results-focused content.

races.name: Ronde Van Vlaanderen

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 Tour of Flanders ran 271km from Antwerp to Oudenaarde, the longest version of Belgium's biggest race in recent years. The route featured six flat cobbled sections and 16 'hellingen' - the short, steep cobbled climbs that define the Ronde. The finale centered on the Kwaremont-Paterberg-Koppenberg triangle in the Flemish Ardennes: the Oude Kwaremont was climbed three times (final pass at 16.7km to go), the Paterberg twice (final at 13.3km to go), plus the brutal Koppenberg at 45.3km to go. The race is traditionally decided on the final Kwaremont-Paterberg combination, with a 13km run-in to the finish in Oudenaarde after the final Paterberg.

favorites.description: The Tour of Flanders rewards complete classics specialists - raw power for the cobbled bergs, bike handling through narrow lanes, tactical awareness over 270km, and a sharp finishing kick from a small group. Modern winners are almost always among a handful of generational talents.
rouleurs: Mathieu van der Poel (Alpecin-Deceuninck) - multiple-time winner and the modern king of the Ronde
Tadej Pogačar (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) - 2023 winner, always a threat when targeting the cobbled Classics
Mads Pedersen (Lidl-Trek) - former world champion with Flanders-specific power
Wout van Aert (Visma-Lease a Bike) - perennial podium threat, multiple times runner-up
puncheurs: Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Deceuninck) - Van der Poel's wingman and a serious threat in his own right
Tom Pidcock (Q36.5 Pro Cycling) - explosive puncheur capable of surviving the bergs and sprinting
Stefan Küng (Groupama-FDJ United) - rouleur with long-range attack pedigree

narratives: Antwerp hosts the start for 2026 - returning the race to its historic start city with an extended 271km route
The Kwaremont-Paterberg combination at 16.7km and 13.3km to go remains the traditional decider
Team tactics in the long approach to the final 100km are critical - strong teams protect leaders through the early bergs
Weather in early April in Flanders is notoriously variable - crosswinds, rain, and cold can transform the tactical picture
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1913, De Ronde van Vlaanderen (Tour of Flanders) is Belgium's biggest one-day race and the second Monument of the cycling calendar. It has finished in Oudenaarde since 2012. Eddy Merckx, Johan Museeuw, Tom Boonen, and Fabian Cancellara share the record with three wins each (Tom Boonen also has three; Cancellara's three came in 2010, 2013, 2014). Mathieu van der Poel has recently joined the three-wins club with consecutive victories. The finish in Oudenaarde after the Kwaremont-Paterberg finale has produced some of cycling's most iconic duels and long-range solos.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Flanders is nearly seven hours - tune in from the first Kwaremont (136km to go, roughly 4h in) for tension, or skip to the Koppenberg (45km to go) for the iconic climb action. The critical TV window is the final 75 minutes from the second Kwaremont to the finish. The Kwaremont-Paterberg finale at 16.7km/13.3km to go is destination television. Spoiler caution: highlights and thumbnails often reveal the outcome - disable autoplay and avoid YouTube sidebar.

races.name: Ronde Van Vlaanderen (Women)

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 Tour of Flanders Women covered 164.1km starting and finishing in Oudenaarde, taking in six flat cobblestone sections and nine climbs - seven of which are cobbled. The Wolvenberg (the only tarmac climb) opens the climbing at 82km into the race. The decisive Flemish Ardennes finale includes the Kerkgate cobbles and the Molenberg (70km from finish), where selections traditionally begin. The closing sequence follows the men's template with the Kwaremont-Paterberg combination as the final deciders - the race is usually won by a solo rider or a small group contesting the finish line in Oudenaarde after the final Paterberg. The route winds through the so-called Flemish Ardennes with its iconic cobbled bergs.

favorites.description: The Tour of Flanders Women rewards the same complete classics specialist profile as the men - power on the bergs, bike handling through narrow Flemish lanes, positioning into each cobbled sector, and a tactical eye for the decisive moments in the final 20km. The race has been dominated by generational talents in recent years.
rouleurs: Lotte Kopecky (SD Worx-Protime) - multi-time winner and the modern queen of the Ronde
Demi Vollering (FDJ-SUEZ) - European champion with the power and climbing for the cobbled bergs
Pauline Ferrand-Prévot (Visma-Lease a Bike) - multi-discipline superstar with Classics pedigree
Marlen Reusser (Movistar) - rouleur capable of long-range solo moves
puncheurs: Elisa Longo Borghini (UAE Team ADQ) - former winner, Monument-level consistency
Puck Pieterse (Fenix-Deceuninck) - explosive cyclocross-bred talent
Lorena Wiebes (SD Worx-Protime) - fast finisher who can survive a reduced sprint
Elisa Balsamo (Lidl-Trek) - powerful finisher with Classics pedigree

narratives: Demi Vollering's arrival at FDJ-SUEZ shifted the tactical landscape - her rivalry with Lotte Kopecky has defined recent Ronde editions
The race typically breaks open on the Molenberg at 70km to go - watch for the key selection there
Weather in early April can transform Flanders - cold rain and crosswinds shape tactics dramatically
Long-range solos have become the template for winning - expect moves from 40km or beyond
raceDetails.historicalContext: The women's Tour of Flanders was first held in 2004 and joined the Women's WorldTour calendar in 2016. It is one of the most prestigious one-day races on the women's calendar and shares its iconic Flemish Ardennes finale with the men's race. Recent winners include Lotte Kopecky (multiple times), Demi Vollering, Elisa Longo Borghini, and others. The race typically produces aggressive, long-range racing given the short distance relative to the men's event - attackers must commit early to avoid the final sprint.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Tune in for the final 90 minutes - the race opens up on the Wolvenberg (82km to go) and the Molenberg (70km to go) is historically where the winning move is launched. The critical TV window is the final hour from Berg Ten Houte (40km to go) through the final Kwaremont/Paterberg. Spoiler caution: avoid YouTube sidebar and autoplay as highlights often reveal the finish.

races.videoNote: FloBikes 2026 Tour of Flanders / Ronde van Vlaanderen Femmes full race broadcast (3:12:32). raceDetails.courseSummary: Cholet Agglo Tour (formerly Cholet-Pays de la Loire) is a French UCI 1.1 one-day race held April 6, 2026 in the Pays de la Loire region around the town of Cholet. The ~195km parcours rolls through the Mauges hills of western France, with multiple short climbs and a finishing circuit in central Cholet typically producing a reduced-bunch or small-group finish.

favorites.description: A French Pro Series classic drawing French ProTeams, Belgian classics squads and WorldTour spring riders. Puncheurs and strong sprinters with climbing legs typically win.

raceDetails.historicalContext: Cholet-Pays de la Loire / Cholet Agglo Tour has been held since 1978 and is a staple of French Pro Series racing. Past winners include French Classics specialists and WorldTour rouleurs.

races.name: Itzulia Basque Country

raceDetails.courseSummary: Itzulia Basque Country (Vuelta al País Vasco) is a six-stage UCI WorldTour race that ran April 6-11, 2026, starting in Bilbao and finishing in Bergara. The total distance was around 809km across unrelentingly hilly Basque Country terrain. The 2026 edition followed the traditional formula: five punishing road stages loaded with short, steep climbs, plus an individual time trial. Basque roads are narrow, technical and often wet at this time of year, making Itzulia one of the hardest one-week races on the calendar.

keyClimbs.name: Alto de Usartza
keyClimbs.notes: Short, brutally steep Basque climb

keyClimbs.notes: Classic San Sebastián-area climb

favorites.description: Itzulia is one of the top-tier Ardennes/Classics warm-ups, drawing every major GC rider and Classics specialist who can climb. Pure rouleurs struggle; the race rewards climbers who can also handle short, explosive finishes and a time trial.

Ardennes specialists using Itzulia as a sharpener for Amstel/Flèche/Liège

TT specialists targeting the individual time trial stage

narratives: One of the hardest one-week races on the calendar - attrition is relentless

Basque weather (wet, cold) often a decisive factor
Final-week Giro d'Italia preparation for many GC riders
raceDetails.historicalContext: Itzulia Basque Country dates to 1924 and is a fixture of the WorldTour. Past winners include Alberto Contador, Alejandro Valverde, Primož Roglič, Jonas Vingegaard and other all-time greats. The race is famous for its passionate Basque fans and short, vicious climbs.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Coverage on Eurosport/GCN+ in Europe, ETB Basque public TV, and various WorldTour broadcast partners. Highlights on the Itzulia YouTube channel. Stages are short by WorldTour standards but packed with climbing - expect a climbing spectacle every day.
raceDetails.gcDynamics: GC typically determined by a combination of the ITT and the last two mountain stages. Time gaps between top climbers are small, so bonus seconds and late attacks matter. Crosswinds on exposed Basque roads can also reshape GC unexpectedly.

races.name: Région Pays de La Loire Tour

raceDetails.courseSummary: Région Pays de la Loire Tour is a French UCI 2.1 stage race held April 7-9, 2026 across the Pays de la Loire region. The 2026 edition followed its traditional three-stage format: two flat-to-rolling road stages favouring sprinters and puncheurs, plus an individual time trial that typically decides GC. The race is a popular sprint and early-season GC showcase for French Pro Teams and WorldTour squads.

favorites.description: Pays de la Loire Tour rewards fast rouleurs with TT ability - pure climbers rarely appear. The race draws French Pro Teams, Belgian cobbled-classic specialists and WorldTour sprinters/rouleurs.
sprinters: Top French and Belgian sprinters targeting the flat stages
WorldTour fast men using the race for spring form
gcContenders: Rouleurs with strong TT ability - TT specialists typically win overall
Puncheurs who can survive flat stages and gain on rolling TT courses

narratives: Sprint opportunities vs. GC-deciding time trial - classic Loire formula

Loire Valley wind a constant factor on exposed stages
raceDetails.historicalContext: Dating from 1974 (as Circuit de la Sarthe), the race evolved into a Région Pays de la Loire event that attracts strong French and European teams as part of the Spring stage-race circuit. Past winners skew toward strong rouleurs and TT specialists.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Coverage via French regional TV and highlights on organiser YouTube. Watch the time trial stage closely - it typically decides GC.
raceDetails.gcDynamics: GC almost always comes down to the TT stage. Flat stages rarely produce GC shakes unless echelons form. Bonus seconds at intermediate sprints and stage wins can also tip a tight GC.
