races.name: Scheldeprijs

raceDetails.courseSummary: Scheldeprijs (Schelde Prize) is a Belgian UCI Pro Series one-day race held April 8, 2026, running from Terneuzen (Netherlands) to Schoten (Belgium) over 202.8km. Traditionally known as 'the sprinters' Classic', it's one of the flattest races on the cobbled-Classics calendar, featuring short sections of cobbled roads and one iconic passage of the Broekstraat kasseien, before finishing with three laps of a Schoten finishing circuit. The race almost always ends in a bunch sprint, making it the premier pure-sprint opportunity of the Flemish spring.

keySectors.name: Broekstraat kasseien
keySectors.description: Main cobbled sector - can cause splits but usually reabsorbed before the finish

favorites.description: Scheldeprijs is the fastest sprinters' race of the Classics season. It has been called the unofficial World Championship for sprinters. Winners are always top WorldTour sprinters with strong positioning teams.
sprinters: Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Deceuninck) - multi-time winner and Flemish home favourite
Tim Merlier (Soudal-QuickStep) - recent multi-time winner with lightning-fast kick
Dylan Groenewegen (Team Jayco AlUla) - big-team sprinter targeting another Schelde win
Fabio Jakobsen - Pure sprint specialist when in form

narratives: Fastest race of the Classics season - the sprinters' World Championship
Merckx's record of 3 Scheldeprijs wins has been exceeded only by modern greats
Final bunch-sprint tune-up before Paris-Roubaix and the Ardennes
Crosswinds across Zeeland and Broekstraat cobbles the two main split risks
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1907, Scheldeprijs is one of the oldest races on the calendar and the oldest cycling race in Flanders. Legendary winners include Marcel Kittel (5 wins - the record), Mario Cipollini, Alessandro Petacchi, Robbie McEwen, Mark Cavendish and recent Flemish stars Tim Merlier and Jasper Philipsen. The race is a pilgrimage for sprint fans.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Coverage on Sporza (Belgium), Eurosport/GCN+, and highlights on Flanders Classics YouTube. The first 150km is often tactical breakaway-vs-peloton chess; the real race is the final 30km after the Broekstraat and on the Schoten circuit. Watch team sprint trains on the final 2km.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Scheldeprijs (Women) is the women's edition of the iconic Belgian sprinter's Classic, held April 8, 2026. The ~130km course runs from Terneuzen or a nearby Zeeland/Antwerp-area start into the Schoten finishing circuit, sharing the finale and some course with the men's race. It features the Broekstraat cobbled sector and a fast, technical run-in, typically ending in a bunch sprint - the women's sprinters' Classic of the spring.

races.name: Paris-Roubaix

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 123rd edition of Paris-Roubaix, 'The Hell of the North' or 'Queen of the Classics', ran 258.3km from Compiègne to the Roubaix velodrome on April 12. The men's race featured 30 sectors of pavé spread across the Northern French countryside. The first half of the race is largely flat tarmac; the cobbled action begins at sector 30 (Troisvilles to Inchy, 162.5km to go) and intensifies through the day. Key five-star sectors include the Trouée d'Arenberg (sector 19, 95.3km to go, 2.3km), Mons-en-Pévèle (sector 11, 48.6km to go, 3km), and Carrefour de l'Arbre (sector 4, 17.1km to go, 2.1km). The finish comes after a short sector of easy municipal cobbles leading onto the Roubaix velodrome where riders complete a lap and a half before the line.

keySectors.name: Quiévy to Fontaine au Tertre

keySectors.description: Five-star - the most iconic sector. Dead-straight brutal pavé through a forest, historically where the race breaks open

keySectors.name: Hornaing to Wandignies
keySectors.description: Long four-star sector adding to post-Arenberg attrition

keySectors.name: Tilloy to Sars-et-Rosières

keySectors.description: Short sector after Carrefour - attritional

keySectors.name: Willems to Hem
keySectors.description: Penultimate sector - last significant pavé before Roubaix

favorites.description: Paris-Roubaix is the ultimate cobbled Classic, rewarding riders with unique combinations of bike-handling, raw power, cornering skill, and tactical intelligence. Pure climbers struggle - the race favours rouleurs, ex-cyclocross specialists, and Classics hardmen. Luck with mechanicals and positioning into each sector is as important as watts.
rouleurs: Mathieu van der Poel (Alpecin-Deceuninck) - three-time defending champion, has redefined Roubaix dominance
Tadej Pogačar (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) - targeting his first Paris-Roubaix win after getting closer each year
Mads Pedersen (Lidl-Trek) - cobbled specialist with the power for Roubaix's extreme demands
Filippo Ganna (Ineos Grenadiers) - powerhouse rouleur increasingly focused on the Monuments
Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Deceuninck) - Van der Poel's finishing man and a Roubaix threat in his own right
puncheurs: Wout van Aert (Visma-Lease a Bike) - perennial Roubaix contender with the engine for the pavé
Stefan Küng (Groupama-FDJ United) - multi-time Paris-Roubaix top-ten finisher
Florian Vermeersch (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) - previous Roubaix runner-up, built for the cobbles

narratives: Mathieu van der Poel sought to become only the third rider ever to win four consecutive Paris-Roubaix, after Octave Lapize and Francesco Moser
The early race route changes for 2026 - concentrated cobbled sectors in the opening - accelerated the racing from the start
Tadej Pogačar continues his Monument chase - Roubaix is among the few still missing from his palmarès
Team tactics on the Arenberg and Mons-en-Pévèle historically set the tone - the move that wins is usually clear by the Carrefour de l'Arbre
raceDetails.historicalContext: Paris-Roubaix dates to 1896 and has been held 120+ times since. Belgians dominate the winner's list with 57 victories. Compiègne has hosted the start since 1977 (Paris itself from 1896-1967). The cobbled sectors are rated 1-5 stars based on length and roughness; five-star sectors are Arenberg, Mons-en-Pévèle, and Carrefour de l'Arbre. The race finishes in the historic Roubaix velodrome - a unique cycling venue where winners have a distinctive lap-and-a-half to the line. Eddy Merckx, Roger De Vlaeminck, and Tom Boonen hold records with four wins each. Mathieu van der Poel won three consecutive editions (2023-2025).
raceDetails.watchNotes: Paris-Roubaix is nearly six hours of television - tune in from the first sector (Troisvilles, ~3h into the race) for the tension, or skip to the Arenberg (95km to go) for the historic flashpoint. The critical TV window is the final 90 minutes from Mons-en-Pévèle (48.6km to go) through Carrefour de l'Arbre (17.1km to go). The velodrome finish is iconic. Spoiler caution: race highlights and YouTube thumbnails often spoil the outcome. Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations.

Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations.

races.name: Paris-Roubaix (Women)

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 6th edition of Paris-Roubaix Femmes (rebranded as Paris-Roubaix Femmes Hauts-de-France for 2026) ran 143.1km from Denain to the Roubaix velodrome on April 12, with 20 cobbled sectors totalling over 33km - the most in the women's race's history. For 2026 the route was sharpened by rerouting slightly east via Briastre so that the first four cobbled sectors now follow one another in quick succession, creating unprecedented cobble density in the early kilometres. Previous editions allowed a quiet hour to find rhythm; this year the Hell of the North began almost immediately. Key five-star sectors include Mons-en-Pévèle (sector 11, 48.6km to go) and Carrefour de l'Arbre (sector 4, 17.1km to go), with four four-star sectors including the new Haveluy to Wallers addition.

keySectors.description: Five-star - long and technical, historically decisive

keySectors.description: Five-star - the traditional race-deciding sector

favorites.description: Paris-Roubaix Femmes favours powerful rouleurs with exceptional bike handling, cobbled experience, and the nerves to handle the chaotic positioning battles. The women's race has already produced outsider winners (Alison Jackson, 2023) given how much luck and handling matter. With the new early-cobbled start, mechanical resilience is more important than ever.
rouleurs: Lotte Kopecky (SD Worx-Protime) - multi-time Classics winner with the power and handling for Roubaix
Pauline Ferrand-Prévot (Visma-Lease a Bike) - defending champion (2025), soloed away with 25km to go last year
Marianne Vos (Visma-Lease a Bike) - versatile rider who excels on the cobbles
Elisa Longo Borghini (UAE Team ADQ) - previous winner, combines cobbled experience with top-end power
Lorena Wiebes (SD Worx-Protime) - fast finisher who has podiumed from the velodrome sprint
puncheurs: Alison Jackson (Picnic PostNL) - 2023 winner, proved outsiders can strike at Roubaix
Chiara Consonni (UAE Team ADQ) - emerging classics-sprinter profile
Mischa Bredewold (SD Worx-Protime) - 2025 Amstel winner with Roubaix potential

narratives: Defending champion Pauline Ferrand-Prévot returned to defend her Roubaix title after a historic solo win in 2025
The 2026 route's concentrated early cobbles fundamentally changed the tactical shape of the race - no quiet first hour
Haveluy to Wallers as a brand-new four-star sector for the women created an additional pre-Arenberg test
The women's Roubaix is open to anyone with the right tactical instinct and luck — surprise winners are part of the race's DNA
raceDetails.historicalContext: Paris-Roubaix Femmes was first held in 2021 - a belated but prestigious addition to the women's WorldTour calendar. The inaugural winner was Lizzie Deignan, who soloed to victory in horrific weather on a muddy cobbled epic. The race has quickly become a fixture on the Spring Classics calendar and is consistently the most unpredictable race of the season. The 2026 edition was the sixth running and the first under the 'Hauts-de-France' branding. The finish in the Roubaix velodrome is shared with the men's race, making it one of cycling's most iconic venues.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Tune in for the full race from the opening cobbled sectors - with the new 2026 format there's no quiet opening and chaos often strikes early. The critical TV window is the final hour from Mons-en-Pévèle (48.6km to go) through Carrefour de l'Arbre (17.1km to go). The velodrome finish is must-watch. Spoiler caution: YouTube search results and thumbnails often show the winner - disable autoplay.

Per youtube-cycling-analyzer: USABLE/SAFE — extended-highlights format, opens mid-race at the cobbled sectors, winner only at the natural velodrome finish. Tier 1 official broadcaster. Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations.

races.name: Wörthersee Gravel Race
races.description: UCI Gravel World Series qualifier. Top 25% in each category earn entry to the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships (Nannup, Australia) and 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships (Houffalize, Belgium).

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gravel race in Velden am Wörthersee, Austria. Elite men's and elite women's classifications contest the same course.

broadcast.notes: UCI Gravel World Series events are typically not livestreamed; results and short recap clips appear on the UCI YouTube channel and event social media.

raceDetails.courseSummary: 5-stage, 632km race across rugged Galician terrain: short opening ITT, one flat sprint stage, two hilly days, and a decisive mountain stage with summit finish. Finishes atop Monte Trega over the Atlantic.

favorites.description: A race decided on the mountain stage to Cabeza de Meda, with the opening ITT creating early GC gaps.
gcContenders: All-round climbers who can limit losses in the 15km opening ITT
Pure climbers hoping to flip the GC on Stage 4 (summit finish at Alto Cabeza de Meda)
Riders targeting Vuelta preparation — historically competitive in Galician terrain
stageHunters: Sprinters for Stage 2 (Vilalba → Barreiros, flat finale)
Breakaway specialists for Stage 3 (rolling parcours Carballo → Padrón)
Puncheurs / late attackers for Stage 5 (Monte Trega summit finish)

narratives: Galician cycling tradition: race honors the Camino de Santiago pilgrimage route, traversing classic Spanish cycling terrain
Spring Vuelta prep: traditionally a preparation race for Vuelta a España GC contenders
Movistar spotlight: the Spanish team targets home soil success, particularly on the Monte Trega finale
2026 date shift: race moved from its traditional late-February slot to April for the first time
raceDetails.historicalContext: 5th edition. Previously held in late February / early March; moved to April in 2026. Past winners include Jonas Vingegaard.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Stage 4 (Cabeza de Meda summit finish) is the GC-deciding day. Stage 1 ITT sets early gaps. Stages 2-3 are for sprinters/puncheurs.
raceDetails.gcDynamics: Short ITT means climbers can concede little — pure climbers must attack hard on Stages 4 and 5 to flip the GC

stages.name: Stage 1: A Coruña ITT
stages.description: Short individual time trial in A Coruña, opening test for GC contenders

stageDetails.courseSummary: 15km ITT in A Coruña with a 1.5km climb at 5.7% providing the first time check
stageDetails.watchNotes: Pure ITT specialists favored, but GC contenders needing to lose as little time as possible

stages.name: Stage 2: Vilalba → Barreiros
stages.description: Flat stage likely to end in a sprint finish along the Galician coast

stageDetails.courseSummary: 148.6km from Vilalba to Barreiros, flat profile along the coastal roads of northern Galicia
stageDetails.watchNotes: Sprint teams expected to control. Watch for crosswind risk on exposed coastal sections

stages.name: Stage 3: Carballo → Padrón
stages.description: Hilly 169km stage through Galician countryside from Carballo to Padrón

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling 169km parcours with multiple short climbs — a stage for puncheurs and breakaway specialists
stageDetails.watchNotes: Reduced group expected at the finish. Watch late attacks on the final rises

stages.name: Stage 4: Xinzo de Limia → Alto Cabeza de Meda
stages.description: Queen stage with summit finish on Alto Cabeza de Meda

stageDetails.courseSummary: 145.7km climbing stage finishing atop Alto Cabeza de Meda — the decisive GC day
stageDetails.watchNotes: GC will be decided here. Watch for attacks on the final climb from pure climbers

stages.name: Stage 5: As Neves → Monte Trega
stages.description: Final stage with summit finish atop Monte Trega overlooking the Atlantic

stageDetails.courseSummary: 154.7km from As Neves to the summit of Monte Trega — dramatic coastal climbing finale
stageDetails.watchNotes: Final GC shuffling possible. Watch for late attacks from anyone within striking distance

races.name: Ronde Van Limburg
races.videoNote: FloBikes hosts the full replay. Avoid FloBikes/YouTube highlight clips — their descriptions reveal the winner.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Ronde van Limburg is a Belgian UCI 1.1 one-day race held April 15, 2026 in the Limburg province of Belgium. The ~195km parcours uses the punchy hills and narrow roads of Limburg - similar terrain to Amstel Gold Race across the nearby Dutch border. Multiple short, sharp climbs and a hilly finishing circuit typically produce a reduced-bunch or small-group finish, making this race a natural warm-up for Amstel Gold and the Ardennes Classics.

keyClimbs.name: Cauberg-style Limburg hellings
keyClimbs.notes: Representative Limburg hilltop - multiple similar climbs shape the race

favorites.description: Ronde van Limburg favours puncheurs and Ardennes specialists sharpening for Amstel Gold and the Ardennes Monument the following weekend. Belgian and Dutch Pro Teams dominate.
puncheurs: Belgian and Dutch ProTeam puncheurs targeting Ardennes week
WorldTour riders using the race as a final Amstel sharpener
classicsRiders: Experienced Classics riders who know the Limburg hills

narratives: Warm-up race for Amstel Gold two days later
Belgian and Dutch Pro Teams dominate the start list
Punchy Limburg climbs demand precise positioning
raceDetails.historicalContext: Ronde van Limburg has been contested since 1919 and is one of the oldest Belgian one-day races. Past winners include Eddy Merckx, Rik Van Looy, Johan Museeuw and modern Belgian Classics stars. The race has evolved into a perfect Amstel tune-up.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Coverage on Sporza (Belgium) and Eurosport highlights. Watch the final circuit for Ardennes specialists testing their form.

races.name: Brabantse Pijl

primary.notes: Free-to-air via SBS / SBS On Demand. SBS holds rights to Grand Tours and Monuments through 2028.

raceDetails.courseSummary: De Brabantse Pijl (The Brabantian Arrow) is a one-day hilly Classic in Flemish Brabant, Belgium, organized by Flanders Classics. The 2026 men's edition runs 162.5km from Beersel to Overijse on April 17, signalling the transition from the cobbled Flemish Classics to the Ardennes week. The route tackles a rolling parcours south of Brussels, featuring a heavy concentration of short, punchy climbs on narrow roads. The finale consists of three laps of a local circuit around Overijse that includes the iconic S-bend climb of the Schavei and the S-bend into the finish, with the last kilometre a slight drag that suits puncheurs rather than pure sprinters.

keyClimbs.name: Hertstraat
keyClimbs.notes: Recurring early-finale climb on the Overijse circuit

keyClimbs.name: Holstheide
keyClimbs.notes: Sharp kicker before the final circuit

keyClimbs.name: Moskesstraat
keyClimbs.notes: Classic launchpad for attacks on the penultimate lap

keyClimbs.name: Schavei
keyClimbs.notes: Final classified climb, often where the winning selection is made

favorites.description: Brabantse Pijl suits puncheurs and classics riders who can handle dozens of short climbs and still produce a reduced-sprint kick. It's a traditional proving ground for Ardennes week and has been won by Alaphilippe, Van Aert, Evenepoel and other world-class one-day specialists.
puncheurs: Tiesj Benoot (Visma-Lease a Bike) - Classics specialist at home in Flemish Brabant
Maxim Van Gils (Red Bull-BORA-hansgrohe) - rising punchy rider targeting Ardennes week
Marc Hirschi (Tudor) - proven one-day winner on this type of terrain
classicsRiders: Mathieu van der Poel (Alpecin-Deceuninck) - if on the startlist, an automatic favourite
Wout van Aert (Visma-Lease a Bike) - former winner and local hero
Tom Pidcock (Q36.5) - versatile puncheur with a strong kick
outsiders: Ben Healy (EF Education-EasyPost) - long-range attacker who thrives on repeated climbs
Dorian Godon (Decathlon CMA CGM) - reigning-style Ardennes specialist

narratives: The hinge race between Flanders cobbles and Ardennes hills - who transitions best?
Flemish home crowd racing with Amstel Gold just two days away
Short, repeated climbs reward punch and positioning over pure power
Moskesstraat and Schavei on the final circuit traditionally decide the race
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1961, De Brabantse Pijl has grown from a regional race into a key pre-Ardennes classic. Past winners include Eddy Merckx, Johan Museeuw, Philippe Gilbert, Julian Alaphilippe, Wout van Aert and Remco Evenepoel, confirming its status as a race for the best one-day riders. Since Flanders Classics took over organisation it has settled into a late-week slot before the Ardennes triple.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Small climbs come thick and fast - don't expect big gaps early. The race typically comes alive on the second of three finishing laps. The Schavei inside 3km to go and the technical run-in to Overijse reward riders with classics instinct. Watch for Ardennes specialists using this as their final sharpener before Amstel, Flèche and Liège.

races._notes: FloBikes event page for the men's race. Extended highlights also available at /video/15792480-2026-de-brabantse-pijl-men-extended-highlights.
races.videoNote: FloBikes 2026 De Brabantse Pijl (Men) full race broadcast (3:02:36). Per youtube-cycling-analyzer: SAFE — full live broadcast with neutral pre-race commentary; winner only named in the final minutes of the 3-hour broadcast. Tier 1 official broadcaster. races.name: Classic Grand Bescançon Doubs

primary.notes: No confirmed Australian broadcast for this race.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Classic Grand Besançon Doubs is a French UCI 1.2 one-day race held in the Doubs department around the city of Besançon. Taking place on April 17, 2026, the roughly 190km course rolls through the Jura foothills with a series of short category-3 and category-4 climbs and a punchy circuit finale. The parcours tends to favour puncheurs and late-attacking breakaway riders; the race rarely ends in a bunch sprint because of the repeated elevation changes in the final hour.

favorites.description: A UCI 1.2 race drawing French Continental teams, Pro Conti squads and a handful of WorldTour development riders. Winners are typically French puncheurs or in-form Continental riders looking to upgrade.
puncheurs: Top French Continental team leaders on hilly one-day form
Emerging U23/elite rider from a French or Belgian Conti squad
outsiders: Breakaway specialists willing to commit from distance
Riders using this as a sharpener for Tour du Jura the next day

narratives: Traditional opener to a Franche-Comté weekend double with Tour du Jura
Chance for French Continental teams to beat Pro squads on home roads
Puncheur-friendly finish often decides the race in the last 20km
raceDetails.historicalContext: Held under various names in the Besançon area, this classic has been a platform for emerging French talent. It pairs with Tour du Jura the following day for a Franche-Comté weekend popular with French teams.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Small race but good terrain for punchy racing. Expect the key selection to come on the final circuit. French TV and race organiser social channels are typical coverage sources.

races._notes: FloBikes only has 2024 link, no 2026 deep link. Cyclism'Actu highlights video (tJ-8MB4ol8s) reveals winner Jegat in description. TNT Sports Final KMs short reveals winner via thumbnail. No spoiler-safe option.

races.name: Tour du Jura

primary.notes: No confirmed Australian broadcast for this race.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Tour du Jura is a hilly French UCI 1.2 one-day race contested through the Jura mountains in eastern France, held on April 18, 2026, one day after the Classic Grand Besançon Doubs. The roughly 190-200km route strings together several categorized Jura climbs and typically ends with a hilly finale that tests climbers and rouleurs. It is the tougher half of the Franche-Comté weekend double and often produces a small-group or solo finish.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Haute Joux area
keyClimbs.notes: Representative mid-race Jura climb - several similar ascents shape the race

keyClimbs.name: Final category-2/3 climb
keyClimbs.notes: Decisive late climb where strong climbers typically attack

favorites.description: Tour du Jura favours climbers and strong puncheurs from French Continental teams, with occasional appearances by WorldTour development squads using the race for hard training. Breakaway success is common on the longer climbs.
climbers: French Continental climbers with Alpine pedigree
Breakaway specialists targeting a signature win
puncheurs: Riders fresh off Classic Grand Besançon Doubs the day before

narratives: Harder of the Franche-Comté weekend double
Jura climbs give pure climbers a chance over puncheurs
French Continental teams battle Pro squads on mountainous home roads
raceDetails.historicalContext: Tour du Jura has grown into one of France's signature 1.2 hilly races, with past winners progressing to Pro Team and WorldTour contracts. The race is a staple of the French April calendar.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch for the moves on the final categorized climb - the race rarely comes back together after that. Coverage via race social channels and short highlights on FFC/French regional TV.

races._notes: FloBikes only has 2024 link, no 2026 deep link. Cyclism'Actu highlights video (YV0TnhWL9jY) - despite the question-style title - reveals winner Riccitello in description. TNT Sports Final KMs short reveals winner via thumbnail. No spoiler-safe option.

races.name: Liège-Bastogne-Liège U23
races.description: U23 version of La Doyenne — Ardennes classic over punchy Belgian climbs

primary.notes: No confirmed Australian broadcast for this race.
U23 races typically not broadcast internationally. Check race organizer's social media for short highlight clips.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Liège-Bastogne-Liège U23 is the under-23 version of La Doyenne, run over a shortened but still brutal Ardennes course, held on April 18, 2026. The ~170km parcours uses many of the same iconic climbs as the elite Monument - Côte de la Redoute, Côte de la Roche-aux-Faucons and the rolling valleys around the Ourthe - finishing in Liège. It is widely regarded as one of the most prestigious U23 races on the calendar and a proven launch pad to the WorldTour.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Redoute
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic elite-LBL climb; traditional launchpad for attacks

keyClimbs.name: Côte des Forges
keyClimbs.notes: Penultimate major climb

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Roche-aux-Faucons
keyClimbs.notes: Decisive final climb in U23 and elite races alike

favorites.description: A showcase for the next generation of Ardennes specialists and GC hopefuls. Past editions have been won by riders who went on to WorldTour Classics and Grand Tour success.
climbers: Top U23 climbers from WorldTour development teams
Emerging French, Belgian and Italian Ardennes specialists
puncheurs: Punchy riders who can handle the Roche-aux-Faucons and still sprint
Development-team leaders targeting a U23 monument on their palmarès

narratives: Scouting showcase - WorldTour teams watch results closely for future signings
Same iconic climbs as the elite Monument the following Sunday
La Redoute and Roche-aux-Faucons traditionally decide the race
raceDetails.historicalContext: LBL U23 has been won by riders who later won the elite Monument, making it one of the most predictive races for future Ardennes talent. The race shares most of the final third of its route with the elite men's Liège-Bastogne-Liège.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Coverage is typically limited to race highlights on organiser channels and Belgian regional TV. The race finishes before the elite weekend so serves as an early preview of likely Monument tactics.

races._notes: No spoiler-safe coverage found. FloBikes has no LBL U23 2026 listing. YouTube candidates include The Young Peloton channel (which reveals 'Francesco Busatto wins Liege-Bastogne-Liege U23' in their channel listing) and pirate livestream lures. No safe option.

races.name: Amstel Gold Race

primary.notes: Live coverage from 06:30 ET; full replay and highlights available on-demand. $39.99/month or $149.99-155.88/year.
Live on TNT Sports 2 from 13:55 BST; streamed via HBO Max (cycling moved from Discovery+ to HBO Max in March 2026). £30.99/month or £25.99/month on 12-month Saver plan.
Live coverage from 06:30 ET; full replay and highlights available on-demand. CA$49.99/month or CA$215.88/year.
Free-to-air. Live coverage 23:00–01:15 AEST; full race + highlights replays via dedicated SBS On Demand series page.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 60th edition of the Amstel Gold Race covers 257.2km from Maastricht through the rolling Limburg region of the Netherlands, with a finish just past the summit of the Cauberg in Berg en Terblijt (near Valkenburg). The 2026 course features 33 ascents - the 'hellingen' or short Dutch hills - spread across a technical, serpentine route. The main change for 2026 is a slightly shorter distance between the final climb and the finish line, potentially concentrating the decisive action. Key markers include Wolfsberg (km 86.9), a run of climbs through Camerig, Vaalserberg, and Eperheide in the middle, then the finale of Gulperberg, Kruisberg, Eyserbosweg, Fromberg, Keutenberg (225.9km), a penultimate Cauberg at km 235.6, followed by Geulhemmerberg and the final Bemelerberg at km 247 before the fast run-in to the line after a final rise past the Cauberg summit.

keyClimbs.name: Gulperberg
keyClimbs.notes: Second pass - begins the decisive finale

keyClimbs.name: Kruisberg
keyClimbs.notes: Short but steep, attritional

keyClimbs.name: Eyserbosweg
keyClimbs.notes: Narrow and punishingly steep - often where selections begin

keyClimbs.name: Fromberg
keyClimbs.notes: Longer drag - momentum climb

keyClimbs.name: Keutenberg
keyClimbs.notes: The steepest climb in the race - historical long-range attack launchpad (Pogačar 2023)

keyClimbs.name: Cauberg (2nd pass)
keyClimbs.notes: Third-to-last climb - another potential selection point

keyClimbs.name: Geulhemmerberg
keyClimbs.notes: Second-to-last climb

keyClimbs.name: Bemelerberg
keyClimbs.notes: Final categorised climb before the run-in to the finish past the Cauberg summit

favorites.description: Amstel Gold rewards versatile classics riders who can handle 33 hills across 257km while retaining explosive power for the tight final 30km. The race has been won by cobbled specialists and Grand Tour winners alike, making it a transitional Classic between the cobbles and the pure hill climbers. The short but twisty finale after the Bemelerberg requires positioning, bike handling, and a sharp finishing kick.
puncheurs: Tadej Pogačar (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) - defending champion of the Ardennes with multiple wins, the overwhelming favourite when he starts
Mathieu van der Poel (Alpecin-Deceuninck) - former winner (2019), combines explosive power and bike skills
Mattias Skjelmose (Lidl-Trek) - 2025 winner, proven ability to beat the Ardennes big three in tight finishes
Remco Evenepoel (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) - two-time LBL winner, increasingly potent over punchy terrain
Tom Pidcock (Q36.5 Pro Cycling) - previous podium finisher, thrives on explosive hilly Classics
sprinters: Marc Hirschi (Tudor Pro Cycling) - on-form puncheur capable of out-kicking a reduced group
Michael Matthews (Jayco AlUla) - experienced finisher who can survive the climbs and deliver a fast sprint

narratives: Opening Sunday of the Ardennes triptych - sets the tone for Flèche Wallonne and Liège-Bastogne-Liège
The only Dutch men's WorldTour event - Dutch fans pack the Cauberg and the hellingen, adding unique atmosphere
Recent editions have been decided late (Pidcock 2024 outsprinting breakaway companions, Skjelmose 2025 beating Pogačar/Evenepoel in Valkenburg) - expect the winning move to come in the final 10km
Team tactics around the Keutenberg are pivotal - it's the steepest climb of the day and a proven launchpad for long-range moves
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Amstel Gold Race is the youngest of the Spring Classics, first raced in 1966 with Jean Stablinski winning the inaugural 302km edition. It is the only Classic held in the Netherlands and takes its name from the Dutch brewery. Before 2017, the race traditionally finished atop the Cauberg, making it a decisive summit-style finale; since then, the finish has moved to beyond the Cauberg, encouraging longer-range attacks and a more tactical finale. Recent winners include Wout van Aert, Mathieu van der Poel, Tadej Pogačar, Tom Pidcock, and Mattias Skjelmose - a roster that reflects the race's appeal across rider types.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Amstel Gold is long - tune in for the final 90 minutes when the race attacks the decisive loop. Key moments: the Keutenberg at 31km to go (steepest climb, often the launchpad), the second Cauberg at 21.6km to go, and the final 5km after the Bemelerberg. The twisty, narrow roads make positioning crucial and favour riders with strong teams. Spoiler caution: YouTube thumbnails and sidebar often reveal the final metres - disable autoplay.

races._notes: FloBikes event page hosts both men's and women's editions. Men's replay: /events/15183666-2026-amstel-gold-race/videos?playing=15806826 ; Men's extended highlights: /video/15807073-highlights-2026-amstel-gold-race-men.
races.videoNote: FloBikes 2026 Amstel Gold Race (Men) full broadcast (3:06:38). Per youtube-cycling-analyzer: SAFE — full live broadcast with no pre-finish spoilers possible; winner only at natural finish line. Tier 1 official broadcaster. Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations.

races.name: GP Féminin de Chambéry

primary.notes: No confirmed Australian broadcast for this race.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Grand Prix Féminin de Chambéry is a French UCI 1.2 women's one-day race held on April 19, 2026 in and around the Savoyard city of Chambéry. The ~110-120km course uses the hilly terrain of the Massif des Bauges pre-Alps, with several categorized climbs and a finish typically in downtown Chambéry. It's a platform for French and European Continental and ProTeam women's squads in a packed April calendar.

favorites.description: A 1.2 women's race drawing French Continental and development teams, with occasional WorldTour guest appearances. Winners are typically punchy climbers or strong breakaway riders.
climbers: French and European ProTeam climbers in spring form
Continental team leaders using the race for a result
puncheurs: Riders with a late-attack toolkit suited to hilly French finales

narratives: Support race alongside the men's Franche-Comté weekend for French cycling coverage
Savoie hills favour climbers and strong rouleurs
Stepping-stone race for emerging French and European women's talent
raceDetails.historicalContext: A relatively new addition to the French women's calendar, GP Féminin de Chambéry has helped fill a gap in domestic one-day racing and regularly sees podiums from French Continental squads and development riders.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Coverage is typically limited to race organiser social media and brief highlights. Watch for late attacks on the run-in to Chambéry.

races._notes: France 3 Auvergne-Rhone-Alpes had a DIRECT (live) stream (w3yLLh34kPM) but description appears to be a summary referencing pre-race link. Other YouTube candidates (y6tfyWnQn8o, O1Lm_HHJE4U) reveal winner Celia Gery in description. No spoiler-safe option for 1-star race.

races.name: Amstel Gold Race (Women)

primary.notes: Free-to-air on SBS / SBS On Demand - SBS carries all women's Monuments and major classics.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 Amstel Gold Race Ladies Edition is longer and tougher than ever - 158.1km from Maastricht to Valkenburg with 21 climbs. The race opens through Limburg's hellingen before hitting a crucial opening cluster (Kruisberg, Eyserbosweg, Fromberg, Keutenberg) that softens legs early. The real business starts with four passes of the 17.8km Cauberg-Geulhemmerberg-Bemelerberg finishing circuit, climbing the iconic Cauberg five times in total (the men climb it three times). The finale features the Cauberg with 21.6km to go, then with 1.7km to go for a near-summit finish after the climb. The compact circuit format, combined with 21 climbs in 158km, makes this one of the most intense one-day races on the women's calendar.

keyClimbs.name: Kruisberg
keyClimbs.notes: Opening hard climb - early filter

keyClimbs.name: Eyserbosweg
keyClimbs.notes: Narrow, steep - begins to shed climbers

keyClimbs.name: Keutenberg
keyClimbs.notes: Steepest climb of the day - long-range attack potential

keyClimbs.name: Cauberg (1st pass)
keyClimbs.notes: First of five Cauberg ascents

keyClimbs.name: Cauberg (2nd pass)
keyClimbs.notes: Second pass - circuit pattern emerging

keyClimbs.name: Cauberg (3rd pass)
keyClimbs.notes: Midway through the finishing circuits

keyClimbs.name: Cauberg (4th pass)
keyClimbs.notes: Penultimate Cauberg - major attack platform

keyClimbs.name: Bemelerberg (final)
keyClimbs.notes: Last hill before the final Cauberg

keyClimbs.name: Cauberg (5th/final pass)
keyClimbs.notes: Decisive final climb - finish just past the summit

favorites.description: Amstel Gold Ladies favours pure puncheurs with explosive repeated efforts, combined with climbing durability to survive 21 hills. Five ascents of the Cauberg exhaust even the best, and the near-summit finish rewards those with a punchy kick in the final kilometre.
puncheurs: Demi Vollering (FDJ-SUEZ) - European champion and previous Ardennes triple winner, the overwhelming favourite
Marianne Vos (Visma-Lease a Bike) - two-time winner, always a podium threat on hilly terrain with her fast kick
Puck Pieterse (Fenix-Deceuninck) - explosive puncheur with cyclocross power perfect for the Cauberg
Pauline Ferrand-Prévot (Visma-Lease a Bike) - multi-discipline champion with the punch and climbing for Amstel
climbers: Elisa Longo Borghini (UAE Team ADQ) - Monument winner who thrives on relentless hilly Classics
Kasia Niewiadoma-Phinney (Canyon//SRAM zondacrypto) - long-range attacker who can go from 30km out
Cédrine Kerbaol (EF Education-Oatly) - emerging talent for long, selective races
sprinters: Lorena Wiebes (SD Worx-Protime) - if the group is large and the Cauberg selections ease, she's the fastest on the line

narratives: Opening Sunday of the Ardennes triptych for the women - form here signals for Flèche and Liège
Defending champion Mischa Bredewold took 2025 from a small split group - expect similar tactical finales
Five Cauberg ascents makes this one of the toughest circuit-finale races on the women's calendar
Team tactics on the Keutenberg (84km to go) set up the finale - strong teams can shred the bunch here
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Amstel Gold Race Ladies Edition has been part of the Women's WorldTour with the revitalised event returning on a modern course in 2017. Previous editions have been won by Marianne Vos, Demi Vollering, and in 2025 by Mischa Bredewold. It is part of the Ardennes triptych alongside La Flèche Wallonne Féminine and Liège-Bastogne-Liège Femmes. The race has grown steadily in prestige and now covers over 150km - among the longest one-day races on the women's calendar.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Tune in for the final 90 minutes - especially the third Cauberg pass (39km to go) onwards. Attacks often go on the fourth Cauberg (21.6km to go) or on the Bemelerberg. The finish comes just past the final Cauberg summit, which means the last 1.7km is the decisive section for any sprint from a small group. Spoiler caution: avoid YouTube sidebar, autoplay, and comments.

races._notes: Direct deep link to women's replay within the shared 2026 Amstel Gold Race event page (men + women).

races.name: Monaco Gravel Race

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gravel race in Monaco – Peille, Monaco. Elite men's and elite women's classifications contest the same course.

broadcast.notes: UCI Gravel World Series events are typically not livestreamed; results and short recap clips appear on the UCI YouTube channel and event social media.

races.name: GP Samarkand

primary.notes: No confirmed Australian broadcast for this race.

raceDetails.courseSummary: GP Samarkand is a Uzbek UCI multi-stage race held in and around the historic Silk Road city of Samarkand, starting April 20, 2026. The race uses a mix of flat stages for sprinters and one or two hillier finales through the surrounding Zarafshan valley. It attracts Asian Continental teams and some European squads looking for early-season points on fast, dry roads.

favorites.description: A Continental stage race dominated by Asian and eastern-European teams, with occasional European breakaway specialists. Sprinters take the flat stages and a punchy climber typically wins the GC.
sprinters: Continental team sprinters targeting flat stages
gcContenders: Climbers from Kazakh, Uzbek and other Asian Continental squads

narratives: Highly scenic race on Silk Road roads around Samarkand
Early-season points opportunity for Asian Continental teams
GC typically decided on the one hilly stage
raceDetails.historicalContext: GP Samarkand has grown on the UCI Asia Tour calendar as Uzbekistan invests in hosting international cycling. Recent editions have been won by Kazakh, Uzbek and Iranian riders.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Coverage is typically limited to organiser social media and Asian cycling channels. Expect a mix of sprint stages and one GC-defining hilly day.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 49th Tour of the Alps covers 760km across five days of mountainous racing in Austria and Italy, with 14,620m of total elevation gain. The 2026 route runs Monday April 20 to Friday April 24, traditionally serving as a final Giro d'Italia tune-up for GC contenders. The race features only one true mountain finish (Val Martello on Stage 2) but includes three stages that finish on descents, rewarding well-rounded riders who can descend as well as climb. A new 'Bonus Sprint' rule adds 6/4/2 seconds intermediate bonuses, often on climbs, directly impacting the GC battle. The race starts in Innsbruck, Austria, and concludes in Bolzano, Italy - uniting the Tirol and Südtirol/Alto Adige regions across the Alpine border.

gcContenders: Egan Bernal (INEOS Grenadiers) - team leader, continued comeback arc, Giro prep
Thymen Arensman (INEOS Grenadiers) - 2025 Alps podium, full-blown Giro GC contender
Giulio Pellizzari (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) - rising Italian climber, on home roads

Michael Storer (Tudor Pro Cycling) - defending champion, breakthrough year continues

Lennert Van Eetvelt (Lotto Intermarché) - puncheur-climber profile
favorites.description: 5-stage Innsbruck-to-Bolzano climbing race, the definitive Giro d'Italia tune-up; relentless Alpine ascents with no time trials make pure climbers the GC favourites. Compact format means GC gaps appear immediately.

narratives: Final Giro d'Italia form barometer — historically previews the Giro pink jersey contenders
Arensman vs Bernal at INEOS — co-leadership tension as both target Giro GC
Storer (Tudor) defending against the WorldTour heavyweights — underdog narrative
Compact 5-stage format with daily climbing means GC gaps appear immediately — no waiting for the third week
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Tour of the Alps was previously known as the Giro del Trentino until 2016. First raced in 1963, it has become a staple of the spring calendar and the traditional final Giro d'Italia warm-up for GC contenders. Past winners include Giro champions and future Grand Tour contenders - the race has a strong track record of identifying the year's Giro favourites. Memorable editions include Geraint Thomas's 2017 victory (just days before Michele Scarponi's tragic death) and Romain Bardet's 2022 win. Organizers are based in Arco, the Garda Trentino town that hosts a stage finish each year.
raceDetails.gcDynamics: With only one summit finish (Stage 2 to Val Martello) and three descent finishes, GC time is won and lost differently than in most modern stage races. Expect the major GC swings on the Val Martello mountain finish (Stage 2), the Queen Stage 4 with 3,600m of climbing over Passo Redebus and Povo, and the short but punchy Stage 5 finale in Bolzano. Descending skills matter - Stage 3 to Arco and parts of Stage 4 are decided after the final climb. The Bonus Sprint rule (6/4/2s) on intermediate climbs adds tactical variety throughout.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The Tour of the Alps is one of the best-kept secrets of the spring calendar - compact, scenic, and highly competitive. Tune in for the full Stage 2 to Val Martello (only mountain finish) and Stage 4 Queen Stage. Stages 1, 3, and 5 reward attacking riders with descent finishes. Spoiler caution: as a pro race, post-stage highlights and sidebar content frequently reveal winners.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 90th edition of La Flèche Wallonne runs 208.8km from Herstal (a new host city in the Liège area) to Huy, featuring three ascents of the iconic Mur de Huy (1.3km at 9.6% average, peaking near 20%) at km 134.4, km 171, and at the finish line (km 208.8). The opening 100km delivers the peloton to the finishing circuits via two early climbs - Côte de Trasenster (km 18.2) and Côte des Forges (km 48.8) - before entering the 37.2km finishing loop that includes Côte d'Ereffe (2.1km at 5%) and Côte de Cherave (1.3km at 8.1%) immediately before each Mur de Huy ascent. The race is almost always decided on the final Mur, with the explosive 'slow-motion sprint' up its 20% slopes traditionally crowning a pure puncheur.

Mur de Huy famously rewards perfectly-timed kick — does anyone risk a long-range move vs the traditional final-200m sprint?
raceDetails.historicalContext: First run in 1936, La Flèche Wallonne ('The Walloon Arrow') is the second-oldest race in the Ardennes triptych. Originally a Tournai-to-Liège point-to-point, the race was transformed in 1985 when the finish moved to the summit of the Mur de Huy. That decision made the Mur one of the most iconic climbs in pro cycling - 1.3km of twisting, fan-lined road rising to 20%. The finish has produced the famous 'slow-motion sprint' where riders are reduced to grinding turnovers in the final metres. Recent editions have also seen the addition of the Côte d'Ereffe and Côte de Cherave in the finale to create more selective racing before the Mur. Alejandro Valverde's five wins remain the record; Julian Alaphilippe and Tadej Pogačar are among recent multiple winners.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Flèche Wallonne is often a TV tactic race - tune in for the final 60km when the circuits begin. The second Mur de Huy at 37.8km to go is the tactical benchmark; the third Cherave at 5.7km to go is where the winning move often begins. Every edition features the final 1.3km on the Mur as destination viewing - the 'slow-motion sprint' is unique in cycling. Spoiler caution: the Mur summit finish is heavily covered, so YouTube previews and sidebar recommendations often contain the outcome.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 29th edition of La Flèche Wallonne Féminine covers 148.2km with a summit finish atop the iconic Mur de Huy (1.3km at 9.6% average, peaking near 20%). The race starts from the Grand Place in Huy and opens with the Bohissau, Courrière, and Durnal climbs before reaching the 37.2km finishing circuit. Riders complete two laps of the final loop, which includes Côte d'Ereffe (2.1km at 5%) and Côte de Cherave (1.3km at 8.1%) before each Mur de Huy ascent. The race finishes atop the third Mur - the decisive 'slow-motion sprint' up the 20% slopes typically crowns a puncheur with strong short-effort power.

raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1998, La Flèche Wallonne Féminine shares the finish-line Mur de Huy summit with the men's race, making it one of cycling's most iconic women's one-dayers. The race has been dominated by specialists - Marianne Vos (four-time winner in the early years), Evelyn Stevens, Anna van der Breggen (seven consecutive or near-consecutive wins), and more recently Demi Vollering and Kasia Niewiadoma. As part of the Ardennes triptych, it offers the mid-week test between Amstel Gold (Sunday) and Liège-Bastogne-Liège Femmes (the following Sunday).
raceDetails.watchNotes: Tune in for the final 45-60 minutes - the first Mur de Huy at 37km to go sets the tactical tone, but the race is almost always decided on the final Mur. The second Cherave at 5.7km to go is where the winning move typically begins. The final 1.3km up the Mur is destination TV: watch for the timing of the decisive kick, usually in the last 200m. Spoiler caution: the Mur summit finish is heavily covered in highlights - disable YouTube autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Vuelta a Asturias (Vuelta Ciclista a Asturias Julio Álvarez Mendo) is a three-stage Spanish UCI 2.1 race held across April 23-25, 2026 in the Principality of Asturias. Known for short but brutal stages that crisscross the Cantabrian Mountains, it features the famous Alto del Naranco summit finish in Oviedo. Despite its modest length, the race is genuinely mountainous and regularly produces selective GC battles among Spanish Pro Team and invited WorldTour squads.

raceDetails.historicalContext: Vuelta a Asturias dates back to 1925 and is one of Spain's oldest stage races. Past winners include Luis Herrera, Alberto Contador, Nairo Quintana and other climbing legends. The Alto del Naranco summit finish is a symbol of Asturian cycling.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Short stages, big climbs - every kilometre matters. Watch for GC moves on the final two climbs of each stage. Coverage typically on RTVE Spain and TeleDeporte, with highlights on YouTube.
raceDetails.gcDynamics: GC almost always determined by summit finishes and the Cantabrian mountain stages. Flat stages are rare on this parcours, so bonus seconds and summit attacks dominate.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Omloop van Borsele Women is a Dutch UCI 1.2 one-day race held in Zeeland on April 25, 2026. The ~130km flat course winds through the polders and dykes around 's-Heerenhoek, with the wind from the North Sea the primary selection factor. Crosswind splits (waaiers) are common and the race regularly ends in a bunch sprint or small-group finish, depending on weather.

favorites.description: A flat, wind-affected Dutch Classic favouring strong sprinters with classic-rider skills, or teams willing to force echelons. Historically won by Dutch and Belgian fast-finishers.
sprinters: Dutch and Belgian sprinters with crosswind-handling skills
WorldTour women's sprinters using the race as a sharpener
rouleurs: Strong rouleurs who can force or survive echelons on the Zeeland dykes

narratives: Wind decides everything - echelons are the main threat
Dutch home-roads race with strong Continental and WorldTour participation

raceDetails.historicalContext: Omloop van Borsele is a staple of the Dutch women's calendar and traditionally attracts a strong start list. Past winners include top Dutch and Belgian sprinters and rouleurs.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Weather forecast is the single most important variable - windy days produce echelon carnage, calm days a bunch sprint. Coverage typically via organiser channels and Dutch regional TV.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Tour de Bretagne Cycliste is a French UCI 2.2 (Continental) week-long stage race through Brittany, running from April 25 to May 1, 2026. The race typically features seven stages of undulating, windswept Breton roads with a mix of flat finishes, punchy hilltops and a small individual time trial. It is a cornerstone of French development cycling and a showcase for young Continental talent.

favorites.description: Tour de Bretagne is fought between French Continental teams, European development squads and national teams, making it a talent-spotting race. Puncheurs and rouleurs with strong TT legs typically dominate GC.
gcContenders: Top French Continental rouleurs with good TT ability
European national team leaders (Belgian, Dutch, Italian U23 selections)
sprinters: Development-team sprinters targeting the flat Breton stages
stageHunters: Breakaway riders using the hillier stages to escape the bunch

narratives: Key French development race - WorldTour scouts watch closely
Breton wind and narrow roads produce echelons and crashes
GC typically decided by the combination of punchy finishes and the short TT
raceDetails.historicalContext: Tour de Bretagne dates back to 1967 and has launched the careers of many French WorldTour riders. It remains one of the most prestigious French U23/Continental stage races.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Coverage is typically highlights-only via organiser channels, Bretagne Télé and French regional sports media. Expect a week of racing with wind, short climbs and a small time trial deciding the overall.
raceDetails.gcDynamics: GC is typically shaped by the short TT and one or two hilly finishes. Bonus seconds at intermediate sprints matter. Crosswind echelons can also eliminate GC contenders early in the week.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gran Premio della Liberazione Donne is an Italian UCI 1.2 women's one-day race held in Rome on April 26, 2026 (Italy's Liberation Day weekend). The race is contested on a short circuit in and around the Caracalla area of central Rome, with multiple laps producing a fast, punchy race typically decided by a reduced bunch sprint.

favorites.description: A showcase race for Italian and European women's Continental teams, traditionally won by sprinters and puncheurs who can survive dozens of hard laps in the Roman heat.
sprinters: Italian ProTeam and Continental sprinters - home-roads advantage
European Continental women's sprinters targeting UCI points
puncheurs: Riders who can attack off the short drag into the finish on the final laps

narratives: Flagship Italian one-day race on Liberation Day weekend
Fast, technical Rome circuit rewards positioning and bike-handling
Italian Continental teams vs. European ProTeams for home-roads glory
raceDetails.historicalContext: Held in conjunction with the men's Gran Premio della Liberazione, the women's race has become a staple of the Italian April calendar since its creation, with Italian and European winners from both Continental and ProTeam squads.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Coverage via RAI Italy (often), organiser social channels and Italian cycling YouTube. Expect a criterium-style race decided in the final lap.

races.name: Liège-Bastogne-Liège

primary.notes: ASO Monument rights on Peacock. Sun Apr 26. $10.99/mo Premium.

Live coverage from 06:10 EDT. CA$49.99/mo or CA$215.88/yr.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 112th edition of Liège-Bastogne-Liège, cycling's oldest Classic ('La Doyenne'), runs 259.5km from Liège down to Luxembourg via Bastogne, then back north through the Ardennes hills to finish in central Liège. The 2026 route retains the classic architecture with a reworked middle section: the Côte de Saint-Roch now comes later at km 83.7, then the Col de Haussire (last used in 1995) at km 132.4. After Vielsalm, three climbs in 10km - Wanne, Stockeu, and Haute-Levée - ignite the back half. The finale stacks Col du Rosier (longest of the day), Maquisard, Desnié, the iconic Redoute at 34km to go, Côte des Forges at 23.3km, and the final attacking platform on the Roche-aux-Faucons with 13.3km to a fast Liège finish. Ten classified climbs total over a day of relentless attrition.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Saint-Roch
keyClimbs.notes: First major test - short but brutally steep, sets the tone after 83km

keyClimbs.name: Col de Haussire
keyClimbs.notes: Returning to the route for the first time since 1995

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Wanne
keyClimbs.notes: Opens the crucial back-half climbing trilogy

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Stockeu
keyClimbs.notes: Short, vicious pitch - first major selection point

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Haute-Levée
keyClimbs.notes: Third of the Wanne-Stockeu-Haute-Levée trio in quick succession

keyClimbs.name: Col du Rosier
keyClimbs.notes: Longest climb of the day - winding ascent with sharp bends

keyClimbs.name: Col du Maquisard
keyClimbs.notes: Attritional climb between Rosier and the finale

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Desnié
keyClimbs.notes: Softens legs before La Redoute

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Redoute
keyClimbs.notes: The iconic climb - traditional long-range launchpad, narrow and twisting with fans three-deep

keyClimbs.name: Côte des Forges
keyClimbs.notes: Key springboard for attacks between Redoute and Roche-aux-Faucons

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Roche-aux-Faucons
keyClimbs.notes: Final decisive climb - last realistic chance to escape before the fast run-in to Liège

favorites.description: La Doyenne — 250+km Monument over 10 classified Ardennes climbs (La Redoute, Roche-aux-Faucons), a war of attrition that almost always selects on the final hour into Liège. Roche-aux-Faucons (~13km from finish) is the modern decisive climb.
climbers: Tadej Pogačar (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) - three-time winner, the dominant force in the Ardennes with proven ability to solo from La Redoute
Remco Evenepoel (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) - two-time winner on home roads, thrives on La Doyenne's selective finale
Primož Roglič (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) - multi-Monument contender with the explosive punch for the steep Ardennes climbs
Enric Mas (Movistar) - Grand Tour climber who typically contends on longer Classics
puncheurs: Tadej Pogačar (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) - defending champion, calls Liège his most-suited Monument; chasing yet another Ardennes win
Remco Evenepoel (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) - two-time LBL winner, peak Ardennes form after Amstel
Mattias Skjelmose (Lidl-Trek) - climbing into peak form, can follow the heavyweights
Giulio Ciccone (Lidl-Trek) - 2025 LBL runner-up, climbing pedigree perfect for La Doyenne
Juan Ayuso (Lidl-Trek) - third Lidl-Trek leader, aggressive on Roche-aux-Faucons style climbs
Ben Healy (EF Education-EasyPost) - long-range attacker, profile matches modern LBL race patterns
Tom Pidcock (Pinarello-Q36.5) - returning to Ardennes form, podium-capable on the right day

narratives: Pogačar chasing another Monument scalp; could extend his stranglehold on Ardennes Classics
Evenepoel-Pogačar rematch after both targeted LBL — Belgian wants to deny Pogačar on home roads
Lidl-Trek deploys three legitimate co-leaders (Skjelmose/Ciccone/Ayuso) — tactical question of who races for the win
Roche-aux-Faucons (~13km from finish) is the modern decisive climb — whoever attacks there typically wins solo or in a small group
raceDetails.historicalContext: Liège-Bastogne-Liège is the oldest Classic on the calendar, first run in 1892. Known as La Doyenne ('the old lady'), it is the toughest of the Ardennes Classics and the final Monument of the spring. From 1992-2019 the race finished in the industrial suburb of Ans with the Côte de Saint-Nicolas as the final climb; in 2019 the finish returned to central Liège with the Roche-aux-Faucons as the closing selective climb. The race typically covers over 250km with around 4,300m of climbing - Tony Rominger once compared the total elevation to an Alpine stage of the Tour. Iconic climbs include La Redoute, introduced 50 years ago, where legends such as Philippe Gilbert, Bob Jungels, and recent dominators have all made race-winning moves.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The race doesn't really start until the Côte de Saint-Roch at 175km to go, and often sits neutralised through the Haussire. Tune in for the trio of Wanne/Stockeu/Haute-Levée - the first genuine selection. The crucial hour of TV is the final 60km from Col du Rosier onwards. Watch for the first moves on La Redoute (34km to go) - historically where the race is won. The Roche-aux-Faucons is the last chance for escapes; if the group is still together over its summit, expect a bunch kick in Liège. Spoiler caution: YouTube sidebar/autoplay frequently contains the finishing kilometres in thumbnails - disable autoplay and avoid the sidebar.

topRiders.name: SEIXAS Paul
topRiders.outsiderNote: 19yo French phenom — Tour de l'Avenir 2025 winner; Strade Bianche, LBL, and Tour debut on the program

topRiders.name: TIBERI Antonio
topRiders.outsiderNote: Italian GC kid; beat Del Toro on summit at UAE Tour 2026

topRiders.name: BISIAUX Léo
topRiders.outsiderNote: Decathlon's hilly-classics future; Seixas's running mate

topRiders.name: REX Tim
topRiders.outsiderNote: Visma neo-pro; turned heads at Tour de l'Ain 2025

topRiders.name: BAUDIN Alex
topRiders.outsiderNote: Quietly broken through to top-20 of recent-form rankings; sneaky GC threat

races.name: Tour of Türkiye

primary.notes: No confirmed Australian broadcast for this race.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 61st Presidential Cycling Tour of Türkiye is a 2.Pro UCI ProSeries stage race running April 26 - May 3, 2026, across Turkey's scenic coastal and interior regions. The race traditionally traverses the Turquoise Coast and Anatolian interior over 7-8 stages, mixing sprint opportunities, hilly attacking days, and at least one summit-finish queen stage (historically around the Meryem Ana climb or similar Aegean ascents). As a ProSeries event, it draws a mix of WorldTour sprinters, opportunistic breakaway riders, and ProTeam GC hopefuls. Full stage details were being finalized at time of writing - the route typically includes Antalya, the Aegean coast, and inland climbing.

gcContenders: Frank van den Broek (Picnic PostNL) - Dutch climber targeting GC in ProSeries stage races
Kevin Vauquelin (Arkéa-B&B Hotels) - previous Turkey podium finisher, all-rounder
Eddie Dunbar (Jayco AlUla) - Irish climber using Turkey as late-spring racing
Hugh Carthy (EF Education-EasyPost) - climbing specialist often racing ProSeries events
Santiago Buitrago (Bahrain Victorious) - climbing specialist if racing calendar aligns
stageHunters: Mark Cavendish - if racing, always a sprint threat on flat stages
Jakub Mareczko (various teams across years) - Italian sprinter with Turkey pedigree
Phil Bauhaus (Bahrain Victorious) - German sprinter, multi-time Turkey stage winner
Kaden Groves (Alpecin-Deceuninck) - fast finisher targeting ProSeries wins
Olav Kooij (Visma-Lease a Bike) - young Dutch sprinter

narratives: A showcase of Turkey's cycling ambition - the race runs along the Turquoise Coast and Aegean regions featuring dramatic scenery
Mix of WorldTour teams and ProTeam squads creates tactical variety and opportunity for outsiders
The Meryem Ana or similar summit finishes typically decide the GC - a single mountain day can be decisive
Sprint stages attract top WorldTour finishers - Turkey is one of the spring's best ProSeries sprint battlegrounds
Race often runs into early May, overlapping with the Giro d'Italia start
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1963, the Presidential Cycling Tour of Türkiye is Turkey's most prestigious stage race and one of the longest-running international events in Asia/Middle East. It is a staple of the UCI ProSeries calendar and has hosted some of cycling's best sprinters and stage hunters. Mark Cavendish is a multiple stage winner. The race's profile has elevated since its UCI WorldTour inclusion in certain years, and it continues to draw a strong international field. The iconic Meryem Ana climb on the Aegean coast has historically decided the GC.
raceDetails.gcDynamics: GC is typically decided on one or two mountain stages - the rest of the race is shaped by sprint stages and bonus seconds. Riders with a strong climbing day and solid consistency through sprint stages (avoiding time losses) are the traditional winners. Echelons on crosswind-exposed coastal roads can occasionally cause surprise time gaps. The queen stage is usually the focal point.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Tune in for the designated mountain stage(s) - historically this is the most important GC day. Sprint stages provide top-tier finishes from WorldTour fast men. Live coverage is often available via Eurosport/Discovery+ in Europe. Spoiler caution: ProSeries races sometimes have limited live coverage internationally, so on-demand viewing risks sidebar spoilers. Specific stage details to be finalized - stages array below reflects a typical 8-stage template.

stages.name: Stage 1: Opening stage
stages.description: Opening stage typically for the sprinters - specific route to be confirmed.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The race traditionally opens with a flat coastal stage suited to the sprinters. Historical start locations include Antalya and other Mediterranean cities. Expect a largely flat profile with a bunch sprint finish unless crosswinds disrupt the plan.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch for the opening sprint battle among WorldTour finishers - Turkey's early sprint stages often crown the week's first stage winner.

stages.name: Stage 2: Second stage
stages.description: Second stage typically sprint-friendly - route TBD.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Typically a second flat/rolling stage for the sprinters. Profile historically includes coastal roads with limited elevation gain. Crosswind risk on some editions.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Another sprint opportunity for the fast men. Watch for echelon action if wind is a factor.

stages.name: Stage 3: Hilly/Transitional
stages.description: Transitional hilly stage before the queen stage - route TBD.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A typically hilly transitional stage with a few short climbs. Could favour a breakaway or a reduced-bunch sprint from a group of puncheurs and classics-style riders.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Breakaway day is possible - watch for aggressive attacks from non-GC riders hunting a stage win before the mountain day.

stages.name: Stage 4: Queen stage (summit finish)
stages.description: Queen stage with summit finish - historically the GC-deciding day, often featuring the Meryem Ana climb.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The GC-deciding stage of the race. Historically featured a summit finish on the Aegean coast near the iconic Meryem Ana climb (4.2km at 8.4%) or similar. The biggest elevation day of the race with multiple classified climbs and a demanding final ascent.
stageDetails.watchNotes: THE critical stage for the overall. GC contenders will emerge in the final climb. Watch for bonus-second swings on intermediate climbs as well. Disable YouTube autoplay - stage highlights reveal the winner.

stages.name: Stage 5: Transitional
stages.description: Transitional stage post-queen - could be sprint or breakaway depending on final route.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A transitional stage between the queen stage and the final sprint days. Typically a mix of rolling terrain with the potential for either a sprint or breakaway finish.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Often a day for the breakaway with GC teams recovering from the queen stage. Watch for aggressive riders targeting a stage win.

stages.name: Stage 6: Flat/Sprint stage
stages.description: Sprint-friendly stage - route TBD.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Typically a flat sprint stage approaching the final stages of the race. Expect bunch finishes with WorldTour fast men contesting the win.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Sprint battle for the fast men. Points classification often comes alive here.

stages.name: Stage 7: Hilly/late-race
stages.description: Late-race hilly stage - route TBD.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A later-race hilly stage that could shake up the GC if gaps are tight, or otherwise produce a breakaway or small-group finish.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch for attacks if GC is still undecided. Last chance for breakaway-focused riders before the final sprint day.

stages.name: Stage 8: Final stage (Istanbul)
stages.description: Traditional final stage on a flat circuit - sprinter's celebration.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Traditionally a flat finale on a circuit course, often in Istanbul. The final sprint for the fast men and celebration for the overall leader on the final podium.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Sprint day - watch for the final battle among the top fast men. Also a chance to see the GC leader cross the line victorious.

races.name: Liège-Bastogne-Liège (Women)

primary.notes: Dedicated Peacock Femmes page. 156km Bastogne–Liège. $10.99/mo Premium.
Cycling moved Discovery+ → HBO Max on March 26, 2026. Same-day Ardennes coverage with men's race.
Femmes streamed alongside men's coverage; specific event page TBC closer to race day. CA$49.99/mo.
Free-to-air on SBS Viceland + SBS On Demand. Dedicated women's series page confirmed.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 10th edition of Liège-Bastogne-Liège Femmes extends the route slightly from Bastogne to Liège, approximately 156km with ten climbs and 2,200m of elevation gain. Unlike previous years, the women skip the Côte de Saint-Roch, instead opening with the Col de Haussire at km 28.9. The decisive stretch begins with Wanne (3.6km at 5.1%) at 88.4km to go, followed quickly by Stockeu (1km at 12.5%) and Haute-Levée (2.2km at 7.5%). After the Col du Rosier (4.4km at 5.9% with 63.3km to go) and Maquisard, the race attacks the iconic La Redoute with 34km remaining, then Côte de Cornémont and Côte des Forges, before the Côte de la Roche-aux-Faucons (1.3km at 11%) serves as the final launchpad 13.4km from the line. A flat, fast run-in to Liège follows.

keyClimbs.notes: Opens the climbing after the early warm-up flat

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Wanne
keyClimbs.notes: Start of the crucial 10km triple-punch

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Stockeu
keyClimbs.notes: Brutally steep - first real selection point

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Haute-Levée
keyClimbs.notes: Third climb of the trio - legs start to fracture the group

keyClimbs.name: Col du Rosier
keyClimbs.notes: Longest climb of the race - extended effort tests durability

keyClimbs.name: Col du Maquisard
keyClimbs.notes: Transition climb into the finale

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Desnié
keyClimbs.notes: Softens legs before La Redoute

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Roche-aux-Faucons
keyClimbs.notes: The final decisive climb - last real chance to go clear before Liège

favorites.description: 156km Ardennes Monument finale with new 2026 climbs (Col du Maquisard, Côte de Desnié) before La Redoute, making the run-in even more selective and climber-friendly than ever.
climbers: Demi Vollering (FDJ-SUEZ) - European champion, previous Ardennes dominator who swept the triple in 2023
Elisa Longo Borghini (UAE Team ADQ) - two-time Monument winner with the climbing strength for La Doyenne
Kasia Niewiadoma-Phinney (Canyon//SRAM zondacrypto) - former Flèche Wallonne winner, always a contender in the Ardennes
Cédrine Kerbaol (EF Education-Oatly) - emerging climbing talent with strong long-range aggression
puncheurs: Demi Vollering (FDJ-SUEZ) - two-time LBL winner (2023, 2024), 2025 third — headline favourite
Puck Pieterse (Fenix-Deceuninck) - rising Ardennes star coming off strong Amstel/Flèche campaign
Elisa Longo Borghini (UAE Team ADQ) - climber profile suits the lengthened LBL route
Lotte Kopecky (SD Worx-Protime) - world champion, top-5 last year, leads strong SD Worx unit
Katarzyna Niewiadoma (Canyon//SRAM zondacrypto) - pure climber, Ardennes Classics specialist
Marlen Reusser (Movistar) - powerful and tactically smart, dangerous on selective finale
Cédrine Kerbaol (EF Education-Oatly) - aggressive climber, recent Ardennes podiums

narratives: Defending champion Kim Le Court-Pienaar is OUT after wrist fracture at Flanders — title is wide open
New climbs Col du Maquisard and Côte de Desnié before La Redoute mean fewer survivors and a more climber-friendly race than ever
Vollering bidding for third LBL crown to match the Anna van der Breggen-era dominance
FDJ-SUEZ vs SD Worx-Protime vs Visma trident — the three super-teams set the tactical chessboard
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 2017, Liège-Bastogne-Liège Femmes quickly established itself as one of the toughest one-day events on the women's calendar, following the men's template across the Ardennes hills. The race's 10 editions have seen dominance from Anna van der Breggen, Demi Vollering, and Elisa Longo Borghini among others. As the closing Ardennes Monument, it rewards riders who have carried form through Amstel Gold and Flèche Wallonne.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The key window for viewing is the final 90 minutes - from the Wanne-Stockeu-Haute-Levée trio at 88km to go through to the finish. La Redoute at 34km remaining is historically where the winning move is launched; if the group is still together on the Roche-aux-Faucons, watch for explosive attacks in the final 13km. Spoiler caution: avoid YouTube autoplay and sidebar recommendations as these frequently preview the finale.

Per youtube-cycling-analyzer: USABLE — neutral title, official broadcaster, substantial 17-min extended highlights package; winner reveal expected only at the natural finish line per the broadcast standard. Tier 1 official broadcaster (NBC Sports). Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 79th Tour de Romandie covers 851.9km across six days (April 28 - May 3) through the French-speaking regions of Switzerland, with 14,183m of total elevation gain and 17 classified climbs. The 2026 edition swaps last year's two time trials for a single opening prologue in Villars-sur-Glâne, adding 165km to the road-racing total. The sole mountaintop finish comes on the concluding Stage 5 at Leysin overlooking the Rhone Valley and Lake Geneva. Each road stage offers three or four classified climbs, creating a punchy, aggressive race that traditionally serves as the final Giro d'Italia tune-up for Grand Tour contenders.

gcContenders: Tadej Pogačar (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) - Romandie debut, world champion, Tour de France prep
Primož Roglič (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) - two-time Romandie winner (2018/2019), home-tier roads
Florian Lipowitz (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) - co-leader option for Red Bull, rising GC star
Lenny Martinez (Bahrain-Victorious) - pure climber, Leysin profile suits perfectly
Antonio Tiberi (Bahrain-Victorious) - GC contender continuing 2025 trajectory
Matteo Jorgenson (Team Visma | Lease a Bike) - all-rounder, past Romandie podium pedigree
stageHunters: Stefan Küng (Groupama-FDJ United) - prologue specialist, strong TT pedigree
Rémi Cavagna (Decathlon CMA CGM) - TT specialist with classics strength
Matteo Jorgenson (Visma-Lease a Bike) - attacking climber who targets stage wins
favorites.description: 6-stage Swiss WorldTour race opening with a 3.2km prologue and ending on a Leysin summit finish; no long ITT this year shifts the GC entirely toward climbers.

narratives: Pogačar–Roglič reunion — first Romandie meeting since 2020 Tour de France drama
Pogačar's first Romandie start — validation of the race's prestige and a new Tour-prep template
No long ITT in 2026 (just 3.2km prologue) tilts everything toward Leysin summit finish — pure climbers favoured
Red Bull duo Roglič/Lipowitz outnumbers UAE — tactical question of who attacks Pogačar first
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1947, the Tour de Romandie is one of Switzerland's premier stage races and a staple of the WorldTour calendar. Past winners form a who's who of cycling: Eddy Merckx, Bernard Hinault, Stephen Roche, Tony Rominger, Tyler Hamilton (later voided), and in recent decades Simon Špilak, Chris Froome, Primož Roglič, Geraint Thomas, Aleksandr Vlasov, Adam Yates, Carlos Rodríguez, and João Almeida. The mountaintop finish at Leysin has become iconic - in 2024 Juan Ayuso's GC lead famously collapsed there, with Carlos Rodríguez taking over and winning the race.
raceDetails.gcDynamics: With only the Leysin mountain finish on Stage 5 as a pure summit test, the GC battle builds slowly. The opening 3.2km prologue provides a few seconds of initial separation. Stages 1-4 feature punchy climbs and bonus sprints but most finish with descents or flat run-ins, keeping GC gaps tight. Expect the overall to be decided in the final 15.6km climb to Leysin on the last day - a 10.9km climb at 6.7% average that has historically produced dramatic GC swings.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Tune in for the Leysin mountain finish on Stage 5 - it's almost always GC-decisive. Stage 1 (Martigny) features a 12km 9.1% climb at Ovronnaz that could create early gaps. Stage 4 (Broc-Charmey) is the second hardest day with back-to-back Jaunpass climbs. The prologue is typically decided in fractions of a second. Spoiler caution: GC results can leak via sidebar recommendations - disable YouTube autoplay.

keyClimbs.name: Charmey
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 4 summit finish in the Pre-Alps — first GC-decisive climb of the race.

keyClimbs.name: Leysin
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 5 queen-stage summit finish in the Vaudoise Alps — the GC-decisive climb of the 2026 Tour de Romandie.

stages.name: Prologue: Villars-sur-Glâne (ITT)
stages.description: Short opening prologue around Villars-sur-Glâne to set the initial GC order.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A 3.2km loop around Villars-sur-Glâne opens the 2026 Tour de Romandie. Two 90-degree turns on the approach to the finish line are the only obstacles on paper, while unpredictable spring weather in Switzerland could become a factor. Historically the Romandie prologue is decided in fractions of a second, though Rohan Dennis's 9-second margin in 2021 shows the stage can occasionally create bigger gaps.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Prologue specialists and TT-capable GC riders will contest the win and secure a few seconds of advantage. Watch the weather - rain could tip the balance between specialists and conservative riders. The opening time gaps set the tactical landscape for the rest of the race.

stages.name: Stage 1: Martigny - Martigny
stages.description: Valley-road stage ending with a 12km climb at Ovronnaz (9.1% avg) before a 33km descent to Martigny for a likely reduced sprint.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 1 stays mainly on the Rhone Valley roads to and from Martigny. Three circuits to the north-west cross a category 3 La Passe climb. After following the Rhone to an intermediate sprint in Saxon, the peloton hits the first major climb of the week: Ovronnaz, a 12km ascent averaging 9.1%. From the summit there are 33km remaining - mostly descent - back to Martigny, where a reduced bunch sprint is the expected outcome.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The Ovronnaz climb at 12km 9.1% is a serious climber's test. GC contenders can use it to drop rivals, but 33km of descending gives reduced-group pure climbers a tough task to hold off the bunch. Expect a fast finish in Martigny from a group of 20-40 riders.

stages.name: Stage 2: Rue - Vucherens
stages.description: Relentless punchy day with three circuits over the cat-3 Vuillens climb - stage for puncheurs.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 2 covers 173.1km from the medieval village of Rue to Vucherens, though the two towns are less than 10km apart. The serpentine route passes the finish line early for a trio of circuits, each featuring a third-category climb of Vuillens just after an intermediate sprint in Chapelle. From the final Vuillens summit only 3.8km remain for a fast finish - a classic puncheur's day.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Three passes of Vuillens will whittle down the group, with the final ascent as the launchpad for attacks. The 3.8km from summit to finish is just long enough for determined attackers to stay clear or for a fast reduced sprint. Watch for repeat aggression from classics-style riders looking for a stage win.

stages.name: Stage 3: Orbe - Orbe
stages.description: Jura Mountains stage with the major 21.8km Col Mollendruz climb before a 32km descent to Orbe.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 3 loops 176.6km around the medieval town of Orbe in a valley of vineyards surrounded by narrow gorges and limestone cliffs. After the early Suchy climb and rolling uncategorised terrain, the final 64.6km loop south features the third-category Oulens-Sur-Echallens followed by the stage's big test: the Col Mollendruz, a 21.8km ascent in the Jura Mountains. From the top, all 32km back to Orbe are downhill.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The 21.8km Col Mollendruz is one of the longest climbs of the week and could produce major GC action - though the 32km descent gives dropped riders a chance to regroup. Bold descenders may take the stage from a small group. Potential for breakaway success if the GC teams don't control aggressively.

stages.name: Stage 4: Broc - Charmey
stages.description: Shortest road stage but hardest per kilometre - two category-2 and two category-1 climbs with double Jaunpass ascents.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 4 packs heavy climbing into 149.6km. Both sides of Jaunpass are climbed: the shorter 5.6km side averages 8.3% with pitches at 10.3%, while the longer 8km side averages 7.8% with 9.3% sections. Between Jaunpass ascents the peloton loops through Gruyères, Les Moulins, and Saanen for the category-2 climb of Saanenmöser. A second Jaunpass ascent comes on the long side with 17km remaining to the finish.
