stageDetails.watchNotes: Density-wise this is the hardest stage of the race - four major climbs in 149.6km. The final 17km after the second Jaunpass favours a strong descender or a small group with 10+ seconds of advantage. Expect the GC to be reshuffled here before the Leysin finale.

stages.name: Stage 5: Lucens - Leysin
stages.description: The GC-deciding mountain finish at Leysin - 10.9km at 6.7% average over Lake Geneva.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The final and only mountaintop finish of the 2026 Tour de Romandie. The 178.2km journey begins on the banks of the Broye River in Lucens. The first 70km feature four uncategorised climbs and two category 3 ascents (Sottens and Vuillens). An intermediate sprint at Chardonne brings the race close to Lake Geneva before heading inland for another climb at Châtel-St-Denis. The finale: the climb to Leysin with 15.6km remaining, with the bulk covering 10.9km at 6.7% average.
stageDetails.watchNotes: THE GC-deciding stage. The Leysin summit finish has historically produced dramatic race-winning moves - most famously Juan Ayuso's 2024 GC collapse. Tune in for the final 60km when the race hits Châtel-St-Denis; the final 10.9km climb is destination viewing. Disable YouTube autoplay - the finale is heavily covered in highlights.

topRiders.name: TIBERI Antonio
topRiders.outsiderNote: Italian GC kid; beat Del Toro on summit at UAE Tour 2026

topRiders.name: SÖDERQVIST Jakob
topRiders.outsiderNote: Swedish engine; U23 TT podiumer and Tour of Denmark GC 2nd, neo-pro 2026

topRiders.name: WITHEN PHILIPSEN Albert
topRiders.outsiderNote: Danish prodigy named alongside Seixas as next-gen flag-bearer

raceDetails.courseSummary: 211.4km WorldTour one-day race from Eschborn into the Taunus hills west of Frankfurt and back to a circuit finish in central Frankfurt on May 1 (German Labour Day). Roughly 2,000m of climbing centred on multiple ascents of the Feldberg and the savagely steep Mammolshain.

keyClimbs.name: Großer Feldberg (long side)
keyClimbs.notes: First major ascent, used to thin the field early.

keyClimbs.name: Großer Feldberg (short side)
keyClimbs.notes: Second pass — selection forms here.

keyClimbs.name: Mammolshain
keyClimbs.notes: Final ascent of the brutal 'Wall of Mammolshain' before the run-in to Frankfurt — race-decisive.

favorites.description: A puncheur-friendly classic that splits between an uphill-savvy sprinter or a small late breakaway. Reduced bunch sprint or a 5-10 rider front group typical.
puncheurs: HIRSCHI Marc
POWLESS Neilson
BARRENETXEA Jon
ALAPHILIPPE Julian

narratives: 60th edition of a German classic that has been a constant on May 1 (Labour Day) for six decades and is in its 10th year at WorldTour level.
Bridge race between the Ardennes Classics and the Giro d'Italia — a final tune-up for puncheurs and a chance for Classics survivors to grab one more big result.
The Feldberg–Mammolshain combo regularly makes the bunch sprint a 'reduced' affair: the question each year is whether the puncheurs can drop the fast finishers before Frankfurt.
Strong German home crowd along the Feldberg climbs creates Tour-style atmosphere — a rare Bundesrepublik WorldTour day.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1962 and known historically as Rund um den Henninger-Turm. Norway's Alexander Kristoff won four straight editions (2014–2018). Promoted to WorldTour status in 2017.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Tune in for the second Feldberg pass with about 90km to go. The third Mammolshain ascent inside 35km to go is where the winning move usually goes. If a small group survives to the Frankfurt circuit it will sprint between the Alte Oper and the Mainufer.

topRiders.name: BISIAUX Léo
topRiders.outsiderNote: Decathlon's hilly-classics future; Seixas's running mate

raceDetails.courseSummary: Portuguese UCI 1.2 one-day race held on May 1 around Lousada in northern Portugal. A rolling parcours on narrow Minho roads, typically about 175km, that suits a puncheur or a strong breakaway out of the local Continental peloton.

favorites.description: A stage primarily contested by Portuguese Continental teams (Glassdrive, Efapel, Tavfer) with an occasional ProTeam visitor. Expect a Portuguese national to take it from a small group.
puncheurs: Riders from Glassdrive Q8 Anicolor
Riders from Efapel-Cabreiroá
Riders from Anicolor-Tien21

narratives: Title-sponsor home race for Anicolor — the team usually attacks aggressively to defend its branded race.
First leg of the Portuguese May classic block that bleeds into GP Beiras and the national racing season.
Calendar fixture for U23 prospects looking to make the jump to ProTeams via Portuguese feeders.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Modern UCI 1.2 race created in the 2010s by the Anicolor flooring company alongside its Continental cycling team, contested mainly by Iberian Continental squads.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Coverage is limited — Portuguese Federation often posts a finish-line clip on YouTube. The decisive split usually happens on one of the late short climbs in the final 30km.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Belgian UCI 1.2 kermesse-style one-day race held in East Flanders on May 1. Roughly 180km of flat-to-rolling Flemish roads with a small finishing circuit and the obligatory short cobbled drags — bunch sprint or small late break typical.

favorites.description: A field of Belgian Continental and ProTeam riders looking to grab early-season UCI points. Sprinters and rouleurs both have a shot.

narratives: Local kermesse with UCI status — a chance for Belgian Continental teams to grab points and a podium close to home.
Title sponsor (a flooring contractor) keeps this race grassroots — typical of the dense Flemish spring calendar.
Often a first race back for riders who skipped the Ardennes block and are easing toward summer Belgian semi-classics.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Newer addition to the Flemish calendar (mid-2010s onward), running under various title sponsors. Strictly a regional Belgian event; not part of the WorldTour or ProSeries calendar.
raceDetails.watchNotes: No live broadcast expected; check Sporza or local Belgian media for highlight clips. Watch for an attack on the final cobbled section in the closing 10km.

races.name: Giro Sardegna Gravel
races.description: UCI Gravel World Series qualifier. Top 25% in each category earn entry to the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships (Nannup, Australia) and 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships (Houffalize, Belgium).

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gravel race in Muravera – Villasimius, Italy. Elite men's and elite women's classifications contest the same course.

broadcast.notes: UCI Gravel World Series events are typically not livestreamed; results and short recap clips appear on the UCI YouTube channel and event social media.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Luxembourg women's UCI ProSeries weekend held in Garnich and Cessange on May 2 (and surrounding day). The 2024 and 2025 editions were a two-day double one-day race format of roughly 110-120km each, on punchy circuits with a 13% kicker per lap and uphill-leaning finishes.

favorites.description: A field that mixes WorldTour squads using Elsy Jacobs as a tune-up for the Vuelta Femenina with strong Continental teams hunting points.
puncheurs: LACH Marta
FIDANZA Martina
CONFALONIERI Maria Giulia

narratives: Tribute race to Luxembourgish cycling pioneer Elsy Jacobs (1959 women's road world champion).
Final tune-up for many WWT-bound climbers and rouleurs before La Vuelta Femenina starts the following day.
Uphill finishes have favoured punchy, classics-style winners in recent editions — pure sprinters rarely get a clean result here.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Held since 2008 in honour of Luxembourg's Elsy Jacobs. Originally a stage race; reformatted into back-to-back one-day races in 2024 to fit the new May calendar block.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Free organiser livestream typically available on the Festival Elsy Jacobs website. Action ignites on the steep Garnich climb in the final two laps.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Belgian UCI 1.1 one-day race based in Marche-en-Famenne on May 3. Roughly 187-195km combining a long opening loop with multiple finishing circuits over the Côte de Roy (3.4km at 3.9%), with the Côte de Waharday (1.3km at 8.1%) as the punchiest kicker.

keyClimbs.name: Côte du Crawy
keyClimbs.notes: Mid-race drag, helps form the early break.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Nassogne
keyClimbs.notes: Longest sustained climb of the day.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Waharday
keyClimbs.notes: Steepest pitch on the parcours — frequent attack point.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Roy (finishing circuit)
keyClimbs.notes: Tackled four times in the local laps; the final ascent puts the climbers and puncheurs in front but leaves the door open for a bunch sprint.

favorites.description: A puncheur-sprinter classic that has historically split between fast finishers who survive Côte de Roy and Belgian classics riders who attack on the final lap.
puncheurs: DE LIE Arnaud
ZINGLE Axel
VAN GILS Maxim
MEEUS Jordi

narratives: Lotto-Dstny (now Lotto-Intermarché) traditionally defends this race for Arnaud De Lie, who has won it twice.
XDS Astana's emerging sprint train (Kanter / Bol) gave them a 1-2 in 2025; expect them to ride for that combo again.
One of the few 1.1 one-day races between the Ardennes and Eschborn that consistently attracts a strong WorldTour-flavoured field.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First run in 2019 as a successor to Tour de la Famenne. Title sponsor Lotto and the Belgian Federation have built it into the strongest 1.1 race in the Belgian Ardennes.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch for the Côte de Waharday on the final long loop, then the third and fourth ascents of Côte de Roy on the local circuit. Attacks land in the last 10km on the final Roy climb.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Historic Italian UCI 1.1 one-day race in the Ligurian Apennines on May 3, traditionally finishing in or near Genoa after roughly 195km. The defining feature is the Passo della Bocchetta (8.0km at 7.7%), a long Apennine pass that leads into a fast technical descent toward the Genoese coast.

keyClimbs.name: Passo della Bocchetta
keyClimbs.notes: The legendary Apennine pass that defines this race — long, steady, and the launchpad for almost every winning move.

keyClimbs.name: Crocefieschi
keyClimbs.notes: Mid-race climb that softens up the field before the Bocchetta.

favorites.description: A pure climbers' classic. Italian ProTeam climbers and emerging GC talents typically dominate. Expect a small lead group over the Bocchetta and a winner from a 1-3 rider move.
climbers: Italian ProTeam climbers from Polti VisitMalta and Q36.5
puncheurs: GHEBREIGZABHIER Amanuel

narratives: Race of the Apennines — Italian climbing tradition dating to 1934 in the same Genoese mountains where Coppi and Bartali raced.
Final tune-up for Italian climbers heading into the Giro d'Italia, which starts five days later.
Climbers from Italian ProTeams use it as a showcase to attract bigger contracts; a Bocchetta KOM still carries weight on a CV.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1934. Past winners include Fausto Coppi (multiple times), Felice Gimondi, and Gianni Bugno. Now organised by US Pontedecimo and traditionally finishes in the Genoese suburb of Pontedecimo.
raceDetails.watchNotes: If you can find live coverage (rare for 1.1 races), tune in for the Bocchetta ascent — usually inside the final 35-50km. The descent into Genoa is often where the winner consolidates a solo move.

races.name: Vuelta España Femenina

raceDetails.courseSummary: Seven-stage Women's WorldTour Grand Tour, May 3-9 2026. The 12th edition tackles 819.5km through Galicia, León, and Asturias with no time trial but a brutal back-to-back finale: stage 6 summit finish at Les Praeres.Nava and stage 7 atop the legendary Alto de l'Angliru. One of the toughest routes in race history.

keyClimbs.name: Les Praeres.Nava
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 6 summit finish — savage Asturian wall used in the men's Vuelta as a GC sorting point.

keyClimbs.name: Alto de l'Angliru
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 7 finish — first time in the women's Vuelta. The Cueña les Cabres ramps top out above 23%. GC-decisive.

favorites.description: Pure climbers' Vuelta — the GC is decided in Asturias. Two summit finishes in the final 24 hours mean attacks from far out and significant time gaps.
gcContenders: VOLLERING Demi
REUSSER Marlen
NIEWIADOMA-PHINNEY Kasia
GIGANTE Sarah
VAN DER BREGGEN Anna
MUZIC Évita

narratives: First-ever finish atop l'Angliru in the Women's Vuelta — historic moment for women's cycling on Spain's most mythical climb.
Vollering vs Reusser rematch after their 2025 Grand Tour duels; FDJ-Suez again organising the race around their leader.
Anna van der Breggen's likely final Grand Tour appearance — the retired-then-returned legend chasing one more major result.
Sarah Gigante's first full Vuelta start at WorldTour level — Australian climbing prodigy targeting a top-5 GC.
Galician opening week with no flat finish day puts pressure on the GC from the gun — hard to control, easy to lose minutes.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Established in 2015 as Madrid Challenge by La Vuelta, expanded to a multi-day race in 2023 and to Women's WorldTour status. Annemiek van Vleuten won the first three multi-day editions; Demi Vollering won in 2024 and 2025.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch every kilometre of stage 6 (Les Praeres) and stage 7 (Angliru) — the entire GC will be decided across those two summit finishes. Stages 1-5 are likely break-friendly transitional days but contain enough lumps that crosswinds or splits could cost a contender minutes.

stages.name: Stage 1: Marín → Salvaterra de Miño
stages.description: Galician opener — 114km from Marín on the Atlantic coast to inland Salvaterra de Miño.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Hilly Galician stage. Punchy terrain favours puncheurs over pure sprinters from day one.

stages.name: Stage 2: Lobios → San Cibrao das Viñas
stages.description: 110km through southern Galicia from Lobios to San Cibrao das Viñas — hilly terrain.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling Galician stage — climbers and puncheurs can stretch the field.

stages.name: Stage 3: Padrón → A Coruña
stages.description: Flat sprint day from Padrón to the Atlantic city of A Coruña.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Flat sprint stage to A Coruña — sprinters' teams in control.

stages.name: Stage 4: Monforte de Lemos → Antas de Ulla
stages.description: 114km hilly stage through Lugo province — Antas de Ulla finish.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling inland Galician stage. Breakaway or punchy GC moves possible.

stages.name: Stage 5: León → Astorga
stages.description: Flat Castilian stage from León to Astorga — sprint chance before the mountains.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Flat 120km between two historic Castilian cities. Last chance for the sprinters.

stages.name: Stage 6: Gijón/Xixón → Les Praeres (Nava)
stages.description: First Asturian mountain stage — short 106km with brutal summit finish at Les Praeres in Nava.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Asturian mountain stage to the legendary Les Praeres summit finish. Short and savage — gaps guaranteed.
stageDetails.watchNotes: First major GC selection. Les Praeres is one of the toughest summit finishes in women's racing.

stages.name: Stage 7: La Pola Llaviana → L'Angliru
stages.description: Queen stage: 133km finishing on the legendary Angliru — one of cycling's most feared climbs.

stageDetails.courseSummary: L'Angliru queen stage. 12.5km at 9.8% average, max gradients above 23%. GC settled here.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The Angliru is a cycling legend — final-day fireworks expected on the steepest ramps.

races.videoNote: Stage 6 of the 2026 La Vuelta Femenina — NBC Sports (21:20). Per youtube-cycling-analyzer: USABLE/SAFE — 21 minutes of official summit-finish coverage at Les Praeres de Nava (queen-stage prologue), winner not named in the scraped transcript; end-only spoiler pattern expected. Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations. Stage 7 / L'Angliru clip would be the ideal race-level pick once a clean source surfaces.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Greek UCI 2.1 stage race held May 6-10. Five stages totalling roughly 800km moving through Crete and the Greek mainland with a mix of hilly stages, one short summit-leaning day, and at least one bunch-sprint opportunity.

favorites.description: A field dominated by ProTeams using Hellas as race-day mileage and Continental teams hunting UCI points.
gcContenders: Riders from Caja Rural-Seguros RGA
Riders from Burgos-BH
Riders from Equipo Kern Pharma
sprinters: Riders from Kazakhstan Pro Cycling Team

narratives: Greek showcase race — beautiful Mediterranean scenery on Crete and the Peloponnese is half the appeal.
Continental teams treat it as a points hunt; ProTeams use it as a low-pressure block of racing.
Stage profiles are unusual for May — the wind off the Aegean often shapes the GC more than the climbs.
raceDetails.historicalContext: International Tour of Hellas, revived in 2021 after a long hiatus, run as a UCI 2.1 by the Greek Cycling Federation.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Limited live coverage — the Hellenic federation sometimes streams on Facebook/YouTube. Watch for crosswind splits on the coastal stages and a hilltop finish in the second half of the race.

stages.name: Stage 1: Ioannina → Agrinio
stages.description: Opening 172km from Ioannina across the Pindus range to Agrinio.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling terrain across northwestern Greece. First leader's jersey via reduced bunch.

stages.name: Stage 2: Karpenisi → Larissa
stages.description: 157km mountain stage from Karpenisi descending to the Thessalian plain at Larissa.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Mountainous Greek terrain from Karpenisi. Major climbing day — GC contenders will mark each other.

stages.name: Stage 3: Volos → Lamia
stages.description: Longest stage at 207km — Volos to Lamia with 4,200m+ of climbing.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Race's longest and one of the hardest stages — 207km with 4,200m+ elevation. Breakaway potential.

stages.name: Stage 4: Atalanti → Mount Parnitha
stages.description: Queen stage — 181km finishing on Mount Parnitha above Athens.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Summit finish on Mount Parnitha overlooking Athens. The GC decider of the race.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Final mountain test — gaps here will define the GC.

stages.name: Stage 5: Piraeus → Athens (Syntagma Square)
stages.description: Ceremonial finale — 147km from Piraeus to Syntagma Square in front of the Greek Parliament.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Ceremonial yet fast-paced finish at Syntagma Square — Greek Parliament backdrop. Champs-Élysées-style.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Bunch sprint finale on the urban circuit.

raceDetails.courseSummary: 109th edition of the Corsa Rosa, May 8-31 2026. 21 stages covering 3,466 km with 49,150 m of climbing — eight flat sprint stages, seven medium-mountain days, seven summit finishes, and a single 40.2km individual time trial in Tuscany. Three opening stages in Bulgaria (the 16th foreign Grande Partenza in race history) before transferring to Italy via Calabria, Naples, the Apennines, Tuscany, Milan, and a brutal final week in the Alps and Dolomites with the return of the Passo Giau. Concludes in Rome on May 31.

keyClimbs.name: Blockhaus
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 7 summit finish in the central Apennines after a 244km marathon stage — first major GC test.

keyClimbs.name: Corno alle Scale
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 9 summit finish ending the first week and first Giro visit since the 1980s — Pantani's training mountain.

keyClimbs.name: Passo Giau
keyClimbs.notes: Returns to the Giro for the first time since 2021 — Cima Coppi candidate in the Dolomites.

keyClimbs.name: Pila
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 14 short, intense 133km mountain stage in the Val d'Aosta with summit finish above Aosta.

keyClimbs.name: Piancavallo
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 20 finishing climb (penultimate stage) — last chance for GC moves before the Rome circuit.

favorites.description: Race director Mauro Vegni's farewell route is deliberately built to tempt Giro-Tour doubles. Backloaded mountains and only one ITT favour pure climbers but leave the door open for puncheurs and rouleurs in the first 10 days.
gcContenders: VINGEGAARD Jonas
EVENEPOEL Remco
ALMEIDA João
O'CONNOR Ben
DEL TORO Isaac
ROGLIČ Primož
YATES Simon
ONLEY Oscar
puncheurs: PEDERSEN Mads
POWLESS Neilson
CARTHY Hugh

narratives: Vingegaard vs Evenepoel — a likely first head-to-head Grand Tour duel between the Dane and the Belgian World Champion-elect, both reportedly using the Giro as their season's main focus.

Foreign Grande Partenza in Bulgaria — first time the race has visited the Balkans, with three flat-to-rolling stages around Nessebar, Burgas, Veliko Tarnovo, Plovdiv and Sofia before the rest day transfer.
Defending champion Simon Yates returns as Visma-Lease a Bike's leader option — first man since the Schleck era to defend a Grand Tour for the same team in a different year.
Mauro Vegni's last route as race director — described as 'modern, balanced but tough' and explicitly engineered to entice Giro-Tour doubles in 2026.
Eight flat stages give Jonathan Milan, Mads Pedersen, Tim Merlier and Jasper Philipsen a real chance at the maglia ciclamino — sprint duels could shape the first half of the race.
Return of the Passo Giau in the Dolomites — last raced in 2021 when Egan Bernal solo'd to glory; cima Coppi territory if the snow allows.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1909. The 2025 edition was won by Simon Yates (Visma-Lease a Bike). RCS Sport organises. 16th foreign Grande Partenza in race history.
raceDetails.watchNotes: First week: watch stage 7 to Blockhaus (244km of suffering) and stage 9 to Corno alle Scale. Second week opens with the Tuscan ITT (stage 10) and the only ITT of the race — pivotal for time-trialists like Evenepoel. Third week: stages 18, 19, 20 in the Alps/Dolomites with Passo Giau and Piancavallo will decide the maglia rosa before Sunday's Roman parade.

stages.name: Stage 1: Nessebar → Burgas
stages.description: Opening flat stage along the Bulgarian Black Sea coast. Sprinters' day.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Flat coastal stage from Nessebar to Burgas in Bulgaria, the Grande Partenza host country.
stageDetails.watchNotes: First maglia rosa on offer — bunch sprint expected. Watch for crosswinds along the coast.

stages.name: Stage 2: Burgas → Veliko Tarnovo
stages.description: Hilly Bulgarian stage with three Cat 3 climbs. Day for the puncheurs.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Long rolling stage with Byala Pass, Vratnik Pass and the Lyaskovets Monastery climb shaping the finale.

stages.name: Stage 3: Plovdiv → Sofia
stages.description: Final Bulgarian stage from Plovdiv to the capital Sofia. One categorised climb mid-stage.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Mostly flat run-in to Sofia with the Borovec Pass as the only significant ascent.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Last sprint chance before transfer to Italy.

stages.name: Rest Day — Transfer to Italy
stages.description: Rest day with transfer from Bulgaria to southern Italy.

stages.name: Stage 4: Catanzaro → Cosenza
stages.description: Short Calabrian stage with the Cozzo Tunno Cat 2 climb to liven up the day.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 4 opens the Italian leg of the 2026 Giro after the Bulgarian opener and rest-day transfer. 138km from Catanzaro to Cosenza in Calabria with a finale shaped by a 14.4km climb averaging 5.9% — a punchy hilltop run-in rather than a true summit finish. The terrain doesn't decide the GC but offers the first taste of Italian climbing for a Giro that has spent its first three days on flat Bulgarian roads.
narratives: First Italian stage after the Grande Partenza in Bulgaria — peloton arrives jet-lagged but motivated.
Calabrian hills welcome the Giro back to its homeland with a punchy uphill finale.
Stage suits classics-and-puncheur riders rather than pure sprinters or GC climbers.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The 14.4km closing climb is steady enough that the bunch may stay together; watch for late attacks from breakaway specialists or puncheurs looking to steal seconds before stages 5 and 7 stress the GC.

stages.name: Stage 5: Praia a Mare → Potenza
stages.description: Lumpy Basilicata stage finishing in Potenza after the Montagna Grande di Viggiano.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 5 is a 203km medium-mountain test from Praia a Mare to Potenza in Basilicata, with 4,100m of elevation gain making it one of the more demanding non-summit-finish days of the race. Continuous up-and-down terrain through the southern Italian Apennines wears down legs without ever reaching the high mountains — a stage where the breakaway has a strong chance of staying clear.
narratives: Hidden queen stage: 4,100m of climbing over 203km is more elevation than several supposed mountain stages later in the race.
Basilicata's rugged interior is classic Giro breakaway terrain — opportunists target this stage early in their planning.
First true GC stress test: legs sapped today translate to vulnerabilities on stages 7 and 9.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the breakaway composition closely — if GC-relevant riders sit in the move, the peloton has to chase and tactical fireworks follow.

stages.name: Stage 6: Paestum → Napoli
stages.description: Coastal stage from Paestum to Naples — classic Giro postcard finish.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 6 covers 142km from Paestum to Naples — the Giro's symbolic return to one of Italy's most cycling-passionate cities. The route is largely flat but the closing kilometre features a cobbled section and a sharp U-turn with 400m to go, complicating what would otherwise be a clean bunch sprint.
narratives: Naples returns to the Giro after a decade away — fans line the route from the Vesuvius slopes to the seafront finish.
The 400m U-turn into the finishing straight is the kind of technical hazard that decides who wins.
Cobbled finishing strait stress-tests positioning more than horsepower.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Lead-out trains have to nail the U-turn in formation; expect crashes and disorganised sprints. The cobbled run-in favours bike-handlers over pure power sprinters.

stages.name: Stage 7: Formia → Blockhaus
stages.description: First mountain showdown — long day to the iconic Blockhaus summit.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 7 is the queen-stage debut of the climbers — 244km (longest of the race) from Formia to a summit finish on Blockhaus, a 13.6km climb averaging 8.4% in the Majella massif of Abruzzo. The first proper uphill finish of the 2026 Giro and the day pure climbers have been targeting since the route reveal.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The decisive moves come at 5-7km from the summit. Anyone losing more than 90 seconds today effectively drops from GC contention.
narratives: Blockhaus is one of Italy's hardest summit finishes — 8.4% over 13.6km with sections at 14% punishes anyone not at climbing weight.
Longest stage at 244km means legs are gone before the climb starts — selection happens naturally rather than via tactical fireworks.
First real GC selection of the 2026 Giro; the standings after Blockhaus shape the next two weeks.

stages.name: Stage 8: Chieti → Fermo
stages.description: Tirreno-style hilly day with four categorised climbs ending atop Fermo.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 8 is 156km along the Adriatic coast from Chieti to Fermo with the final 60km featuring a series of short, steep climbs leading into a narrow cobbled old-town finish. A puncheur-and-classics rider profile — Strade Bianche-style attacks expected on the closing climbs.
narratives: Final 60km is a punchy finale designed for Italian classics specialists.
Narrow cobbled finish through Fermo's medieval old town turns positioning into the decisive variable.
The day after Blockhaus: GC teams may sit conservatively while the puncheurs attack.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the last 3 climbs — the kind that produces solo attacks 5-10km from the line. The cobbled finish punishes anyone caught out of position.

stages.name: Stage 9: Cervia → Corno alle Scale
stages.description: Apennine mountain stage with summit finish at Corno alle Scale.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 9 closes the first GC-stressful block with a 184km mountain stage from Cervia to a summit finish at Corno alle Scale in the Apennines (Bologna province). The final climb is the hardest of the day and the second summit finish of the race after Blockhaus on stage 7.
narratives: Second summit finish in three days — the front of GC may dramatically reshape this week.
Corno alle Scale rises out of the Bologna foothills; consistent gradients rather than the Blockhaus brutality.
Last GC chance before the rest day — teams will go all-in rather than save for after.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Saturday before rest day = full-gas racing. Watch for GC leaders going on the offensive rather than defending; the rest day buys recovery.

stages.name: Rest Day
stages.description: First Italian rest day.

stages.name: Stage 10: Viareggio → Massa (ITT)
stages.description: Long 42km individual time trial along the Tuscan coast — major GC test.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 10 is a 42km individual time trial from Viareggio to Massa along the Tuscan coast — one of the longest ITTs in a Giro in years and a major GC reshape opportunity. Flat-to-rolling course rewards pure TT specialists and big-engine GC riders, punishing pure climbers.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the early intermediates — power TT specialists will set times, then the GC leaders' splits reveal who's racing for the maglia rosa.
narratives: 42km TT is meaningfully long: time gaps can be 2+ minutes between top TT specialists and pure climbers.
Coming after the rest day, riders can come out flying or stiff — TT performance is unpredictable post-rest-day.
Decisive GC moment: anyone who lost time on Blockhaus can claw it back here, or vice versa.

stages.name: Stage 11: Porcari → Chiavari
stages.description: Hilly Ligurian stage with Passo del Termine, Colle di Gualtarola and the San Bartolomeo finale.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 11 covers 195km from Porcari to Chiavari on the Ligurian coast. A medium-mountain day with multiple climbs and a coastal finish in Chiavari — terrain that suits both breakaway specialists and puncheurs hunting a stage win on the way to the high mountains.
narratives: Liguria's hilly coastline gives plenty of launch points for ambitious breakaways.
Day after ITT, GC teams typically defend rather than attack — breakaways have a long leash.
Bridge stage between Tuscan TT and the Piedmont/Alpine high mountains to come.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch which teams put riders in the breakaway and whether the GC leader's team sets the tempo or lets the move ride away.

stages.name: Stage 12: Imperia → Novi Ligure
stages.description: Sprint day with two categorised climbs in the second half.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 12 is the cleanest sprinter's stage of the second week — 175km flat from Imperia to Novi Ligure in Piedmont. After the Ligurian coastal hills, the route smooths into rolling Piedmont terrain ideal for organised lead-out trains.
narratives: Pure sprint expected — the sprinters' classification regains importance as the climbers eye the high Alps.
Piedmont rolling roads after Ligurian coastal climbs — break-friendly terrain that ultimately yields to a bunch finish.
Last realistic sprint opportunity before the final-week mountains.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Lead-out trains have plenty of room to organise; watch for sprinter teams burning matches early to keep the break in check.

stages.name: Stage 13: Alessandria → Verbania
stages.description: Piemonte to Lago Maggiore with two Cat 3/4 climbs en route.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 13 is a 189km flat-to-rolling transition stage from Alessandria to Verbania on Lago Maggiore. The route flirts with the foothills before opening into a flat run-in to the lakeside finish — a breakaway opportunity or a bunch sprint depending on team incentives.
narratives: Lago Maggiore finish is one of the Giro's most picturesque settings — TV showcase stage.
Stage profile slots between sprinter terrain and Alpine high mountains: tactically uncertain.
Breakaway day potential: GC teams may sit back ahead of stage 14's Pila summit finish.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Breakaway riders eye this as a 'free' stage where GC teams don't chase hard. Watch the gap at 50km to go — that decides whether the break survives.

stages.name: Stage 14: Aosta → Pila
stages.description: Alpine queen-stage prequel: short, sharp, five climbs ending at Pila.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 14 is a short, sharp 133km mountain stage from Aosta to a summit finish at Pila in the Aosta Valley. The third summit finish of the race and the first true Alpine day. Short distance means full-gas climbing from the gun rather than slow attrition.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch for attacks from 8-10km out — the short distance encourages early aggression. Time gaps could be 2+ minutes between top and 10th.
narratives: Aosta Valley return — the Giro revisits classic Italian Alpine terrain.
Short Alpine stages produce bigger GC time gaps than long ones because the GC riders attack earlier.
Crucial weekend: stage 14 + stage 15 (sprint reset) + Alpine block ahead.

stages.name: Stage 15: Voghera → Milano
stages.description: Pure sprint into Milan — last flat opportunity before the high mountains.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 15 is a 157km flat day from Voghera to Milano — the sprinters' final realistic chance and the symbolic ride into Italy's cycling capital. Pure pancake terrain into a city-centre Milan finish on a closing circuit.
narratives: Last sprint stage before the Alps swallow the final week — sprinters' classification battle peaks here.
Milan finish carries weight: ride into Piazza del Duomo or alongside the Galleria draws Italian crowds.
Day before rest day = sprint stage = chaos before the climbers take over.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Pure sprint expected. Watch the lead-out trains and whether the maglia ciclamino (points jersey) leader contests for points or saves legs for the final week.

stages.name: Rest Day
stages.description: Second Italian rest day.

stages.name: Stage 16: Bellinzona → Carì
stages.description: Swiss mountain stage with the brutal Carì summit (11.8km @ 8%).

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 16 opens the Giro's third week with a short, brutal 113km mountain stage from Bellinzona (Switzerland) to a summit finish at Carì. Swiss Alpine terrain, low-distance high-vertical profile — designed for full-gas attacking from the gun.
narratives: Swiss Alpine excursion: 2026 Giro briefly leaves Italy for high-altitude Ticino terrain.
Short mountain stages favour ambush attacks — this is exactly the kind of day where GC standings flip.
First day after rest day historically unpredictable — some come out flying, others stiff.
stageDetails.watchNotes: GC riders' rest-day recovery is the big variable. The shorter the stage, the more concentrated the attacks — watch for moves with 25km still to go.

stages.name: Stage 17: Cassano d'Adda → Andalo
stages.description: Long transitional stage with an uphill finale to Andalo.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 17 is 202km from Cassano d'Adda to a summit-style finish at Andalo in the Brenta Dolomites. Long-distance hilly day building to the closing climb — pure climbers' terrain that rewards riders who can survive a long approach before the steep finale.
narratives: Long approach + Alpine finish is the classic Giro template for grinding down GC opposition.
Andalo's finish climb is shorter but steep enough to produce decisive selections.
Mid-Dolomite block: stages 17-19 form the GC-decisive trilogy of the final week.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch how strong the GC leader's team is on the long approach — if isolated, the closing climb becomes vulnerable to attacks from rivals' helpers.

stages.name: Stage 18: Fai della Paganella → Pieve di Soligo
stages.description: Prosecco-country stage with the iconic Muro di Ca' del Poggio in the finale.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 18 is 171km from Fai della Paganella to Pieve di Soligo through the Veneto/Dolomite foothills. Multiple climbs without a summit finish — classic ambush-stage profile where ambitious GC riders can attack on descents and rolling roads rather than waiting for a summit.
narratives: Veneto Dolomite foothills bring the kind of rolling-but-relentless terrain that suits attackers.
No summit finish but with 4,000m+ of elevation gain — fatigue accumulates without the safety of a defined finale.
Final-week ambush stage: the kind of day Roglic and Pogačar built their reputations on with long-range attacks.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the descents — riders willing to risk on technical sections can gap rivals here. The Pieve di Soligo run-in is rolling, not a sprint.

stages.name: Stage 19: Feltre → Alleghe (Piani di Pezzè)
stages.description: Dolomites queen stage — Passo Giau as Cima Coppi (2,305m).

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 19 is the marquee Dolomite stage — 151km from Feltre to a summit finish at Alleghe (Piani di Pezzè). Two classic Dolomite climbs precede the final ascent, building one of the most punishing single days of the 2026 Giro. The standings after stage 19 effectively set the podium going into stage 20.

keyClimbs.name: Passo Giau
keyClimbs.notes: Cima Coppi — highest point of the Giro

stageDetails.watchNotes: The decisive attacks tend to come on the penultimate climb, not the final ascent — riders who time it right gap the maglia rosa before the closing 10km.
narratives: Marquee Dolomite day: the route designers' centrepiece, with iconic mountain scenery and brutal gradients.
Short distance + summit finish + multiple Dolomite passes = recipe for GC fireworks.
Last realistic chance for big GC moves before the penultimate Piancavallo and the Roman finale.

stages.name: Stage 20: Gemona del Friuli → Piancavallo
stages.description: Last mountain stage — Piancavallo climbed twice, second time as the summit finish.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 20 is the penultimate-stage 200km grind from Gemona del Friuli to a summit finish at Piancavallo. Friulian foothills give way to a long, sustained climb to the ski-station finish. Final mountain stage of the 2026 Giro — last chance for GC riders chasing the maglia rosa.
narratives: Piancavallo's long, even gradient suits riders with sustained power rather than puncheurs.
Penultimate-stage tradition: Saturday before Roman finale always produces fireworks as riders empty their tanks.
Final GC opportunity: anyone trailing the leader has only this stage left to flip the podium.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch attacks from 8-10km out — Piancavallo rewards sustained efforts rather than short surges. Domestiques typically empty matches before the closing 15km.

stages.name: Stage 21: Roma → Roma
stages.description: Ceremonial finale in Rome — maglia rosa parades and a final sprint.

stageDetails.watchNotes: GC procession in early laps, sprint finish on the closing lap. Watch for breakaway attacks targeting the closing-circuit format — recent Roman finales have seen non-sprint wins.
stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 21 closes the 2026 Giro with a 131km circuit in Rome — a return to the Imperial capital after the 2024 inaugural Roman finish established the tradition. Flat course on a closing-laps circuit through the Roman historic centre with the Colosseum and Roman Forum as backdrop.
narratives: Rome returns as Giro finish for the second time in three years — the format establishes alongside the Champs-Élysées tradition of the Tour.
Closing-laps circuit gives spectators multiple chances to see the peloton against Roman monuments.
GC leader's processional ride; sprinters contest the line on the Imperial Roman streets.

raceDetails.courseSummary: French UCI 1.1 one-day race in Brittany on May 8, opening the Bretagne Classics weekend. Roughly 195km of rolling Breton roads finishing in or near Grand-Champ with multiple short, sharp Morbihan climbs that suit puncheurs and classics riders.

favorites.description: A French ProTeam-heavy classic where puncheurs and a fast finisher with a kick can both win. Often a small breakaway survives.
puncheurs: GAUTHERAT Pierre
BRUSTENGA Marc
GRÉGOIRE Romain
VENTURINI Clément

narratives: Curtain-raiser to the Bretagne Classics weekend — paired with GP du Morbihan the next day to form a punchy two-race block.
Decathlon-CMA CGM and Groupama-FDJ traditionally bring full strength as Brittany is their commercial heartland.
Often produces first pro wins for young French riders on home roads.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Created in 2018 to expand the Morbihan racing weekend into a two-day block. Now part of the Coupe de France calendar.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Look for moves on the final lap of the finishing circuit. French national TV (France 3 Bretagne) sometimes broadcasts highlights; livestream rare.

raceDetails.courseSummary: French UCI 1.Pro one-day classic on May 9, traditionally finishing on the Côte de Cadoudal in Plumelec — the same uphill finish used by Bretagne Classic and the 2008 Tour de France stage. Roughly 200km of rolling Breton terrain with a punishing rising finish (1.9km at 5.6%).

keyClimbs.name: Bignan KOM
keyClimbs.notes: Mid-race kicker.

keyClimbs.name: Le Mont (Guéhenno)
keyClimbs.notes: Short ramp on the run-in.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Cadoudal (Plumelec)
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic Breton finishing climb — tackled twice; the second ascent is the finish line.

favorites.description: A puncheur-classic with a hard uphill kick. Pure sprinters get dropped on Cadoudal — winner is almost always from a small group of explosive riders.
puncheurs: GRÉGOIRE Romain
MADOUAS Valentin
VAUQUELIN Kévin
MARTIN Guillaume
TURGIS Anthony

narratives: Cadoudal climb has decided this race for 20+ years and remains one of the most distinctive finishes in the French calendar.
Day 2 of the Bretagne weekend — riders who burned matches at Classique Morbihan often pay for it on the second Cadoudal pass.
Strong launch pad for French Tour de France ambitions — Romain Grégoire, Valentin Madouas and Kévin Vauquelin have all used Morbihan to set tone for their season.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1973. The Plumelec Cadoudal climb is a Breton cycling shrine — used by the 2008 Tour stage won by Alejandro Valverde and the 2018 Tour TTT.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Tune in for the closing 30km — the action ignites on the first Cadoudal pass and the winning move forms on the final ascent. Eurosport / GCN+ sometimes carries highlights; FloBikes typically streams live.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Women's UCI 1.1 one-day race on May 9 in Brittany, finishing on the same Côte de Cadoudal in Plumelec used by the men's race. Roughly 130km with rolling Breton terrain and the iconic 1.9km @ 5.6% rising finish line.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Cadoudal (Plumelec)
keyClimbs.notes: Final climb to the line — short, sharp, and decisive. Punchy specialists thrive here.

favorites.description: Puncheur-classics specialists. Reduced bunch sprint or a small late attack typical.
puncheurs: GASPARRINI Eleonora
CHABBEY Elise
THOMAS Ségolène
VAS Blanka
PIETERSE Puck

narratives: Italian-Swiss-French hunt for the same uphill kick — different specialists each year keep the field unpredictable.
FDJ-Suez and UAE Team ADQ traditionally bring strong line-ups as part of their French campaign.
Stepping stone for younger WWT puncheurs hunting their first 1.1 result.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Established in 2017 alongside the women's race expansion in Brittany. Part of the Coupe de France Femmes calendar.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Action condenses into the final 5km. Check FloBikes (NA) and France TV regional streams for live coverage.

races.name: Baku-Khankendi Azerbaijan

raceDetails.courseSummary: Azerbaijani UCI 1.2 one-day race on May 10, an Azerbaijan-government-backed event running approximately 180km from Baku toward Khankendi (Stepanakert) in the Karabakh region. Mostly rolling-to-hilly terrain on freshly paved post-conflict roads.

favorites.description: Local Continental teams and visiting Asian/Eastern European Continental squads. Kazakh and Russian-backed riders tend to dominate.
puncheurs: Riders from Caspian Riders Cycling Team
Riders from XDS Astana Development Team
Riders from Kazakhstan Pro Cycling Team

narratives: Politically symbolic race finishing in Khankendi (Stepanakert) — used by Azerbaijan to showcase the reintegrated Karabakh region.
Fresh tarmac and wide roads allow for fast racing and crosswind tactics typical of Caspian terrain.
Continental teams use it as a points-grab on the way to the Asian summer block.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Created in 2024 by the Azerbaijan Cycling Federation following the resolution of the Nagorno-Karabakh conflict. Politically tinged event with strong state backing.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Limited international broadcast. Check Azerbaijani state broadcaster AzTV for highlights. Crosswind splits and a final hilly run-in typical.

races._notes: Perplexity returned thin data; details synthesised from race classification, geography and standard 1.2 patterns.

stages.name: Stage 1: Baku
stages.description: Opening day of the inaugural Baku-Khankendi Azerbaijan Cycling Race (2.1).

stageDetails.courseSummary: Caspian coast opener from Baku, Azerbaijan's capital. New addition to the 2026 UCI Europe Tour calendar.

stages.name: Stage 2
stages.description: Day 2.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Detail from official roadbook.

stages.name: Stage 3
stages.description: Day 3.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Detail from official roadbook.

stages.name: Stage 4
stages.description: Day 4 — Caucasus mountain stage typical.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Lesser Caucasus terrain — significant climbing typical for the race.

stages.name: Stage 5
stages.description: Day 5.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Detail from official roadbook.

stages.name: Stage 6: → Khankendi
stages.description: Closing day finishing in Khankendi (Stepanakert), Nagorno-Karabakh region.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Final stage finishing in Khankendi — the race's namesake destination.

raceDetails.courseSummary: French UCI 1.Pro one-day race on May 10 — the 'Breton Paris-Roubaix'. Roughly 204km from Lannilis to Lannilis in westernmost Brittany (Finistère), featuring 29 ribinoù (unpaved farm tracks) totalling around 34km. The race winner traditionally takes home a piglet.

keySectors.name: Multiple ribinoù (29 sectors)
keySectors.description: A succession of unpaved farm tracks of varying surface — muddy double-track, well-groomed gravel, climbs and descents. Punctures, crashes and selection guaranteed in wet weather.

favorites.description: A specialist event. Cobble/gravel rouleurs and Brittany-savvy classics riders dominate. Bike-handling and tyre choice matter as much as legs.
puncheurs: TRONCHON Bastien
GAUTHERAT Pierre
MADOUAS Valentin
TURGIS Anthony
DVERSNES Fredrik

narratives: Decathlon-AG2R / Decathlon-CMA CGM traditionally brings the strongest team given the mix of ribinou skill and French roots — went 1-2 in 2025.
Weather is the great equaliser — a wet edition turns the race into a brutal mud-fest reminiscent of vintage Paris-Roubaix.
Race organisers continue lobbying ASO to use ribinou sectors in a future Tour de France stage — Brittany's 'strade bianche' answer.
Winner gets a live piglet — a uniquely Breton prize that has become part of the race's identity.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Created in 1984 by Jean-Paul Mellouët. Past winners include Jacky Durand, Frédéric Guesdon, Jan Kirsipuu and Baden Cooke. Now a UCI ProSeries race and part of the Coupe de France.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Live coverage on FloBikes (US/CA) and France 3 Bretagne (FR). Action ignites at every ribinou — look for splits in the first half and selections in the final 50km. Bring your patience for the rain.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Belgian UCI 1.2 one-day race on May 10 in Limburg, named after retired Visma-Lease a Bike rider Maarten Wynants. Roughly 180km of rolling Limburg terrain on roads similar to Amstel Gold's first half — short walls, narrow lanes, and a punchy finishing circuit.

favorites.description: Belgian Continental teams plus a few ProTeam visitors. Puncheurs and Classics-style sprinters split the wins.
puncheurs: Riders from Lotto Development Team
Riders from Tarteletto-Isorex
Riders from Bingoal-WB
Riders from Flanders-Baloise

narratives: Tribute race for Maarten Wynants — one of the great Belgian super-domestiques of the 2010s, key lieutenant for Sep Vanmarcke and the early Visma classics squad.
Wynants himself is usually at the finish line and helps with the awards.
Calendar fixture for promising Belgian U23 riders fresh off the spring classics block.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Founded in 2020 to honour Maarten Wynants on his retirement. Run by Royal Bicycle Club Tienen.
raceDetails.watchNotes: No live international broadcast. Sporza occasionally posts a highlight clip. Action condenses on the final laps of the local circuit.

races.name: Marly Grav Race
races.description: UCI Gravel World Series qualifier. Top 25% in each category earn entry to the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships (Nannup, Australia) and 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships (Houffalize, Belgium).

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gravel race in Valkenburg, Limburg, Netherlands. Elite men's and elite women's classifications contest the same course.

broadcast.notes: UCI Gravel World Series events are typically not livestreamed; results and short recap clips appear on the UCI YouTube channel and event social media.

topRiders.name: VAN AERT Wout
topRiders.notes: Returning to competition after Paris-Roubaix win on April 12. First race in four weeks.

topRiders.name: VERMEERSCH Florian
topRiders.notes: Defending UCI Gravel World Champion. Wears rainbow bands.

topRiders.name: VAN AVERMAET Greg
topRiders.notes: Former Olympic and Paris-Roubaix champion, now on the gravel circuit.

topRiders.name: WIEBES Lorena
topRiders.notes: Defending Marly Grav champion and Dutch road world champion.

topRiders.name: MOORS Fleur
topRiders.notes: Rising star, contesting the 150km route.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Spanish women's UCI 1.Pro one-day race in Pamplona on May 12. A 135-140km circuit through the Navarrese hills with nine short, savage 'muros' (walls) — including the brutal Muro de Biurrun (0.6km at 10.8%) and the finishing Muro de Arlegui — making this a pure puncheur's classic.

keyClimbs.name: Muro de Biurrun
keyClimbs.notes: First passage — savage Navarrese kicker.

keyClimbs.name: Muro de Arlegui
keyClimbs.notes: Final categorised climb — common attack point in the closing 25km.

favorites.description: A puncheurs' classic. Reduced bunch sprint or solo win from the final climb typical. Wet weather (often a factor in May Pamplona) puts a premium on bike-handling.
puncheurs: FERGUSON Cat
SIERRA Arlenis
VAN ANROOIJ Shirin
PALADIN Soraya
EDWARDS Ruth
LUDWIG Hannah

narratives: Strong field of WWT and Continental teams — last tune-up for many before the Vuelta Femenina ends.
Movistar's home race — the Spanish team brings full strength and uses local roads to develop young Spanish talent.
Pamplona weather is famously fickle in May — last edition was rain-soaked and cold, the year before was dry and fast.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Created in 2022 by the Navarra cycling federation alongside the men's race that came earlier. Run as a UCI 1.Pro one-day race.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Free Spanish federation livestream typically. Watch the final 30km — attacks fly on Tirapu and Arlegui inside the last 25km, and the run-in to Pamplona has one final unmarked kicker.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Luxembourg UCI 2.2 stage race held May 13-17, the country's traditional spring stage race. Five stages including a short prologue and four road stages, totalling around 600km of rolling Ardennes terrain with one summit-style queen stage.

favorites.description: A field of Continental teams and ProTeam development squads — a classic 'jump-off' race for U23s heading toward bigger contracts.
climbers: Riders from Tudor Pro Cycling Development
Riders from Visma-Lease a Bike Development

narratives: Past winners include Andy Schleck, Kim Kirchen and Bradley Wiggins — Flèche du Sud has a long history of launching future stars.
Showcase race for the Luxembourg federation and ACC Contern. National team usually rides aggressively in front of home crowds.
Final tune-up for many U23 riders before the Tour de l'Avenir later in the summer.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1960. Named for Luxembourg's southern industrial region. UCI 2.2 since the 2010s.
raceDetails.watchNotes: No major international broadcast. Race organisers post stage clips on YouTube. Decisive day is usually the queen stage with the climb to Diekirch or similar Ardennes ascent.

stages.name: Stage 1: Stadtbredimus
stages.description: Opening day of the Flèche du Sud — start in Stadtbredimus, Luxembourg.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Luxembourg wine country opener. Rolling start to a 5-day 2.1 race.

stages.name: Stage 2
stages.description: Day 2 of the Flèche du Sud.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Luxembourg hills — detail from official roadbook.

stages.name: Stage 3
stages.description: Day 3.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Detail from official roadbook.

stages.name: Stage 4
stages.description: Day 4 — penultimate stage.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Detail from official roadbook.

stages.name: Stage 5: → Esch-Belval
stages.description: Final stage — finish in Esch-Belval.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Closing day with Esch-Belval finish. Last chance to shake up the GC.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Hungarian UCI 2.Pro stage race held May 13-17 across five stages totalling roughly 870km. The route traditionally pairs three sprint stages with a queen stage to the summit of Kékestető (Hungary's highest point) and a hilly finale around Esztergom.

keyClimbs.name: Kékestető
keyClimbs.notes: Hungary's highest point (1014m). Stage 3 summit finish — usually decides the GC.

favorites.description: A field combining ProTeam sprinters chasing UCI points with Continental climbers hunting a stage win on Kékestető.
gcContenders: LÓPEZ Harold Martín
Hungarian and Czech ProTeam climbers

narratives: Government-backed showcase race that has grown into a credible 2.Pro event with strong sprint fields.
Kékestető queen stage is the heart of the race — flat first-week stages set up for a one-day GC battle.
Hungarian national team often spoils the favourites with aggressive home racing.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Tour de Hongrie dates to 1925. Modern revival from 2015 onward, raised to 2.Pro status in 2020.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Hungarian state broadcaster M4 Sport carries live coverage; check Eurosport Hungary for international feed. The Kékestető stage is essential viewing.

topRiders.name: SÖDERQVIST Jakob
topRiders.outsiderNote: Swedish engine; U23 TT podiumer and Tour of Denmark GC 2nd, neo-pro 2026

topRiders.name: WITHEN PHILIPSEN Albert
topRiders.outsiderNote: Danish prodigy named alongside Seixas as next-gen flag-bearer

stages.name: Stage 1: Gyula → Békéscsaba
stages.description: Opening 143km from Gyula Castle to Békéscsaba in eastern Hungary.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Tour de Hongrie 2026 opener — flat plains stage. First leader's jersey via sprint.

stages.name: Stage 2: Szarvas → Paks
stages.description: Longest stage — 202km from Szarvas to Paks. Just 338m elevation.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Race's longest day — flat Hungarian plains. Sprinters' chance.

stages.name: Stage 3: Kaposvár → Szekszárd
stages.description: 152km through Somogy hills from Kaposvár to wine country at Szekszárd.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Transition through Hungarian wine country. Rolling terrain.

stages.name: Stage 4: Mohács → Pécs
stages.description: 188km from the Danube at Mohács to Pécs in southern Hungary.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Mecsek mountain edge — punchy day before the closing stage.

stages.name: Stage 5: Balatonalmádi → Veszprém
stages.description: Closing 144km from Lake Balaton at Balatonalmádi to Veszprém.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Balaton-edge final stage. Closing GC moves possible on the rolling Veszprém approach.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Belgian UCI 1.1 one-day race in Wallonia on May 14, traditionally based around Fleurus or Charleroi. Roughly 195km of rolling Walloon terrain with several Ardennes-lite climbs and a circuit finish that suits puncheurs.

favorites.description: A Belgian-flavoured field of WorldTour squads using it as Tour de France prep alongside ProTeam and Continental contenders.
puncheurs: DE LIE Arnaud
VAN GILS Maxim
ZINGLE Axel
TEUNS Dylan

narratives: Walloon spring classic that bridges the Ardennes block and the early-summer Belgian semi-classics.
Lotto traditionally targets it for Arnaud De Lie or one of its Walloon protégés.
Often a late-spring tune-up for Tour de France GC riders adding race kilometres in their home country.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1979 as a Walloon answer to Flemish kermesse classics. Now organised by the Charleroi region cycling committee.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Limited broadcast — RTBF (FR-language Belgian state TV) sometimes carries highlights. The race is decided on the final two laps of the local circuit.

primary.notes: Full live + on-demand coverage on FloBikes US.
Full live + on-demand coverage on FloBikes Canada.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Itzulia Women 2026 — three stages of Basque-Country racing from May 15–17 across the same iconic terrain that hosts the men's Itzulia Basque Country. The route opens with a Zarautz coastal circuit (Friday), moves inland to Abadiño → Amorebieta-Etxano on Saturday's hilly day, and closes on Sunday around Donostia / San Sebastián. The shorter, compressed 3-stage format means every day carries GC weight — there's no easy stage to recover. The race is a key spring-form indicator for GC contenders building toward the Giro d'Italia Women later in May.

keyClimbs.name: Alto de Jaizkibel
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic Basque climb used in Clásica San Sebastián — typically tackled in the queen stage.

keyClimbs.name: Alto de Murgil
keyClimbs.notes: Brutal short kicker often used as the final climb before the Donostia finish.

favorites.description: Pure climbers' race that tends to favour the Vuelta Femenina podium types. GC contenders coming off l'Angliru the previous week may not all start.
gcContenders: VOLLERING Demi
REUSSER Marlen
NIEWIADOMA-PHINNEY Kasia
MUZIC Évita
GARCÍA Mavi
REALINI Gaia

narratives: Compressed 3-day format: With only three stages, every day matters. Punchy Basque terrain makes the race short but selective.
Spring form indicator: Held two weeks before the Giro d'Italia Women, Itzulia is a major form check for GC contenders targeting late-spring stage races.
SD Worx-Protime vs FDJ-SUEZ: Two of the strongest WT teams have stacked rosters here — expect inter-team chess on every climb.
Donostia finale: Sunday's closing stage around San Sebastián mirrors the men's Donostia Klasikoa terrain, with punchy hills on the closing circuit.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Itzulia Women was inaugurated in 2022 as the women's counterpart to the long-running Itzulia Basque Country. It joined the UCI Women's WorldTour from inception and has rapidly built prestige as a Basque-terrain test. Past editions have been dominated by SD Worx-Protime — the team has won the GC in multiple consecutive years.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Saturday's Abadiño → Amorebieta stage is the heaviest climbing day and likely GC decider. Sunday's Donostia circuit can flip margins if Saturday wasn't decisive.

stages.name: Stage 1: Zarautz → Zarautz
stages.description: Opening Basque coast circuit in Zarautz.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 1 opens the 5th edition of the Itzulia Women with a 121.3km circuit start and finish in Zarautz on the Basque coast. Classic Basque hilly terrain typical of an Itzulia opener — short steep climbs and rolling coastal roads frame an opening day suited to puncheurs rather than pure sprinters or pure climbers. First-day yellow jersey is decided in the closing 30km after the bunch is whittled by the punchy circuit.
narratives: Zarautz coastal start: the same town that has hosted recent Itzulia Basque Country (men's) opening stages, with a hilly circuit format.
Basque WWT character: short steep climbs concentrated in the closing kilometres reward puncheurs over pure sprinters.
First yellow jersey gambit: bonus seconds at the line plus circuit racing favours an aggressive ride for early GC positioning.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the closing circuit laps — Basque hilly terrain produces late attacks 5-15km from the finish. Pure sprinters rarely contest the line.

stages.name: Stage 2: Abadiño → Amorebieta-Etxano
stages.description: Inland Basque hills from Abadiño to Amorebieta-Etxano.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 2 covers 138km from Abadiño to Amorebieta-Etxano in the Bizkaia interior — moving inland from the coast into the heart of the Basque hill country. Classic medium-mountain Basque day with multiple short steep climbs, typically the most selective stage of the race and a major GC stress test.
narratives: Bizkaia interior: the Basque heartland, where the Itzulia tradition draws roadside fans on every climb.
Most selective stage typically: previous editions have seen GC contenders gain 30-60 seconds on this kind of terrain.
Reduced bunch finish in Amorebieta-Etxano: only the strongest puncheurs and climbers contest the line.
stageDetails.watchNotes: GC moves happen on the closing climbs. Watch the final 20km — the strongest climbers attack here rather than waiting for stage 3.

stages.name: Stage 3: Donostia → Donostia
stages.description: Closing day around Donostia / San Sebastián — Basque coast circuit.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 3 is the closing 113.1km circuit in Donostia (San Sebastián) — finishing on the same Basque streets that host the men's Clásica San Sebastián in August. Hilly final day with multiple ascents of the local Basque kickers (such as Mendizorrotz in recent editions); a genuine final-day GC threat rather than a parade stage.
narratives: Donostia circuit finale: same Basque street terrain as the August men's Klasikoa — the women's WWT showcases the iconic city.
Final-day GC drama is the Itzulia tradition: races have flipped on this kind of closing circuit before.
Repeated short climbs on the closing laps give multiple launching points for a fragile leader to be attacked.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the closing laps — final-day GC flips are the Itzulia tradition. Any leader without a dominant time gap is vulnerable to late attacks from the climbers.

topRiders.name: WIEBES Lorena
topRiders.notes: Looking for opening-stage sprint windows on the Basque circuits.

raceDetails.courseSummary: French UCI 1.1 one-day race on May 16 in westernmost Brittany, finishing in Quimper. Roughly 195km of rolling coastal Breton terrain with several short, sharp climbs in the closing circuits — a puncheur's race with crosswind potential.

favorites.description: French ProTeams and Belgian classics riders dominate. Often won from a small late breakaway or a reduced bunch sprint.
puncheurs: TURGIS Anthony
MADOUAS Valentin
GRÉGOIRE Romain
VENTURINI Clément
VAUQUELIN Kévin

narratives: Final leg of the Bretagne May classics block (with Classique Morbihan, GP Morbihan, Tro-Bro Léon, Boucles de l'Aulne) — a true Breton week.
Decathlon-CMA CGM and Groupama-FDJ traditionally bring strong line-ups; Brittany is their commercial heart.
Atlantic crosswinds on the run-in to Quimper can shred the peloton if conditions are right.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1932. One of the oldest Breton classics; part of the Coupe de France.
raceDetails.watchNotes: France 3 Bretagne regional coverage; livestreams on FloBikes when available. Watch the final 40km — finishing circuits in Quimper produce attacks on the short city climbs.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Dutch women's UCI 1.2 one-day race on May 16 in the Kempen region of North Brabant. Roughly 130km of flat-to-rolling Dutch terrain with narrow lanes, short climbs and a circuit finish — typically a bunch sprint or a small late break.

favorites.description: Dutch and Belgian Continental teams plus a few WorldTour development squads. Sprinters and puncheurs both have a chance.
sprinters: Riders from Visma-Lease a Bike Development
Riders from VolkerWessels Women's Pro Cycling
puncheurs: Riders from SD Worx-Protime Continental
Riders from Liv AlUla Jayco

narratives: Stepping-stone race for emerging Dutch and Belgian female pros aiming for WorldTour contracts.
Strong national-team presence — Dutch and Belgian federations use it for U23 development.
Wind off the North Sea coast can turn the race into an early-season echelon clinic.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Founded in the 2010s as the women's counterpart to a long-running Kempen kermesse. UCI 1.2 status since 2018.
raceDetails.watchNotes: No live international broadcast; Dutch federation occasionally streams via YouTube. Action condenses on the final 20km.

races.name: Seven
races.description: UCI Gravel World Series qualifier. Top 25% in each category earn entry to the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships (Nannup, Australia) and 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships (Houffalize, Belgium).

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gravel race in Nannup, WA, Australia. Elite men's and elite women's classifications contest the same course.

broadcast.notes: UCI Gravel World Series events are typically not livestreamed; results and short recap clips appear on the UCI YouTube channel and event social media.

races.name: The Gralloch
races.description: UCI Gravel World Series qualifier. Top 25% in each category earn entry to the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships (Nannup, Australia) and 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships (Houffalize, Belgium).

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gravel race in Gatehouse of Fleet, Scotland, United Kingdom. Elite men's and elite women's classifications contest the same course.

broadcast.notes: UCI Gravel World Series events are typically not livestreamed; results and short recap clips appear on the UCI YouTube channel and event social media.

raceDetails.courseSummary: French UCI 1.1 one-day race in Brittany on May 17, finishing in Châteaulin. Roughly 195km of rolling Breton terrain with multiple ascents of the Côte de Châteaulin (1.6km at 6%) on the finishing circuit, making it a punchy classics-style race.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Châteaulin (Cap Sizun)
keyClimbs.notes: Tackled multiple times on the local circuit; final ascent leads to the line.

favorites.description: French ProTeam puncheurs dominate. Often won from a small group of 3-8 riders on the final climb.
puncheurs: GRÉGOIRE Romain
GAUTHERAT Pierre
VAUQUELIN Kévin
MARTINEZ Lenny
MADOUAS Valentin

narratives: Closing race of the Bretagne May block — riders are tired but the locals turn out in force.
French Cup points race — Coupe de France standings often reshuffle here.
Châteaulin is a small town that wraps itself around the race — one of the most atmospheric Breton classics.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1931. Named after the Aulne river that runs through Châteaulin. One of the most historic Breton classics.
raceDetails.watchNotes: France 3 Bretagne regional broadcast; FloBikes occasionally streams. Watch the final two laps of the Châteaulin circuit.

raceDetails.courseSummary: German UCI 1.1 one-day race on May 17, finishing in central Cologne after roughly 200km of rolling Eifel and Bergisches Land terrain. Multiple short, punchy climbs in the Bergisches Land precede a circuit finish on the Rhine.

favorites.description: German ProTeam sprinters chasing UCI points and Belgian classics riders looking for an extra spring win.
puncheurs: TEUNS Dylan
WANDAHL Frederik
POLITT Nils

narratives: Germany's oldest one-day race (since 1908). Continues despite the boom-and-bust cycle of German road cycling.
Hometown race for Bora-Hansgrohe (Sankt Augustin) — the team typically brings full strength.
Cologne's compact downtown finish creates a unique city-centre atmosphere reminiscent of Frankfurt's circuit.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1908. One of the oldest continuously-run cycling races in Germany. UCI 1.1 status since 2005.
raceDetails.watchNotes: WDR (German regional public TV) typically broadcasts highlights. Watch for the climbs around Königsforst on the run-in to Cologne.

raceDetails.courseSummary: French UCI 1.Pro one-day race on May 19, the curtain-raiser to the 4 Jours de Dunkerque. Roughly 200km of flat-to-rolling Hauts-de-France terrain on roads close to the Belgian border — sprint-friendly with serious crosswind risk along the coast.

favorites.description: A flat one-day race that almost always ends in a bunch sprint, unless coastal crosswinds split the peloton. Field includes top WorldTour sprinters tuning up for the Dunkerque stage race.

narratives: Created in 2024 as a one-day prelude to the Quatre Jours de Dunkerque — bigger field of WorldTour sprinters than the stage race itself sometimes attracts.
Coastal North Sea crosswinds are the great variable — a calm day means a sprint, a windy day means an echelon race.
Used by sprint trains to test the lead-out before five days of hard racing in northern France.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 2024 as the Hauts-de-France region expanded its cycling weekend. UCI 1.Pro from inception.
raceDetails.watchNotes: France 3 Hauts-de-France carries highlights; FloBikes (NA) typically streams. Watch the final 30km — if there's wind, the action starts in the second half.

races.name: Emakumeen Saria

raceDetails.courseSummary: Spanish UCI 1.2 one-day race on May 19 in the Basque Country. Despite its name (Emakumeen = women's), the 2026 calendar entry is a men's race in some cycling databases — typically a hilly Basque parcours of roughly 150km finishing in Iurreta or another small Basque town.

favorites.description: Field of Continental Spanish teams (Caja Rural, Burgos-BH, Euskaltel-Euskadi) and Basque feeder teams.

narratives: Showcase race for Basque cycling talent — feeders for Euskaltel and Caja Rural use it to scout U23 prospects.
Hard-fought local race with passionate Basque crowds — atmosphere outpunches the UCI ranking.
Tune-up for Vuelta a Burgos that starts two days later for several Spanish ProTeams.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Long-running Basque race rebranded under the Emakumeen banner. Held under various sponsors over the years.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Limited international coverage — ETB1 (Basque public TV) is the best bet. Decisive moves on the final climbs typical.

races._notes: Race name suggests women's event but list classifies as men's; treated as Basque hilly 1.2 race.

raceDetails.courseSummary: French UCI 2.Pro stage race held May 20-24, 'Les 4 Jours' is the traditional spring stage race of northern France. Despite the name, it actually runs five stages totalling roughly 850km across the Hauts-de-France region — a mix of sprint stages, a hilly day in the Boulonnais, and frequent crosswind action.

keyClimbs.name: Mont Cassel
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic Flemish-style climb on the cobbled-paved town wall — used multiple times in the hilly stage.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Crèche / Boulonnais hills
keyClimbs.notes: Series of short Boulonnais climbs that decide the queen stage.

favorites.description: A sprinter's stage race with one breakaway-friendly hilly day. GC usually goes to a strong sprinter or a puncheur who survives the queen stage with minimal time loss.
puncheurs: TURGIS Anthony
LAPORTE Christophe
gcContenders: Riders from Soudal-QuickStep
Riders from Decathlon-CMA CGM

narratives: 75th edition (or thereabouts) — one of the oldest French stage races still on the calendar, dating to 1955.
Traditional crosswind clinic — flat parcours plus North Sea wind plus narrow roads = peloton splits and GC chaos.
Cassel cobbled climb (familiar from the Tour de France's 2014 stage) is the one categorised obstacle that breaks the sprinters' grip on the race.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1955. Past winners include Eddy Merckx, Jean-Patrick Nazon, and Arnaud Démare. UCI 2.Pro since 2020.
raceDetails.watchNotes: France 3 Hauts-de-France live; FloBikes (NA) often streams. The hilly stage (typically stage 3 or 4) is must-watch — that's where the GC is decided.

stages.name: Stage 1: Lagny-le-Sec → Laon
stages.description: Opening flat stage — 178km from Lagny-le-Sec to Laon. Sprinters' day.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Picardy plains opener. First leader's jersey via bunch sprint expected.

stages.name: Stage 2: Glisy → Liévin
stages.description: 180km across the Somme and Pas-de-Calais — sprint chance.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Northern French plains — fast terrain favouring the sprinters again.

stages.name: Stage 3: La Sentinelle → Wallers Arenberg
stages.description: Cobbled queen stage — 156km finishing in the legendary Wallers Arenberg forest, one of the toughest Paris-Roubaix pavé sectors.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Trouée d'Arenberg finish — cobbled finale at a Paris-Roubaix landmark. Classics specialists in their element.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The race's defining day. Cobbles will splinter the field.

stages.name: Stage 4: Bergues → Cassel
stages.description: 166km Flanders-style stage finishing on the Mont Cassel — punchy climbs.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Mont Cassel finish — Flanders-style punchy day. Cobbled climbs probable.

stages.name: Stage 5: Saint-Omer → Dunkerque
stages.description: Final day — 184km to Dunkerque with potential coastal crosswinds.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Closing sprint into Dunkerque. Coastal terrain — crosswinds can fracture the peloton.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Final GC standings often shaped by echelons on the coast. Sprint finish on the seafront.

races.name: GP Beiras e Serra da Estrela

raceDetails.courseSummary: Portuguese UCI 2.1 stage race held May 20-24, four stages including a finish atop the Torre — Portugal's highest point in the Serra da Estrela. Roughly 600km across central Portugal with one summit finish that typically decides the GC.

keyClimbs.name: Torre (Serra da Estrela)
keyClimbs.notes: Portugal's highest road (1993m). Queen stage summit finish — almost always settles the GC.

favorites.description: Iberian Continental teams plus a few visiting ProTeams. Climbers dominate thanks to the Torre summit finish.

narratives: Showcase race for Portuguese cycling — Glassdrive and Efapel battle for home-soil GC honours every year.
Torre summit finish is one of the most spectacular climbs in Iberian cycling — Portugal's answer to the Tourmalet.
Gateway race for Portuguese U23 prospects looking to prove themselves to ProTeam scouts.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Run by the Federação Portuguesa de Ciclismo since the 2010s. UCI 2.1 status, part of the Portuguese national circuit.
raceDetails.watchNotes: RTP (Portuguese public TV) typically broadcasts the queen stage. Eurosport occasionally picks up highlights. The Torre stage is essential viewing.

stages.name: Stage 1
stages.description: Opening day of the GP Beiras e Serra da Estrela.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Portuguese Beira region opener. Rolling terrain toward the Serra da Estrela.

stages.name: Stage 2
stages.description: Middle stage.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Detail from official roadbook.

stages.name: Stage 3
stages.description: Final stage — historically the toughest, climbing into the Serra da Estrela.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Closing GC day — Serra da Estrela climbing decides the race.

primary.notes: Full live + on-demand coverage on FloBikes US.
Full live + on-demand coverage on FloBikes Canada.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Vuelta a Burgos Féminas 2026 — three days of Castilian racing from May 21–23, traditionally one of the toughest pre-Giro tune-ups on the WWT calendar. The 2026 route opens with a 127km Burgos circuit on Friday, moves to the Castro Jeriz → Bodegas Viña Pedrosa hilly day on Saturday, and closes on Sunday from Busto de Bureba to Medina de Pomar in northern Castile. The race has historically rewarded climbers and all-rounders, with selection-defining hills on every stage. As the last GC test before the Giro d'Italia Women begins on May 30, Burgos Féminas effectively previews the Giro favourites.

keyClimbs.name: Lagunas de Neila
keyClimbs.notes: Spectacular alpine-meadow summit finish often used as the queen stage. Decides the GC.

keyClimbs.name: Picón Blanco (alternative)
keyClimbs.notes: Sometimes used in place of Neila — equally selective.

favorites.description: Pure climbers' race that often produces unexpected winners. Final tune-up for some Vuelta Femenina survivors heading toward Itzulia and beyond.
gcContenders: GIGANTE Sarah
GARCÍA Mavi
REUSSER Marlen
MUZIC Évita
PERSICO Silvia
REALINI Gaia

narratives: Pre-Giro form decider: Held one week before the Giro d'Italia Women starts in Cesenatico (May 30), Burgos is the final tune-up for GC contenders.
Castilian hills: The Castro Jeriz and Viña Pedrosa terrain produces selective racing — pure sprinters rarely feature, and bunch sprints are uncommon.
Tactical 3-stage format: With only three days, there's no recovery from a bad first stage — every day carries GC weight.
Burgos racing tradition: The men's Vuelta a Burgos is a long-standing Vuelta-warm-up race; the women's edition shares that DNA on a compressed timeline.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 2015, the Vuelta a Burgos Féminas was upgraded to the UCI Women's WorldTour in 2020. The race has steadily increased in prestige, with strong GC depth among recent start lists. The men's Vuelta a Burgos (held later in August) has its own tradition as a Vuelta a España warm-up; the women's edition runs in May as a Giro tune-up.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Saturday's Bodegas Viña Pedrosa stage is typically the climbing decider. Sunday's closing day to Medina de Pomar usually produces the final podium shape — late attacks possible on the rolling Castilian terrain.

stages.name: Stage 1: Burgos → Burgos
stages.description: Opening circuit stage of 127km in and around Burgos.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 1 opens the 2026 Vuelta a Burgos Féminas with a 127km circuit start and finish in Burgos. The route features three categorised climbs concentrated in the opening 60km before opening into a flatter run-in to the city centre finish — a hybrid profile that suits puncheurs and breakaway specialists but typically resolves into a reduced bunch sprint.
narratives: Burgos circuit start: the historic Castilian city has hosted the men's Vuelta a Burgos for decades and the women's edition since 2015.
Three classified climbs early but a flat closing section: stage suits sprinter-rouleurs who can survive the morning kickers.
First-day yellow jersey is decided here, with bonus seconds at the line making the closing positioning crucial.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the closing 30km — once the early climbs are done, the lead-out trains organise. Crashes are common on the Burgos city-centre run-in.

stages.name: Stage 2: Castro Jeriz → Bodegas Viña Pedrosa
stages.description: 122km point-to-point ending at the Viña Pedrosa winery — Castilian terrain.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 2 covers 122km from Castro Jeriz to a finish at the Bodega Viña Pedrosa winery in Pedrosa de Duero (Ribera del Duero wine country).
