No categorised climbs — the flattest day of the race — but a 3% gradient in the final kilometre will sort the finishers and likely produce a sprint between rouleurs rather than pure flat sprinters.
narratives: Wine-country stage: the Ribera del Duero finish at a winery is a TV showcase for Spanish viticulture.
No climbs but a 3% closing kilometre flips the script — pure sprinters may struggle to hold the wheel of stronger rouleurs.
Sprinters' jersey contest: the maglia ciclamino-equivalent is decided largely on this kind of mixed-finish day.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the final kilometre — the 3% ramp means lead-out trains break apart and the strongest finisher wins, not the fastest top-end sprinter.

stages.name: Stage 3: Busto de Bureba → Medina de Pomar
stages.description: 126km final stage from Busto de Bureba to Medina de Pomar — northern Castile.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 3 is the QUEEN STAGE and final-day GC decider — 126km from Busto de Bureba to a mountain finish in Medina de Pomar. The decisive day features three categorised climbs strategically placed in the final kilometres, with the Alto de Bocas appearing twice on the closing circuit, giving GC contenders multiple launching points to attack.
narratives: Queen stage on the final day: GC contenders must launch their decisive moves here — there is no stage 4.
Alto de Bocas double ascent in the closing kilometres is the canonical move-launching point.
Tactical complexity: three climbs in the closing kilometres open multiple attack windows; smart pacing decides the maglia oro.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the second Alto de Bocas ascent — that is the typical winning move, with solo attacks from 10-15km out the standard tactic. The GC is decided in the closing 30km.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Dutch UCI 1.Pro one-day race on May 22, finishing in Veenendaal in the central Netherlands. Roughly 200km of flat polder roads with multiple short, sharp climbs on the Utrechtse Heuvelrug ridge — a classic 'sprinters with hills' race.

favorites.description: A bunch sprint or reduced sprint typical. Crosswinds can shred the field on the polder roads. WorldTour sprinters often start.
puncheurs: EEKHOFF Nils
VAN DER POEL Mathieu (occasional)

narratives: Long-running Dutch classic dating to 1985 (men) — Veenendaal is a small Dutch town that builds the race around a city-centre finish.
Used as a final spring tune-up by Dutch teams (Visma-Lease a Bike, Picnic-PostNL) before the Tour de France build-up.
Polder crosswinds are the X-factor — a calm day means a sprint, a windy day means echelons and chaos.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1985. Past winners include Erik Zabel, Jasper Philipsen, and Olav Kooij. UCI 1.Pro since 2020.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Dutch state broadcaster NOS typically carries highlights; FloBikes streams in NA. Watch the closing 30km — sprinters' teams take control unless the wind has already done its work.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Belgian UCI 1.1 one-day race on May 24 in the Hainaut region of Wallonia, named after Belgian classics legend Claude Criquielion. Roughly 195km on rolling Walloon roads with several short Ardennes-lite climbs and a circuit finish that suits puncheurs.

favorites.description: Belgian Continental and ProTeam puncheurs and Classics riders. Often won from a small late breakaway.
puncheurs: DE LIE Arnaud
VAN GILS Maxim
ZINGLE Axel
MEEUS Jordi
TEUNS Dylan

narratives: Tribute race to Claude Criquielion (1957-2015), 1984 World Champion and double winner of La Flèche Wallonne — the climb's steepest bend, the Virage Criquielion, is named after him.
Lotto traditionally targets it for one of their Walloon puncheurs.
Local Hainaut crowds turn out in force — atmospheric race despite the modest UCI ranking.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Founded in 2016 in honour of Claude Criquielion. Held under various title sponsors but always around Lessines, Criquielion's home town.
raceDetails.watchNotes: RTBF (Belgian French-language TV) usually carries highlights. Watch the final lap of the local circuit — that's where the winning move lands.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Belgian women's UCI 1.Pro one-day race on May 24 in the Port of Antwerp area. Roughly 130km combining Flemish polder flats with around 25km of cobbled and gravel sectors through industrial port roads — Belgium's answer to Paris-Roubaix Femmes in May.

keySectors.name: Multiple cobble + gravel sectors (Antwerp port)
keySectors.description: Mix of cobbled industrial estate roads and gravel access tracks through the port. Punishing on bikes and bodies — bike-handling and tyre choice critical.

favorites.description: Cobble-savvy classics riders dominate. Riders who shone at Paris-Roubaix Femmes and the Flemish classics are the favourites.
puncheurs: KOPECKY Lotte
JACKSON Alison
FERRAND-PRÉVOT Pauline
BACKSTEDT Zoe
VOS Marianne
BROWN Grace

narratives: Belgium's premier women's cobble race after Paris-Roubaix Femmes — a dress rehearsal for the Roubaix specialists.
Industrial port scenery makes it visually distinctive — cranes, container ships, and dust-clouds in the background of every photo.
SD Worx-Protime treats it as a home race and brings full strength.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Founded in 2020 as the women's counterpart to the Sels Trophy / Antwerp Port Epic men's race. UCI 1.Pro since 2023.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Sporza (Belgium) carries highlights; FloBikes (NA) streams when available. Watch every cobble sector — selections happen continuously.

races.name: Gravel Bogotá
races.description: UCI Gravel World Series qualifier. Top 25% in each category earn entry to the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships (Nannup, Australia) and 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships (Houffalize, Belgium).

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gravel race in Chía – Bogotá, Colombia. Elite men's and elite women's classifications contest the same course.

broadcast.notes: UCI Gravel World Series events are typically not livestreamed; results and short recap clips appear on the UCI YouTube channel and event social media.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Belgian UCI 1.Pro one-day race on May 25 in the Port of Antwerp area, formerly the Schaal Sels. Roughly 200km combining flat polder roads with around 35km of cobbled and gravel sectors through industrial port roads — a punchy one-day cobble race in late spring.

keySectors.name: Multiple cobble + gravel sectors (Antwerp port)
keySectors.description: Industrial estate cobbles and port-road gravel mixed across the route. Less famous than Paris-Roubaix sectors but punishing in their own way.

favorites.description: Cobble specialists from the Flemish classics squads. WorldTour rouleurs use it to extend their classics season into May.
puncheurs: VAN BAARLE Dylan
DECLERCQ Tim
STUYVEN Jasper
MOSCON Gianni
TURGIS Anthony
TRENTIN Matteo

narratives: Run as the Schaal Sels for over 90 years before rebranding as Antwerp Port Epic. One of Flanders' oldest races.
Late-spring cobbles fix for classics specialists who miss the March-April calendar.
Soudal-QuickStep treats it as a home race; multiple QuickStep wins over the years.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1921 as the Schaal Sels. Rebranded in 2018 as Antwerp Port Epic. Past winners include Tom Boonen and Niki Terpstra.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Sporza carries Belgian highlights; FloBikes streams in NA. Watch the cobble sectors in the second half — selections are constant and the winning group is usually 5-10 riders.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Belgian UCI 1.2 one-day race on May 25 in the Schelde-Lys region of East Flanders. Roughly 175km of flat-to-rolling Flemish kermesse roads with a finishing circuit and a few short cobbled sectors — typically a bunch sprint or small late break.

favorites.description: Belgian Continental teams and a few ProTeam riders chasing UCI points. Sprinters and Classics-style fast finishers split the wins.

narratives: Sponsored by a Mazda dealership — typical of the dense Flemish UCI 1.2 calendar where local businesses fund regional races.
Continental U23 prospects use it as a points-grab close to home.
Often coincides with the Antwerp Port Epic, splitting the strongest local riders.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Modern addition to the Flemish calendar (mid-2010s). Run as a UCI 1.2 race by a regional cycling club.
raceDetails.watchNotes: No live international broadcast. Belgian regional outlets occasionally post a finish-line clip. Action condenses on the final 20km.

raceDetails.courseSummary: French UCI 2.Pro stage race held May 28-31 across four stages totalling roughly 700km in the Mayenne and Sarthe departments of western France. A balanced parcours: two sprint stages, one hilly stage and a punchy circuit finale around Laval that suits both sprinters and puncheurs.

favorites.description: A WorldTour-flavoured field — top sprinters use it as Tour de France lead-up, French puncheurs target the GC.
puncheurs: MARTINEZ Lenny
VAUQUELIN Kévin
GRÉGOIRE Romain
MADOUAS Valentin
gcContenders: GAUDU David
BARDET Romain

narratives: One of the most popular pre-Critérium du Dauphiné stage races for French ProTeams — a points-rich event in front of home crowds.
Groupama-FDJ traditionally treats it as a home race; multiple French podiums each year.
The Laval circuit on the final day always produces drama — narrow streets, short cobbled section, and tight corners reward bike-handling.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1973. Past winners include Sébastien Reichenbach, Romain Bardet, and Olav Kooij. UCI 2.Pro since 2020.
raceDetails.watchNotes: France 3 Pays de la Loire and FloBikes (NA) carry coverage. The hilly stage and the Laval circuit are essential viewing — that's where GC battles play out.

stages.name: Prologue
stages.description: Opening prologue — short individual time trial to set the early GC.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Short prologue ITT in the Mayenne region — first leader's jersey on offer.

stages.name: Stage 1
stages.description: First road stage through the Mayenne region.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling Mayenne terrain — route detail from official roadbook closer to race day.

stages.name: Stage 2
stages.description: Second road stage — typically punchy for the puncheurs.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Mid-race road stage — distance and profile from official roadbook.

stages.name: Stage 3
stages.description: Final road stage of the 51st Boucles de la Mayenne.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Final stage. Total race distance approximately 540km across prologue + 3 road stages.

raceDetails.courseSummary: French women's UCI 2.1 stage race held May 28-31 across four stages totalling roughly 450km in Brittany. A balanced parcours: a prologue or short ITT, two rolling road stages, and a hilly queen stage in the Brittany interior — finishes typically in or near Plouay.

favorites.description: A field of Continental teams and ProTeam women's squads; sprinters battle puncheurs in a finely balanced GC fight.
puncheurs: MUZIC Évita
GHEKIERE Justine
GERY Célia
SQUIBAN Maeva

narratives: Final tune-up before the Tour de France Femmes for several climbing GC contenders.
FDJ-Suez treats it as a home race — Brittany is the team's heartland.
Coupe de France standings often reshuffle here — points-rich event for French Continental teams.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 2000 as a smaller stage race; expanded to UCI 2.1 in 2018 alongside the women's Coupe de France boom.
raceDetails.watchNotes: France 3 Bretagne carries highlights; some stages stream on the organiser's YouTube. Watch the queen stage and the Plouay circuit.

stages.name: Stage 1: Ploemeur start
stages.description: Opening day of the Bretagne Ladies Tour — Ploemeur Grand Départ.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Brittany coast opener from Ploemeur. Total race distance 278.3km across 3 stages.

stages.name: Stage 2
stages.description: Middle day — typically Breton interior.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Inland Brittany rolling terrain. Detail from official roadbook.

stages.name: Stage 3: → Laval
stages.description: Closing day finishing in Laval (Mayenne).

stageDetails.courseSummary: Laval finale. Last chance to shape the GC.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Giro d'Italia Women 2026 (May 30 – June 7) is the third Grand Tour of the women's calendar — nine stages, 1,177.7km, and 12,100m of climbing across Italy. The race opens with two flat Adriatic days (Cesenatico → Ravenna, Roncade → Caorle) before pivoting to the hilly Veneto interior, a 12.7km mountain ITT at Belluno → Nevegal (stage 4), and back-to-back Alpine mountain days at Sestriere (stage 8, queen) and Saluzzo (stage 9). With its longest distance and most elevation in any recent Giro Women edition, the 2026 route is built for pure climbers and TT specialists who can also climb. The race is the centrepiece of the late-spring women's WT calendar.

keyClimbs.name: Italian Dolomites mountain stage
keyClimbs.notes: Final-week summit finish — typically Passo Pordoi, Passo Sella or similar Dolomite pass. GC-decisive.

keyClimbs.name: Apennine summit finish
keyClimbs.notes: Mid-race queen-stage candidate in the Tuscan-Emilian Apennines.

favorites.description: A pure climbers' Grand Tour. The new May date means GC contenders fresh off the Vuelta Femenina and Itzulia arrive with form but also fatigue. Italian and Dutch climbers traditionally dominate.
gcContenders: VOLLERING Demi
REUSSER Marlen
NIEWIADOMA-PHINNEY Kasia
LONGO BORGHINI Elisa
PERSICO Silvia
GIGANTE Sarah
MUZIC Évita
REALINI Gaia

narratives: Sestriere queen stage: The 101km stage 8 finishes on Sestriere, the iconic Olympic resort — short distance + steep summit = expected GC selection from km 1.
Nevegal ITT (stage 4): 12.7km uphill TT — pure climbers can take time today; TT specialists who can climb are dangerous.
Adriatic flat opener: Two flat days for the sprinters' teams to set up early bonus seconds and stage wins.
Van der Breggen's return: Four-time Giro winner returning to GC racing after a multi-season break, co-leading SD Worx-Protime with world champion Lotte Kopecky.
Longo Borghini's UAE debut: First Giro with UAE Team ADQ after six seasons at Lidl-Trek — Italian champion targeting Maglia Rosa on home roads.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Giro d'Italia Women (also known as Giro Donne) is the longest-running women's Grand Tour, first held in 1988. Multiple editions have been dominated by Anna van der Breggen (4 wins), Marianne Vos, and Annemiek van Vleuten. The 2026 edition is the modern rebooted format under RCS Sport organisation, restored as the women's calendar's most prestigious stage race.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Stage 8 (Sestriere) is the must-watch GC decider — short and savage. Stage 4 (Nevegal ITT) is the second race-of-truth. The Adriatic sprints are pure entertainment for the fast men's teams.

stages.name: Stage 1: Cesenatico → Ravenna
stages.description: Adriatic coast opener — flat 139km from Cesenatico to Ravenna. Day for the sprinters.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 1 opens the 2026 Giro d'Italia Women with a 139km flat coastal stage from Cesenatico to Ravenna on the Adriatic. Cesenatico was Marco Pantani's hometown — a symbolic start for the women's edition of the Italian Grand Tour. Pure sprint terrain along the Romagna riviera, ideal for organised lead-out trains.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Pure bunch sprint expected. Watch for crosswind risk along the exposed Adriatic — a strong onshore wind can split the field.
narratives: Cesenatico start: Marco Pantani's hometown, symbolic for the Italian women's Grand Tour opener.
Pure sprint stage: the Romagna Adriatic coast is dead flat — no climbs, no surprises beyond crosswind risk.
First-day yellow jersey: the maglia rosa goes to the fastest sprinter or whichever team nails the lead-out.

stages.name: Stage 2: Roncade → Caorle
stages.description: Veneto coast stage from Roncade to Caorle. 146km of mostly flat terrain.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 2 is 146km from Roncade H-Farm to Caorle along the Veneto coast — another flat sprinter's stage with the Lagoon-and-Adriatic terrain offering no climbs. Caorle is a historic fishing town finish, and the run-in along the Venetian coast brings crosswind risk if the bora blows.
narratives: Veneto coastal stage continues the flat opening block — sprinters' classification battle takes shape.
H-Farm start salutes the Italian innovation campus — modern symbolism for the women's race.
Bora wind risk: the Venetian Adriatic is famous for sudden offshore winds that fracture the peloton.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Second consecutive sprint stage — watch the lead-out trains and any echelon formation in the closing 30km if wind kicks up from the Alps.

stages.name: Stage 3: Bibione → Buja
stages.description: First test of legs in the Friuli hills. 154km finishing in Buja.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 3 covers 154km from Bibione to Buja in Friuli — first medium-mountain test of the race. The terrain transitions from the Venetian plain into the Friulian foothills, with multiple short climbs in the closing 50km setting up an explosive punchy finale. Puncheurs and breakaway specialists are the favourites.
narratives: First selective day: the Friulian foothills produce a reduced bunch or successful breakaway, not a pure sprint.
Buja finish features a punchy uphill kicker that suits classics-and-puncheur riders.
Tactical bridge stage between the flat opener and the GC-stressful ITT on stage 4.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the closing 30km — late attacks from puncheurs are the typical winning move on this Friulian profile. Pure sprinters may not survive.

stages.name: Stage 4: Belluno → Nevegal (ITT)
stages.description: Mountain time trial up to Nevegal Tudor — short 12.7km but uphill, will create GC gaps.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 4 is the 12.7km individual time trial from Belluno to a summit finish at Nevegal Tudor — a UPHILL TT and the first major GC test of the race. The climb to Nevegal is a sustained mid-gradient effort: a hybrid course where TT-specialists with climbing legs outperform pure power TT riders.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Climbers favoured over TT specialists — watch the splits at the bottom of the climb vs the top. Anyone losing 90+ seconds here is effectively out of GC contention.
narratives: Uphill ITT: 12.7km of mostly climbing rewards pure climbers with TT skills over pure power riders.
Mountain TT GC test: expect time gaps of 60-90 seconds between top contenders and pure sprinters.
Sponsorship note: 'Nevegal Tudor' branding reflects the Tudor watches sponsorship now extending to women's cycling.

stages.name: Stage 5: Longarone → Santo Stefano di Cadore
stages.description: First Dolomite mountain stage — 138km through Cadore valleys with serious climbing.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 5 is the first high-mountain day — 138km from Longarone to Santo Stefano di Cadore in the heart of the Dolomites. Classic Dolomite high-mountain terrain with multiple passes and a summit-style finish; one of the GC-decisive days alongside stage 8.
narratives: First Dolomite high-mountain stage: pure climbers' terrain with multiple Alpine passes.
Santo Stefano di Cadore finish in the heart of the Dolomites is symbolic for Italian cycling tradition.
GC selection day: the standings after this stage shape the race going into the Brescello and Salice Terme transitions.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the closing 30km — Dolomite gradients produce the decisive GC moves. Anyone struggling here cannot recover before stage 8's Finestre/Sestriere queen stage.

stages.name: Stage 6: Ala → Brescello
stages.description: Long 155km rolling stage from Trentino down to the Emilian plain.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 6 is a 155km medium-mountain day from Ala (Trentino) to Brescello (Emilia-Romagna). The route descends from Alpine foothills into the Po valley — a transitional stage with early climbing then long flat run-in that can favour a successful breakaway over a bunch finish.
narratives: Transition stage from Alps to Po valley: terrain rewards an early-stage breakaway that survives.
Brescello finish has a Don Camillo literary heritage — the village inspired the Guareschi novels and films.
GC teams may sit back the day after Cadore high mountains — opportunists target a 'free' breakaway win.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the breakaway composition — if GC teams sit back and the move has the right composition, it has a strong chance of staying clear. Otherwise reduced sprint.

stages.name: Stage 7: Sorbolo Mezzani → Salice Terme
stages.description: Longest stage at 165km, crossing the Po valley with hills towards Salice Terme.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 7 covers 165km from Sorbolo Mezzani to Salice Terme — long-distance medium-mountain stage across the Emilian and Oltrepò Pavese hills. The closing climbs in the Pavese hills set up a puncheur-friendly finale, with the wine country backdrop another scenic TV showcase.
narratives: Oltrepò Pavese wine country: rolling vineyard hills offer multiple late-stage launch points.
Longest stage of the race at 165km — attritional rather than explosive, with breakaway riders well-suited.
Day before the queen stage: GC teams typically conserve, opening the door for stage hunters.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the closing 30km Pavese hills — typical winning moves come from puncheurs attacking 10-15km out. Bunch finishes rare on this profile.

stages.name: Stage 8: Rivoli → Sestriere
stages.description: Short, savage Sestriere queen stage — 101km with summit finish at the iconic Olympic resort.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 8 is the QUEEN STAGE — 101km from Rivoli to Sestriere via the legendary Colle delle Finestre, a gravel-road climb that has produced some of the Giro's most iconic moments. Short distance but stacked with climbing: the GC of the 2026 Giro Donne is decided here.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the Finestre — gravel climb attacks from 8-10km out are the typical winning move. Anyone trailing the leader on time has only this stage to flip the standings.
narratives: Colle delle Finestre: 18.5km gravel climb averaging 9.2% — one of the most iconic and brutal climbs in Grand Tour cycling.
Queen stage of the race: short distance + gravel mountain + summit finish at Sestriere = maximum GC drama.
Historical resonance: Finestre is where men's Giro GCs have famously flipped (Carapaz 2019, Yates collapse 2018). Now it carries that weight in the women's race.

stages.name: Stage 9: Saluzzo → Saluzzo
stages.description: Final mountain stage — 143km Western Alps loop from Saluzzo. Last chance to flip the GC.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 9 closes the race with a 143km mountain circuit in and around Saluzzo (Piedmont). Final-day climbing terrain that can flip the GC if a leader is fragile, with multiple ascents of local Cuneese climbs. Not a parade — the 2026 Giro Donne can change hands on the closing-day circuit.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the final laps of the Saluzzo circuit — final-day GC drama is the recent Giro tradition. Spectators get repeated views as the bunch laps the Piedmont hills.
narratives: Saluzzo circuit finale: not a processional stage but a genuine final-day climbing test.
Cuneese hills around Saluzzo produce repeated climbing on the closing laps — Giro tradition of dramatic final days.
Last GC opportunity: any rider close on time can attack the leader if the team is isolated.

topRiders.name: WIEBES Lorena
topRiders.notes: Targeting the two flat Adriatic opening stages.

topRiders.name: LONGO BORGHINI Elisa
topRiders.notes: First Giro for new team UAE Team ADQ after six seasons at Lidl-Trek.

topRiders.name: VAN DER BREGGEN Anna
topRiders.notes: Four-time Giro winner returning to GC racing after a multi-season break — co-leading SD Worx with Kopecky.

topRiders.name: KOPECKY Lotte
topRiders.notes: World road champion; co-leading SD Worx-Protime alongside Van der Breggen.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Spanish UCI 1.1 one-day race on May 31, finishing in Eibar in the Basque Country after roughly 175km. A pure climber's classic with the brutal final ascent of Arrate (Santuario de Arrate, 5.6km at 7.6%) — the same finish used by the Itzulia Basque Country and the Vuelta a España.

keyClimbs.name: Alto de Arrate (Santuario de Arrate)
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic Basque summit finish — used by both the Itzulia and the Vuelta. Decisive.

favorites.description: Pure climbers — small group or solo finish at the Arrate summit guaranteed.

narratives: Eibar's home race — the Basque town wraps itself around the Arrate finish.
Tune-up for the Critérium du Dauphiné and Tour de Suisse for several Spanish and French climbers.
Movistar / Euskaltel-Euskadi rivalry on home roads always produces aggressive racing.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Long-running Basque race dating to the 1940s, revived as a UCI 1.1 in the 2010s. Always finishes on Arrate.
raceDetails.watchNotes: ETB1 (Basque public TV) carries live coverage; Eurosport sometimes picks up. Watch the final 30km — Arrate decides the race.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Belgian UCI 2.Pro stage race held June 1-5 across five stages totalling roughly 800km through the Walloon hills of southern Belgium. A balanced parcours: one or two sprint stages, two punchy hilly stages on Ardennes-lite climbs, and one queen stage with a summit finish — typically Mont Theux or similar.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Citadelle (Namur)
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic short, savage cobbled climb in Namur often used in finishing circuits.

keyClimbs.name: Mur de Thuin / Mont Theux
keyClimbs.notes: Summit finish or final climb on the queen stage — selective and decisive.

favorites.description: Belgian and French ProTeam puncheurs and Classics riders. GC usually goes to a hilly-stage specialist who can survive both sprint stages.
puncheurs: DE LIE Arnaud
VAN GILS Maxim
ZINGLE Axel
MARTINEZ Lenny
VAUQUELIN Kévin
gcContenders: TEUNS Dylan
VAN AERT Wout (occasional)
POWLESS Neilson

narratives: Walloon answer to the Tour de Suisse and Critérium du Dauphiné — ProTeams use it as a key Tour de France tune-up.
Lotto traditionally targets the GC for one of its Walloon protégés — De Lie or Van Gils.
Wet June weather in the Ardennes can turn the queen stage into an attritional epic.
Strong tradition — past winners include Eddy Merckx, Stephen Roche, and Philippe Gilbert.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1974 as Tour de Wallonie. UCI 2.Pro since 2020. Organised by the Walloon cycling federation in partnership with regional sponsors.
raceDetails.watchNotes: RTBF (Belgian French-language public TV) carries live coverage; Eurosport often picks up the queen stage. Watch the hilly stage and the final two days — that's where the GC is decided.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Walloon hills opener. First leader's jersey on offer.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Final day. Walloon hills typically produce a punchy finale.

stages.name: Prologue
stages.description: 1km opening prologue — short ITT to set the first leader's jersey.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Ultra-short 1km prologue. Symbolic time trial start.

stages.name: Stage 1
stages.description: Longest stage of the race — 203km across the Estonian countryside.

stageDetails.courseSummary: 203km across Estonia's flat to rolling terrain. Sprinters' best chance.

stages.name: Stage 2
stages.description: 157km of rolling Estonian terrain — punchy day for breakaways.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Mid-race day with rolling terrain. Reduced bunch finish or breakaway.

stages.name: Stage 3
stages.description: Final 135km stage — last chance to overturn the GC.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Closing stage. Race-day fireworks if GC margins are tight.

races.name: Gravel Adventure
races.description: UCI Gravel World Series qualifier. Top 25% in each category earn entry to the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships (Nannup, Australia) and 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships (Houffalize, Belgium).

raceDetails.courseSummary: Gravel race in Szklarska Poręba – Jakuszyce, Poland. Elite men's and elite women's classifications contest the same course.

broadcast.notes: UCI Gravel World Series events are typically not livestreamed; results and short recap clips appear on the UCI YouTube channel and event social media.

topRiders.name: SEIXAS Paul
topRiders.outsiderNote: 19yo French phenom — Tour de l'Avenir 2025 winner; Strade Bianche, LBL, and Tour debut on the program

topRiders.name: RICCITELLO Matthew
topRiders.outsiderNote: American climber; high GT placings 2025; the next US GC hope

topRiders.name: BISIAUX Léo

primary.notes: Full live + on-demand coverage on FloBikes US.
Full live + on-demand coverage on FloBikes Canada.

stages.name: Stage 1: Vizille → Saint-Ismier
stages.description: Opening hilly stage from Vizille north of Grenoble to Saint-Ismier.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Punchy 140km opener in the Isère hills. First yellow jersey on offer — puncheurs in the mix.

stages.name: Stage 2: Saint-Martin-Le-Vinoux → Le Puy en Velay
stages.description: Longest stage of the race — 237km from Grenoble to the volcanic city of Le Puy en Velay.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Massive 237km transfer through the Massif Central. Volcanic terrain, late rises before Le Puy.

stages.name: Stage 3: Le Perreux → Le Perreux (TTT)
stages.description: 28km team time trial — major early GC shake-up.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 3 is a 28.4km team time trial around a Le Perreux loop — the only TTT of the race and a direct dress rehearsal for the opening TTT of the 2026 Tour de France in Barcelona. Teams that have honed their TTT cohesion through training camps come into this with a meaningful advantage; GC contenders without strong TT teammates risk losing the race here before the mountains begin.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The TTT loop is flat-to-rolling; watch the team formations through the technical sections and how leaders rotate through their domestiques.
narratives: Tour de France warm-up: with the 2026 TdF opening on a TTT in Barcelona three weeks later, this is the rehearsal — every WT team treats stage 3 as a test run.
Aero choreography: small mistakes by lead riders or unbalanced rotations can cost a leader 30 seconds.
GC-leader teams without TT depth can lose the race here before the mountains decide it.

stages.name: Stage 4: Le Puy en Velay → Montrond-les-Bains
stages.description: 165km transitional hilly day from Le Puy down towards the Loire valley.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling terrain favouring breakaways. Reduced bunch finish a possibility.

stages.name: Stage 5: Saint-Chamond → Villars-les-Dombes
stages.description: Longest flat stage at 198km — sprinters' best chance.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Flat 198km transfer towards the Ain. Sprinters' day; teams will fight for the breakaway window.

stages.name: Stage 6: Saint-Vulbas → Crest-Voland
stages.description: 181km hilly transition into the Alps — pre-mountains warm-up.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Climbing intensifies as the race enters Savoie. Crest-Voland finish at altitude.

stages.name: Stage 7: Le Bridoire → Grand Colombier
stages.description: First mountain summit finish — 133km to the Grand Colombier, one of the toughest climbs in the Jura.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 7 covers 133.3km from Le Bridoire to a summit finish atop the Grand Colombier (Jura) — one of the toughest climbs in regular Tour de France usage. The penultimate stage is GC-decisive on its own, with several intermediate climbs softening up legs before the final ascent.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The Grand Colombier is the kind of climb where attacks at 5km to go decide the day — watch for whether GC leaders ride controlled or attack the climbers in their group.
narratives: Grand Colombier summit finish: a regular Tour de France climb whose gradients average 7.5-8.5% with sections over 10% punish pure power riders.
Jura warm-up climbs in the first 100km set up a selective finale rather than a pure summit drag race.
GC battle: the climbers who survive the TTT deficit start their counter-attack here.

stages.name: Stage 8: Beaufort → Plateau de Solaison
stages.description: Final queen stage — 120km from Beaufort to the Plateau de Solaison summit finish.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 8 queen-stage finale: 120km from Beaufort to a summit finish on the Plateau de Solaison in Haute-Savoie. Short by stage-race standards but stacked with climbing — the day exists to crown the GC winner on a hand-picked alpine finish that mirrors the upcoming Tour de France gravity.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The decisive attacks come in the final 10km. Race radios go silent and the GC riders are isolated for the climb to Solaison — pure climbing legs decide the rainbow jersey of the Dauphiné rebrand.
narratives: Plateau de Solaison summit finish: a Haute-Savoie climb that has been used twice before in the modern Dauphiné era and delivers final-day GC drama.
Short distance, high vertical: 120km of intense climbing rather than slow attrition.
Last chance for GC riders behind on time after the Grand Colombier to flip the standings.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 78th edition of the Critérium du Dauphiné — rebranded for 2026 as the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes — covers 1,204.3km across 8 stages from Vizille (Isère) to a summit finish at the Plateau de Solaison (Haute-Savoie), June 7–14, 2026. The route crosses 10 of the region's 12 départements and serves as the traditional final dress rehearsal for the Tour de France, including a 28.4km team time trial on stage 3 that previews the Tour's opening Barcelona TTT. The decisive final three stages all finish uphill — Grand Colombier on stage 7 and Plateau de Solaison on stage 8 as the queen-stage finale.
narratives: New name, same race: 2026 is the first edition under the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes branding (announced 2025). The Critérium du Dauphiné name retires after 77 years, but the Tour de France warm-up role is unchanged.

Team time trial as TdF rehearsal: The 28.4km TTT on stage 3 (Le Perreux loop) mirrors the format of the Tour de France 2026 opening TTT in Barcelona — three weeks earlier, GC teams will use it as a dress rehearsal.

Three uphill finales: Stages 6, 7 and 8 all finish uphill, with Grand Colombier (stage 7) and Plateau de Solaison (stage 8) as the headline summit finishes. The GC is unlikely to be settled before the final weekend.

Tour de France barometer: As the most direct Tour preview race, the Dauphiné historically reveals who arrives at the Tour with the legs — pure climbers, TT specialists, or all-rounders capable of both.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Stage 7 (Grand Colombier) and stage 8 (Plateau de Solaison) are the GC-defining days. The stage 3 TTT is a must-watch for fans tracking which teams have peaked their TTT execution before the Tour de France.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Dauphiné Libéré was founded in 1947 by Belgian journalist Georges Cazeneuve. Renamed the Critérium du Dauphiné in 2010 when ASO took over organisation, it has been the canonical Tour de France warm-up for more than seven decades. The 2026 rename to Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes reflects expanded regional sponsorship.

keyClimbs.name: Grand Colombier
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 7 summit finish — one of the Jura's toughest climbs, regular TdF feature.

keyClimbs.name: Plateau de Solaison
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 8 queen-stage summit finish in Haute-Savoie.

gcContenders: DEL TORO Isaac (UAE Team Emirates - XRG) — UCI #2; the Dauphiné is his most direct Tour de France preparation, with the summit finishes at Grand Colombier and Plateau de Solaison rewarding his climbing-plus-puncheur profile.
AYUSO Juan (Lidl - Trek) — TdF GC threat using this race as final altitude-work check; the back-to-back uphill finishes mirror what awaits him in July.
ONLEY Oscar (INEOS Grenadiers) — emerging GC climber with the team riding for him here; the TTT is the question mark.
SKJELMOSE Mattias (Lidl - Trek) — versatile across climbs and TTs; could keep Ayuso company on the GC summit days.
SEIXAS Paul (Decathlon - CMA CGM Team) — France's hope for the Dauphiné on home roads; specialties cover both the TTT and the mountain stages.
stageHunters: VAN AERT Wout (Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — stage 3 TTT favorite with Visma; could also target the puncheur-flavoured opening stages.
FORTUNATO Lorenzo (XDS Astana Team) — pure climber suited to breakaway days on the harder summit finishes.
RICCITELLO Matthew (Israel - Premier Tech) — climbing specialist hunting stage results in the breakaways.
BISIAUX Léo (Decathlon - CMA CGM Team) — French puncheur with home-roads motivation; opening stages favour his profile.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Pyrenees women's race opener. Race upgraded to 2.Pro for 2026.

stages.name: Stage 2
stages.description: Middle day — typically the Pyrenees mountain queen stage.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Pyrenees mountain stage — GC decider. Race regularly tackles high Pyrenean cols.

stages.name: Stage 3
stages.description: Closing day.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Final Pyrenees-edge stage. Last chance to overturn the GC.

primary.notes: FloBikes US deep link to Copenhagen Sprint Women.
FloBikes Canada deep link to Copenhagen Sprint Women.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Copenhagen Sprint Women — Denmark's UCI Women's World Tour one-day race in its second edition. Held on Saturday June 13, 2026, the day before the men's race, with start in Roskilde and finish in central Copenhagen across Zealand. The route mirrors the men's design — flat-to-rolling Zealand terrain with a closing city-centre circuit built for sprinters and lead-out trains. Echelon risk on the exposed coastal section.
narratives: Second edition: After the 2025 debut, the women's Copenhagen Sprint is now an established part of the WWT calendar.
Sprinter-targeted: One of the rare WWT one-day races built explicitly for the pure sprinters — clean positioning into the final 3km is the headline tactical theme.
Crosswind risk: Zealand's exposed coastal roads between Roskilde and Copenhagen frequently produce echelons that can split the field before the final circuit.
Season midpoint: Coming between the Giro d'Italia Women and the Tour de France Femmes, the race is a barometer for which sprinters have race-day legs for the summer block.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Bunch sprint is the default outcome, but the wind on Zealand can fracture the field early. Watch positioning into the final 5km — the closing laps reward clean lead-outs.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 2025 as part of the inaugural Copenhagen Sprint weekend, alongside the men's race. The event was conceived in 2023 to give Denmark a women's WorldTour fixture and round out the men's Copenhagen Sprint launch.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Copenhagen Sprint — Denmark's UCI WorldTour one-day race in its second edition. The men's race on Sunday June 14, 2026 starts from Roskilde and finishes in central Copenhagen after a journey across Zealand, including several finishing laps through the city centre. The route is deliberately designed for sprinters, with flat-to-rolling Zealand terrain framing a closing circuit that favours fast finishers and well-drilled lead-out trains. Echelons are always a risk on the exposed Danish coast.
narratives: Second edition: After a successful 2025 debut, Copenhagen Sprint settles into its established WorldTour slot — fans and teams now know what to expect on the city-centre run-in.
Sprinter-targeted course: One of the rare WorldTour one-day races built explicitly for the pure sprinters' tactical playbook. The closing laps reward strong lead-out trains and clean positioning.
Zealand crosswind risk: The exposed coastal terrain between Roskilde and Copenhagen is classic echelon country — a strong side-wind can split the field early and rewrite the script.
Vuelta-and-Worlds barometer: Coming a week ahead of the Tour de France and just before Worlds buildup, the result is an early read on which sprinters have peaked for the second half of the season.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Bunch sprint is the default expected finish, but echelons in the first 100km can flip the race. Watch for the lead-out trains entering the closing laps — positioning at 3km to go is decisive on a city-centre circuit.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Copenhagen Sprint was first announced in December 2023 and launched as both a men's and women's UCI WorldTour event in 2025. It is the only Danish race on the WorldTour calendar (alongside the existing PostNord Danmark Rundt stage race). The 2025 inaugural edition established it among the top-tier one-day races.

sprinters: MILAN Jonathan (Lidl - Trek) — UCI #19, raw power favourite on a finish designed for pure sprinters.
DE LIE Arnaud (Lotto Intermarché) — UCI #10, durable enough to survive an echelon-split early scenario.
MEEUS Jordi (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) — Belgian sprinter with WT-grade lead-out around him.
WÆRENSKJOLD Søren (Uno-X Mobility) — Norwegian time-trial-strong sprinter who suits exposed coastal terrain.
ANDRESEN Tobias Lund (Decathlon CMA CGM Team) — home-roads Dane who can fit either a sprint or a reduced-bunch finish.
echelonRiders: Northern Classics specialists capable of forcing splits on the exposed Zealand coast and surviving the closing laps to contest a reduced sprint.

stageDetails.watchNotes: GC decided here. Difficulty 5/5 — if the time trial didn't settle it, this will.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Tour de Suisse 2026 (89th edition) introduces a 'new beginning' format — the first foreign Grand Départ in race history, opening with a 144km hilly circuit in Sondrio (Valtellina, Italy) on June 17. From there the route swings into Switzerland for four stages: Locarno on the Lago Maggiore, Bad Ragaz featuring the Schwägalp climb, a 23.8km Aarburg ITT, and a Queen Stage closing-day mountain showdown at Villars-sur-Ollon with the Col de la Croix and 4,226m+ of elevation gain. The race is the final dress rehearsal for the Tour de France's Italian-leg overlap and an early read on Tour GC form.
narratives: Italian Grand Départ: For the first time in race history, the Tour de Suisse opens outside Switzerland — stage 1 in Sondrio is described by organisers as 'Il Lombardia-style' demanding from day one.
Aarburg ITT: 23.8km mid-race ITT is the race-of-truth before the Queen Stage. TT specialists who climb well can build a buffer ahead of the closing day.
Villars Queen Stage: 151km Alpine finale with 4,226m of climbing — the Col de la Croix anchors a day organisers describe as 'relentlessly up or down, barely a flat metre in sight'.
Tour de France warm-up: Like the Dauphiné, the Tour de Suisse traditionally previews GC form for the Tour de France two weeks later.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The Queen Stage (Villars-sur-Ollon, June 21) is the must-watch — if the ITT didn't settle the GC, the Col de la Croix and Villars summit finish will. The opening Sondrio circuit (Italy) is also unusually demanding for a stage 1 — expect early GC moves.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Tour de Suisse has been a fixture of the cycling calendar since 1933 and joined the WorldTour at its 2005 inception. Traditionally Switzerland's headline stage race and the canonical Tour de France warm-up alongside the Dauphiné. The 2026 edition marks the first time the race has opened outside Switzerland.

gcContenders: POGAČAR Tadej (UAE Team Emirates - XRG) — UCI #1 and three-time world champion using Switzerland as the final altitude block before the Tour de France; alpine summit finishes are tailor-made for his profile.
JORGENSON Matteo (Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — Visma's GC leader in Vingegaard's absence here; coming off strong spring form, this is his marquee June objective.
MARTINEZ Lenny (Bahrain - Victorious) — pure climber whose ceiling appears on the steepest gradients; the Swiss summits are exactly where he can take time.
TIBERI Antonio (Bahrain - Victorious) — emerging Italian GC rider, second team option on a course that suits climber-led tactics.
WIDAR Jarno (Lotto) — young Belgian GC project being tested at WT stage-race level on terrain that mirrors the Tour.
stageHunters: DE LIE Arnaud (Lotto Intermarché) — flat/punchy opening-stage finisher and durable enough to reach intermediate-terrain breakaway days.
BRENNAN Matthew (Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — versatile Visma support rider with stage-result ambitions on transitional days.

keyClimbs.name: Villars-sur-Ollon
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 5 queen-stage summit finish in the Swiss Vaudoise Alps — the GC-decisive climb of the 2026 Tour de Suisse.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Tour de Suisse Women 2026 runs concurrently with the men's race on shared courses (with shorter distances) from June 17–21. Five stages: Sondrio hilly circuit (109.3km), Locarno along Lago Maggiore (105.3km), Bad Ragaz featuring the Schwägalp climb (120.3km), a 23.8km Aarburg ITT (shared with the men's race), and a 100.3km Queen Stage at Villars-sur-Ollon climbing the Col de la Croix with 2,795m of elevation gain. The shared-format approach gives the women's race the same iconic routing as the men's Tour de Suisse.
narratives: Shared-course format: The 2026 edition continues running on the same routes as the men's race (with shorter distances), highlighting parity in the Tour de Suisse organisation.
Aarburg ITT shared: Same 23.8km technical course as the men's race — direct comparison of women's TT-specialist talent.
Villars Queen Stage: 100.3km Alpine finale with Col de la Croix and the Villars-sur-Ollon summit — described by organisers as 'relentlessly up or down'.
Sondrio Grand Départ: For the first time, the Tour de Suisse Women also starts outside Switzerland — in Italian Valtellina.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The Queen Stage (Villars-sur-Ollon, June 21) is decisive — short distance + 2,795m elevation = fireworks from km 1. The Aarburg ITT will produce meaningful GC gaps before the closing climbs.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Tour de Suisse Women was inaugurated in 2021 alongside the broader women's WorldTour expansion. Run by the same organisers as the men's race, it shares routes and gradually expanded in scope across editions.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 Tour de France starts in Barcelona, Spain for the first time in history, featuring 21 stages over 23 days covering approximately 3,333km. The Grand Départ begins with a team time trial up Montjuïc, followed by three stages in Catalonia before crossing into France. The race features eight mountain stages including summit finishes at iconic climbs, two individual time trials, and rest days in key positions. The third week features a demanding finale through the Alps with classic climbs including Mont Ventoux and the Galibier region.

keyClimbs.name: Mont Ventoux
keyClimbs.notes: The Beast of Provence - legendary summit finish. 21.5km at 7.5% via Bédoin.

keyClimbs.name: Col du Galibier
keyClimbs.notes: Classic Alpine giant, often decisive for GC. 18km at 6.9% to 2642m.

keyClimbs.name: Alpe d'Huez
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic 21 hairpins to Dutch Corner. 13.8km at 8.1%, penultimate stage.

keyClimbs.name: Montjuïc
keyClimbs.notes: TTT opener - short punchy climb through Barcelona park, ridden twice.

gcContenders: Previous Tour winners
Grand Tour specialists
Rising talents from past Vueltas/Giros
stageHunters: Breakaway specialists for medium mountain stages
Sprinters for flat stages
TT specialists for both time trials
climbers: Pure climbers for high mountain stages

narratives: Barcelona Grand Départ brings Spanish crowds and a historic first for Catalunya
Third week Alps gauntlet: Mont Ventoux, Galibier, and Alpe d'Huez in final 5 stages
Two time trials (TTT + ITT) create significant time gaps for TT specialists
Can the yellow jersey survive three consecutive mountain stages before Paris?
New generation of GC riders challenging established order
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Tour de France is cycling's most prestigious race, first held in 1903. This will be the first Grand Départ from Barcelona. Previous Spanish starts have come from Bilbao (2023) and San Sebastian. The race has shaped legends from Coppi and Merckx to Indurain and modern champions. The yellow jersey (maillot jaune) has been the symbol of race leadership since 1919.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The TTT opener will set early time gaps between teams. First mountain summit finish creates initial GC selection. Watch for the first rest day as recovery point before the second major mountain block. The final week's consecutive mountain stages (Ventoux, Alps) typically decide the race. Paris finale is traditionally processional until the Champs-Élysées sprints.
raceDetails.gcDynamics: TTT creates early team gaps → First mountains establish GC hierarchy → Second week mountain stages refine gaps → Final week Alps make-or-break for contenders → ITT as final GC test before Paris

stages.name: Stage 1: Barcelona → Barcelona
stages.description: Team time trial with two climbs of Montjuïc; technical city-center course

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 1 opens the 2026 Tour de France with a 19.7km team time trial through Barcelona — the first Grand Départ TTT since 2009 and the first time the modern Tour has opened outside France since the previous Spanish start in 1965. The course winds through Barcelona's urban core with technical sections that punish team rotation errors, providing an immediate GC-relevant test.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch which teams hold the formation through the technical sections — a single dropped rotation kills the time. GC favourites with weak TT teammates are exposed from kilometre one.
narratives: First-ever Grand Départ in Catalonia: Barcelona hosts the modern Tour's first non-French opening since 2007, with a course built for spectacle through the city centre.
TTT GC stakes: time gaps in this 19.7km effort can be 30+ seconds between top WT teams, meaning leaders with weaker TT teammates start the race already in deficit.
Dauphiné/Suisse rehearsal payoff: teams that focused TTT work on stage 3 of the Dauphiné get tangible benefit here.

stageDetails.watchNotes: Punchy terrain will test the sprinters. Breakaways have a chance if they build enough gap over the climbs. GC teams may use the hills to put pressure on rivals.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Mountain stage with summit finish at the ski station Orcières-Merlette

stages.name: Stage 19: Gap → Alpe d'Huez
stages.description: Short but brutal mountain stage finishing atop the legendary Alpe d'Huez

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 19 is the shorter of the two consecutive Alpe d'Huez ascents — 128km from Gap to a first crowning at Alpe d'Huez. Coming on the second alpine day after the previous day's Orcières-Merlette finish, this is the kind of stage that rewards riders who paced themselves the day before.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Pacing decides: riders who attack on the first ascent may pay tomorrow. Watch for who saves matches for the encore on stage 20.
narratives: First of two Alpe d'Huez summit finishes — the 2026 route is the first ever to ascend the 21-hairpin climb twice in 48 hours.
Short distance (128km) means the GC riders go from the gun — no easing in via a long warm-up.
Alpe d'Huez is the most iconic climb in cycling; ascending it twice in a row is route designer Christian Prudhomme's biggest gamble on the 2026 route.

stages.name: Stage 20: Le Bourg-d'Oisans → Alpe d'Huez
stages.description: Queen stage: Col de la Croix de Fer, Col du Télégraphe, Col du Galibier (roof of the Tour), Col de Sarenne, Alpe d'Huez. ~5,600m elevation gain

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 20 is the longer Alpe d'Huez encore — 171km from Le Bourg-d'Oisans for a second consecutive day finishing at the summit of the 21-hairpin climb. The penultimate stage and queen-stage finale of the 2026 Tour: GC riders who held back on stage 19 unleash the decisive attacks here.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The decisive attacks come at 5-7km from the summit. With Paris being processional, this is the last selective moment of the 2026 Tour — domestiques empty matches here.
narratives: Penultimate-stage Alpe d'Huez encore: the GC decider, with 24 hours' fatigue stacked on top of the first ascent.
Double Alpe is unprecedented in modern Tour history — the 2026 route uses it as the dramatic centrepiece of the closing weekend.
Last GC chance: anyone trailing the leader on time has only this stage to flip the standings before Paris.

stages.name: Stage 21: Thoiry → Paris
stages.description: Ceremonial finale with multiple ascents of Rue Lepic/Montmartre before Champs-Élysées sprint

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 21 is the closing-day Paris parade — 130km from Thoiry to the Champs-Élysées via the 2024-introduced Montmartre climb. The Paris finish keeps the post-2024 format with multiple ascents of the Butte Montmartre adding suspense to what is traditionally a processional sprint stage.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch for breakaway attacks over the Montmartre climbs — recent editions have seen non-sprint stage wins on this format. Lead-out trains have to manage the cobble surges.
narratives: Champs-Élysées tradition with Montmartre twist: post-2024 format keeps multiple ascents of the punchy Butte Montmartre before the sprint.
No longer a pure parade: the Montmartre cobbles can split the bunch and reward late puncheur attacks ahead of the sprint.
Closing day ceremony: GC winner toasts in yellow on the Champs while the sprinters contest the line — both rituals continue.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 45th Donostia San Sebastián Klasikoa (formerly known as the Clásica San Sebastián) is the Basque Country's marquee one-day WorldTour race — a 219km route from and back to San Sebastián's iconic Concha Bay coastline. The course traverses the Basque hills with multiple categorised climbs in the closing 60km, with the Jaizkibel climb traditionally setting up the decisive moves. Held on Saturday August 1, 2026, the race serves as a crossroads of the season: a final hit-out for Tour de France riders heading into the rest period, and an early-form indicator for those building toward the Vuelta a España.
narratives: Tour-de-France crossroads: Held one week after the Tour de France finish, the start list is a mix of TdF post-Tour campaigners and rested classics specialists targeting late-summer form.
Jaizkibel as the decider: The category-1 Jaizkibel climb is the canonical move-launching ascent. Recent editions have added new climbs in the closing 50km, but Jaizkibel remains the signature.
Vuelta warm-up: For climbers building toward the Vuelta a España (starting late August), the punchy Basque hills offer the ideal final sharpening race.
Basque atmosphere: The race finishes in the heart of San Sebastián overlooking the Concha — one of the most distinctive one-day finales in the sport, with traditionally huge crowds.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The final 60km is where the race is decided. Watch the Jaizkibel ascent (~60km from finish) for the first major selection, and then the closing-circuit climbs for solo or small-group attacks. Bunch sprints are rare given the terrain.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1981, the Donostia San Sebastián Klasikoa is one of the modern era's youngest one-day classics but has quickly built prestige — a frequent stop for Vuelta a España GC contenders sharpening form. The race rebranded from 'Clásica San Sebastián' to its current Basque-language name in recent editions to reflect its Basque Country identity.

keyClimbs.name: Jaizkibel
keyClimbs.notes: Classic Basque coastal climb — the iconic launching point of the Klasikoa from the first ascent. Featured in the men's race profile twice.

keyClimbs.name: Erlaitz
keyClimbs.notes: The decisive climb of the race — 10.5% average with sustained 12-15% sections. This is where winning solo attacks are typically launched.

keyClimbs.name: Murgil Tontorra
keyClimbs.notes: Brutal short steep climb 8km from the line — 10.2% average with a ramp section over 20%. The final shake-out before the descent into Donostia.

puncheurs: BILBAO Pello (ESP / Bahrain - Victorious) — home-roads Basque favourite, perfect rider for the Klasikoa's repeated punchy climbs.
GALL Felix (AUT / Decathlon CMA CGM Team) — strong climber with classics ambitions; the Erlaitz gradients reward his profile.
CHRISTEN Jan (SUI / UAE Team Emirates - XRG) — Swiss climber-puncheur, the Klasikoa is exactly the kind of one-day he targets.
BISIAUX Léo (FRA / Decathlon - CMA CGM Team) — French puncheur whose explosive gradients work matches Murgil Tontorra perfectly.
darkHorses: WIDAR Jarno (BEL / Lotto) — Belgian climber-GC project on terrain suited to a long-range attack.
RICCITELLO Matthew (USA / Israel - Premier Tech) — American climber hunting a high-profile one-day result.

primary.notes: FloBikes US deep link to 2026 Tour de France Femmes avec Zwift.
FloBikes Canada deep link to 2026 Tour de France Femmes avec Zwift.

stages.name: Stage 1: Lausanne → Lausanne
stages.description: Swiss Grande Départ — 137km hilly opener around Lausanne with an uphill finish.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 1 opens the 5th edition of the Tour de France Femmes with a 137km circuit start in Lausanne, Switzerland — the second consecutive Grand Départ outside France after 2022's Paris start and 2024's Rotterdam. The hilly Vaud canton terrain framing Lake Geneva favours puncheurs over pure sprinters; expect a chaotic opening day with the yellow jersey up for grabs in the closing kilometres.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the final 30km — the lakeside circuit has small kickers that can split the bunch. Puncheurs and breakaway specialists are the strong favourites for the opening yellow jersey.
narratives: Swiss Grand Départ: first time TdF Femmes has opened in Switzerland — Lausanne was previously the men's Tour grand départ in 2022.
Lake Geneva hills make this a puncheur's opener — the yellow jersey will favour a strong all-rounder, not a pure sprinter.
Day-one yellow jersey gambit: small bonus seconds at the line can put a rider in yellow for a week.

stages.name: Stage 2: Aigle → Geneva
stages.description: 149km from UCI headquarters in Aigle to Geneva — first sprint chance.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 2 covers 149km from Aigle (Swiss UCI headquarters town) to Geneva on largely flat terrain following the Rhône valley. After the hilly opening day, this is the pure sprinters' first chance — a clean run-in to Geneva with the closing kilometres on the Rhône embankments suiting organised lead-out trains.
narratives: Aigle start salutes the UCI headquarters — symbolic nod to women's racing recognition.
First true sprint stage: the pure sprinters who survived the opening hills go for green-jersey points and stage glory.
Geneva finish keeps the race in Switzerland for the second consecutive day before crossing into France.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Pure sprint expected. Watch lead-out positioning in the final 3km along the Rhône embankment — wind direction can flip the favourites.

stages.name: Stage 3: Geneva → Poligny
stages.description: 157km crossing into the French Jura — sprinters' day with rolling middle.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 3 from Geneva crosses into France for the first time and covers 157km to Poligny in the Jura. Officially flagged hilly per route information — the Jura's rolling vineyards and short steep climbs make this a transitional stage that could go either to a reduced sprint or a successful breakaway.
narratives: Border-crossing stage: peloton enters France for the first time after two Swiss days.
Jura terrain is classic breakaway country — short climbs and rolling roads reward opportunists.
Bridge stage between flat sprinting and the Dijon ITT — riders save matches for tomorrow's decisive solo effort.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the breakaway composition — if the morning move contains GC-relevant riders, the peloton has to chase. Otherwise expect a reduced bunch sprint.

stages.name: Stage 4: Gevrey-Chambertin → Dijon (ITT)
stages.description: 21km individual time trial through the Burgundy vineyards from Gevrey-Chambertin to Dijon.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 4 is the GC-decisive 21km individual time trial from Gevrey-Chambertin to Dijon — through the iconic Burgundy wine country to the Burgundian capital. Rolling course with a likely flat finish; long enough to produce meaningful time gaps between TT specialists and pure climbers.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the early intermediates — TT specialists set the times. The GC contenders' splits at 10km reveal who's racing for the maglia jaune.
narratives: Burgundy wine country backdrop: Gevrey-Chambertin is one of France's most famous Grand Cru appellations.
21km is meaningful: time gaps of 60-90 seconds between top TT specialists and pure climbers are realistic.
GC reshape: anyone hoping for yellow in Nice must limit losses here or post a top result.

stages.name: Stage 5: Mâcon → Belleville-en-Beaujolais
stages.description: Beaujolais wine-country hills — 140km, breakaway terrain.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 5 covers 140km from Mâcon to Belleville-en-Beaujolais on hilly Beaujolais terrain. Multiple short climbs through the Beaujolais wine country build to a punchy finish — a stage designed for puncheurs and breakaway specialists rather than pure sprinters or GC climbers.
narratives: Beaujolais wine country stage: vineyard climbs and rolling terrain are pure puncheur territory.
After the GC-stressful ITT, this is a 'free' stage where breakaways have a strong chance.
Short steep climbs in the closing 30km set up explosive late attacks rather than a bunch sprint.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the closing climbs — solo attacks from 10-15km out are typical winning moves on this kind of terrain. Bunch finishes are rare on Beaujolais profiles.

stages.name: Stage 6: Montbrison → Tournon-sur-Rhône
stages.description: 153km into the Rhône valley — pre-mountains hilly transition.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 6 from Montbrison to Tournon-sur-Rhône is a 153km hilly day descending into the Rhône valley. The Forez and Pilat hills provide multiple launching points before a faster descent to the Rhône, where echelon risk is real if a mistral or northerly wind blows.
narratives: Forez and Pilat regional hills offer pure breakaway terrain — a classic French puncheur's stage.
Rhône valley descent introduces wind exposure — mistral wind history makes echelons possible.
Last hilly day before the Mont Ventoux queen stage on stage 7.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Two scenarios: a successful breakaway over the closing climbs, OR mistral-driven echelons that splinter the bunch in the Rhône valley. Watch the wind forecast.

stages.name: Stage 7: La Voulte-sur-Rhône → Mont Ventoux
stages.description: Mont Ventoux summit finish — 144km from the Rhône valley to the Giant of Provence.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 7 is the QUEEN STAGE — 144km from La Voulte-sur-Rhône to a summit finish on the legendary Mont Ventoux. The 'Giant of Provence' is one of the most iconic climbs in cycling: 21km of relentless ascent with the famous moonscape of the upper slopes exposed to mistral wind. This is the day the 2026 Tour de France Femmes is decided.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The decisive attacks come in the final 6km, where the climb steepens above the tree line into the moonscape. Mistral wind from the north can punish riders unable to shelter in groups. This is the make-or-break GC moment of the race.
narratives: Mont Ventoux summit finish: the most iconic mountain in cycling, with crushing gradients and brutal exposed final kilometres on the moonscape.
Queen stage of the 2026 race — GC decided here, with breakaway riders also targeting the rainbow-jersey-style prestige of a Ventoux win.
Historical weight: Ventoux is where Tom Simpson died in 1967; the climb's gravitas in cycling history is unmatched.

stages.name: Stage 8: Sisteron → Nice
stages.description: Longest stage at 175km — sprinter-friendly with a sting in the tail into Nice.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 8 covers 175km from Sisteron to Nice — the longest stage of the 2026 Tour de France Femmes, transitioning from Haute-Provence into the Côte d'Azur. Officially flagged hilly: rolling Provençal terrain with multiple short climbs before descending to the Mediterranean coastline finish in Nice.
narratives: Longest stage of the race: 175km of attritional rolling Provence is recovery from Ventoux for some, opportunity for others.
Côte d'Azur finish in Nice carries the symbolic weight of the French Riviera — the city has anchored Tour finishes since 2024.
Day after Ventoux: GC may sit conservatively while opportunists target a stage win on tired legs.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Breakaway has a strong chance — GC teams are typically defensive the day after the queen stage. Watch the final 20km approach to Nice for late attacks.

stages.name: Stage 9: Nice → Nice
stages.description: Closing GC-decider — 99km Nice circuit with four ascents of the Col d'Eze.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 9 is the final-day finale — a 99km hilly circuit in and around Nice, with the Côte d'Azur hills providing multiple steep launching points before the Promenade des Anglais finish. The shortest stage of the race but stacked with climbing; the Nice circuit can flip yellow on the final day if a GC lead is fragile.

keyClimbs.name: Col d'Eze (×4)
keyClimbs.notes: Climbed four times in the closing circuit

stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch the closing laps of the Nice circuit — final-day attacks can change the GC if the leader is isolated. Multi-time loop format gives spectators repeated views of the peloton.
narratives: Final-day hilly circuit: not a parade — Nice has hosted recent Tour finishes that produced major final-day GC drama.
99km short distance means full-gas racing from the gun, with attacking starts the norm.
Côte d'Azur backdrop and Promenade des Anglais finish frame the closing TV showcase of the race.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The fifth edition of the Tour de France Femmes avec Zwift covers 1,175km across 9 stages from a Swiss Grand Départ (Lausanne, Aigle, Geneva) to a Côte d'Azur double-finale in Nice. With 18,795m of elevation gain, it is the longest and toughest TdF Femmes ever. A 21km individual time trial in Dijon (stage 4) is the only race-of-truth before a series of demanding hilly stages, the Mont Ventoux summit finish on stage 7, and a closing Nice circuit climbing the Col d'Eze four times on the final day.
narratives: Swiss Grande Départ: Lausanne, Aigle (UCI headquarters) and Geneva host the first three stages — a continuation of the race's growing international footprint.
Mont Ventoux debut: The iconic Giant of Provence as a summit finish on stage 7 is a first for the women's race. 21km of climbing at over 7% average will define the GC.
21km Dijon ITT: First TdF Femmes ITT since 2024. TT specialists who can climb will be doubly dangerous given the hilly second half.
Nice GC decider: After Ventoux, the final stage in Nice tackles the Col d'Eze four times in a 99km circuit — race director Marion Rousse calls the route 'mischievous'.
ASO scheduling: ASO chose to start a week after the men's race ends to avoid resource overlap, giving the women's race its own clear window.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Nine stages packed into nine days — fans should expect day-on-day GC pressure with no rest day. The Ventoux stage is the must-watch; the Dijon ITT and Nice finale are the other two decisive moments. Disable autoplay on YouTube — final-KM clips often surface in feeds.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Tour de France Femmes was relaunched in 2022 (after a long hiatus from earlier eras). Annemiek van Vleuten won the inaugural edition (2022), Demi Vollering claimed the 2023 edition, and Kasia Niewiadoma took the 2024 title. The 2026 route is the first to add Mont Ventoux, deliberately raising the GC bar.

keyClimbs.name: Mont Ventoux
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 7 queen-stage summit finish — Giant of Provence.

keyClimbs.name: Col d'Eze (×4)
keyClimbs.notes: Stage 9 closing circuit — climbed four times in the Nice finale.

gcContenders: VOLLERING Demi (NED / FDJ United - SUEZ) — UCI #1 women's climber-and-GC rider, multiple TdF Femmes podiums; Ventoux queen stage is the day to confirm rainbow ambitions.
NIEWIADOMA Kasia (POL / Canyon-SRAM zondacrypto) — Polish climber-GC threat, the rider who broke the Vollering streak in 2024; Ventoux gradients match her style.
LONGO BORGHINI Elisa (ITA / UAE Team ADQ) — Italian veteran climber-classics rider; the route's mixed terrain (Dijon TT + Ventoux) suits her completeness.
REUSSER Marlen (SUI / Movistar Team) — TT-strong GC rider with a Vuelta a Burgos victory under her belt; the Dijon ITT is the cornerstone of her GC card.
FERRAND-PRÉVOT Pauline (FRA / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — MTB world champion turned roadie with home-roads motivation; Ventoux on French soil is the highest possible stage to win.
stageHunters: WIEBES Lorena (NED / Team SD Worx - Protime) — top sprinter favourite for stages 2 + 3 + 8 (the flat days).
BALSAMO Elisa (ITA / Lidl - Trek) — Italian classics-sprinter, hunting the cyclamen jersey on the flat stages.
VOS Marianne (NED / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — all-terrain legend; stage hunt on the mixed-terrain hilly stages 5 + 6 + 9.
CHABBEY Elise (SUI / FDJ United - SUEZ) — Swiss rider with home-roads Grand Départ motivation, suited to puncheur stages.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 83rd Tour de Pologne UCI WorldTour runs August 3–9, 2026 under the slogan 'North-South', tracing a 7-stage route from the Baltic coast at Gdynia to the historic salt-mining town of Wieliczka outside Kraków. Approximately 807km of racing connects coastal sprint stages to the Tatra mountain finales that traditionally decide the GC. Race director Czesław Lang has framed the 83rd edition around the connection between Poland's coastal north and mountainous south.
narratives: North-South slogan: The 2026 route deliberately spans Poland from the Baltic coast (Gdynia) to the southern foothills approaching the Tatra mountains, traversing the country end-to-end.
Tatra summit decider: As in most recent editions, mid-second-half mountain stages in the Tatra range historically settle the GC.
Wieliczka finale: The finish in the UNESCO-listed salt-mining town of Wieliczka brings the race into the cultural heartland of Małopolska.
Tour de France warm-down: For some of the WorldTour's GC contenders, the Tour de Pologne is the first hit-out after the Tour de France — fresh legs or fatigue can flip race tactics.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Bonus seconds at intermediate sprints have historically been decisive given how tight the GC tends to be in Poland — watch for sprinters' teams shifting from pure bunch-sprint mode to bonus hunting on transitional stages.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Founded in 1928 as an amateur event and fully professional since 1993, the Tour de Pologne joined the UCI WorldTour in 2005. It remains the only WorldTour stage race in central Europe, organised by the company Lang since 1993. Past editions have featured a mix of climbing-test stages around Karpacz and Zakopane and bunch-sprint days on the way south.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The ADAC Cyclassics is Hamburg's UCI WorldTour one-day race — Northern Germany's largest cycling event. The 2026 edition (Sunday August 16) starts from Buxtehude in Lower Saxony for the second year running, with the peloton covering 207.4km across the Hamburg Metropolitan region before finishing in central Hamburg. The route includes 1,700m+ of vertical ascent — significantly more climbing than recent editions. The signature Waseberg climb is the race's defining feature, with its final ascent topping out approximately 16km from the line.
narratives: Buxtehude Grand Départ returns: After a successful 2025 debut, Buxtehude hosts the start again, putting Lower Saxony's Hanseatic city on the WorldTour map.
More climbing in 2026: 207km with 1,700m+ of vertical, deliberately heavier than recent editions — organisers want the race to favour puncheurs over pure sprinters.
Waseberg as the launchpad: The final ascent of the Waseberg climb at ~16km from the finish is the canonical move-launching point — winning attacks of recent editions have all gone here.
Hamburg festival: The race is the centrepiece of a multi-event cycling festival in Hamburg, with amateur races sharing the finish line through the city centre.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The Waseberg ascents in the closing 30km decide the race. A reduced group will reach Hamburg most years, with sprinters/puncheurs duking it out on the Elbe-side finish straight.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1996, the ADAC Cyclassics (formerly the EuroEyes Cyclassics) has been on the UCI WorldTour since the series' 2005 launch. Hamburg's premier one-day race, traditionally held in mid-August after the Tour de France and before the Vuelta a España build-up.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 21st Renewi Tour (formerly Benelux Tour, BinckBank Tour and Eneco Tour) runs August 19–23, 2026 — five stages tailor-made for Classics riders and hilly specialists. Four stages take place in Belgium and one in the Netherlands: a 13km ITT on day 2. Stages 1 (Diest) and 4 are sprint-friendly, while stage 3 (Celles → Geraardsbergen) carries a full Flemish-classic profile with multiple cobbled climbs and two laps of a Geraardsbergen finishing circuit featuring the Muur van Geraardsbergen and Bosberg. The race climaxes with a Sunday final that typically rewards punchy GC contenders.
narratives: Five names, one race: Now in its 21st edition, the Renewi Tour has cycled through brand identities (Eneco Tour → BinckBank Tour → Benelux Tour → Renewi Tour) without changing its tactical DNA — Classics riders dominate.
Muur van Geraardsbergen as the centrepiece: Stage 3's twin-loop circuit climbs the iconic Muur and Bosberg multiple times — historically the GC-defining day.
ITT on day 2: A 13km flat ITT in the Netherlands gives TT specialists a chance to grab a GC buffer before the Flemish hills.
Diest Grand Départ: 2026 launches from Diest, Flemish Brabant — a new opening for the race.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Stage 3 (Celles → Geraardsbergen) is the must-watch — the Muur loop will splinter the field. Stage 5 traditionally serves up a late GC twist if margins are thin. The day-2 ITT is the only race-of-truth, but only 13km worth.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held as the Eneco Tour in 2005, the race has cycled through multiple title sponsors (Eneco → BinckBank → Benelux → Renewi). It became a UCI WorldTour event in 2011. The combined Belgian/Dutch terrain — Flemish hills + Limburg climbs + coastal flats — has made it a perennial showcase for Classics-style riders.

keyClimbs.name: Muur van Geraardsbergen
keyClimbs.notes: Cobbled Flemish climb featured in Renewi Tour's traditional Flemish-hills stage — punchy and explosive launching point.

keyClimbs.name: Bosberg
keyClimbs.notes: Cobbled Flemish climb often paired with Muur on the Flemish-hills stage.

VAN AERT Wout (BEL / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — Belgian home-roads favourite, ideal for the cobbled Flemish-hills stage.
VAN DER POEL Mathieu (NED / Alpecin-Premier Tech) — Dutch home-roads star, similar all-terrain dominance.
stageHunters: MERLIER Tim (BEL / Soudal Quick-Step) — Belgian sprinter for flat sprint stages.
PEDERSEN Mads (DEN / Lidl - Trek) — top classics-sprinter for puncheur-friendly stages.

WÆRENSKJOLD Søren (NOR / Uno-X Mobility) — TT-strong rouleur for the prologue/ITT.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Lloyds Tour of Britain Women 2026 is the UK's premier Women's WorldTour stage race — four compressed stages across Britain from August 19 to 22, 2026, ending with a Glasgow finale. The total race distance is 423.6km. The compressed-format approach (4 stages over 4 days) means every stage matters and there is no easy day. The race runs alongside the men's Tour of Britain (Sept 2–6) as part of a 10-day national cycling block organised by British Cycling.
narratives: Compressed 4-stage format: Each stage is a quasi-decider — no rest days, no transition days. GC contenders need to be on top form from stage 1.
Glasgow finale: Scotland's largest city hosts the closing stage — historically the British Tour has used Glasgow's urban circuit to set up punchy late attacks.
British Cycling era: British Cycling took over the organisation of the race (formerly the Women's Tour) in 2024 — the 2026 edition is the third under their stewardship.
Top WWT field: As one of the late-summer WWT stops, the start list typically draws WorldTour leaders looking for points and form before the Worlds.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Mixed British terrain favours all-round riders — strong climbers AND sprinters can both feature on different stages. The Glasgow final stage tends to produce late breaks or reduced-bunch sprints.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Lloyds Tour of Britain Women, formerly known as the Women's Tour, is one of the longest-running British women's professional stage races. It was rebranded and taken over by British Cycling in 2024 as the British governing body consolidated organisation of both the men's and women's events. The 2025 edition was the first under the new branding.
