stageDetails.watchNotes: First red jersey on offer — gaps will be small but specialists will try to grab them.

stages.name: Stage 2: Monaco → Manosque
stages.description: Long hilly transfer day with two Cat 3 climbs in the first half.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 2 leaves Monaco on the first full road stage of the 2026 Vuelta, heading 215km west into Provence to finish in Manosque. The route crosses the Alpes-Maritimes hinterland with a series of mid-stage hills before opening up to longer rolling terrain in the run-in. The Manosque finish is not a true sprint setup — punchier finishers and breakaway specialists are equally viable.
narratives: Longest stage of the first week — 215km will stretch legs after the Monaco ITT opening.
Provençal hills mid-stage are ideal breakaway launch points before the rolling finale.
Hot, dry conditions traditional for early-September French Riviera transition stages.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Final 30km has small kickers that can split the bunch — watch for puncheur attacks rather than a pure sprint finish.

stages.name: Stage 3: Gruissan → Font Romeu
stages.description: First summit finish at Font Romeu (1,800m) via Col de Mont-Louis.

stages.name: Stage 4: Andorra la Vella → Andorra la Vella
stages.description: Short, savage Andorran circuit with four Cat 1/3 climbs in 104km.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Andorra mountain circuit: Port d'Envalira, Collada de Beixalis, Coll d'Ordino, Alto de La Comella.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Classic Andorra explosion stage. Short distance, brutal climbs — early GC indicator.

stages.name: Stage 5: Falset (Costa Daurada) → Roquetes (Terres de l'Ebre)
stages.description: Catalan hilly stage with the Alto de Paüls late.

stages.name: Stage 6: Alcossebre → Castelló
stages.description: Medium-mountain stage finishing atop Pal (Cat 1, 9.6km @ 6.3%).

stages.name: Stage 7: Vall d'Alba → Aramón Valdelinares
stages.description: Pyrenean stage with summit finish at Cerler (12.1km @ 5.8%).

stages.name: Stage 8: Puçol → Xeraco
stages.description: Mediterranean coast sprint stage — the Puerto de Barx is the only obstacle.

stages.name: Stage 9: La Vila Joiosa → Alto de Aitana (Costa Blanca)
stages.description: Six categorised climbs ending atop the legendary Alto de Aitana (Cat 1).

stageDetails.watchNotes: Last stage before the first rest day — GC contenders will not want to lose time here.

stages.name: Rest Day
stages.description: First rest day.

stages.name: Stage 10: Alcaraz–Elche de la Sierra → Elche de la Sierra
stages.description: Rolling Castilla–La Mancha stage with the Puerto de Socovos late.

stages.name: Stage 11: Cartagena–Lorca → Lorca
stages.description: Sprint opportunity with the Alto del Morrón mid-stage.

stages.name: Stage 12: Vera–Calar Alto → Calar Alto
stages.description: Andalusian mountain stage to the high-altitude Calar Alto observatory (2,100m+).

stages.name: Stage 13: Almuñécar–Loja → Loja
stages.description: Medium-mountain Granada-province stage with two Cat 1/2 climbs.

stages.name: Stage 14: Jaén–Sierra de la Pandera → Sierra de la Pandera
stages.description: Brutal short mountain stage finishing atop the Sierra de la Pandera.

stageDetails.watchNotes: Classic Vuelta brutality — short, vicious, gaps guaranteed.

stages.name: Stage 15: Palma del Río–Córdoba → Córdoba
stages.description: Three Cat 3 climbs en route to Córdoba with Puerto Artafi 30km out.

stages.name: Rest Day
stages.description: Second rest day.

stages.name: Stage 16: Cortegana–La Rábida → La Rábida (Palos de la Frontera)
stages.description: Rolling first half then flat coastal finish near Huelva.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 16 starts post-rest-day from Cortegana in Huelva province, 186km west to La Rábida (Palos de la Frontera) — historic departure point of Columbus to the New World. The route is flat with one short rise mid-stage but is otherwise pure sprinters terrain across the Andalusian plain.
narratives: Sprinters' day after the second rest day — pure bunch sprint expected.
Coastal heat and crosswind risk could complicate the lead-out trains.
Symbolic Columbus departure finish at La Rábida adds historical weight.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Pure sprint finish expected — watch the lead-out trains in the final 5km. Echelon risk if there's any Atlantic side-wind.

stages.name: Stage 17: Dos Hermanas–Sevilla → Sevilla
stages.description: The flattest stage of the entire race — pure sprint into Seville.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Stage 17 from Dos Hermanas (Sevilla suburb) covers 189km on a flat-to-rolling route finishing in central Sevilla. Andalusian capital terrain with a city-centre finishing circuit — second consecutive sprinters' day before the time trial.
narratives: Back-to-back sprint stages set up sprint-points classification battle.
Sevilla city-centre run-in is technically demanding with multiple roundabouts.
Heat is the wildcard — Andalusia in early September pushes 35°C+ on plains.
stageDetails.watchNotes: City-centre circuit means positioning at 3km to go is decisive. Watch for crashes in the technical finale.

stages.name: Stage 18: El Puerto de Santa María → Jerez de la Frontera (ITT)
stages.description: 32km flat coastal ITT — likely GC-decisive race-of-truth.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Mainly flat coastal route, with potential crosswinds along the Bay of Cádiz.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Last chance for time trialists. Wind direction could be decisive.

stages.name: Stage 19: Vélez-Málaga → Peñas Blancas (Estepona)
stages.description: Three categorised climbs with summit finish at Peñas Blancas (Cat 1).

stages.name: Stage 20: La Calahorra → Collado del Alguacil
stages.description: Decisive penultimate mountain stage — Cat Especial finish at Collado del Alguacil (8.3km @ 9.8%).

stageDetails.courseSummary: Five categorised climbs including three Cat 1s and the Cat Especial summit finish.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The GC will be settled here. 9.8% average for 8.3km is savage.

stages.name: Stage 21: Carrefour Granada → Granada
stages.description: Ceremonial finale on a Granada circuit including the Alhambra climb (raced five times).

stageDetails.watchNotes: GC processional in the first half, then bunch sprint in the closing kilometres. Final stage of the 2026 Grand Tour calendar.
stageDetails.courseSummary: Final-stage Sunday 21: a short 99km from Carrefour Granada into the centre of Granada — historic Andalusian city finishing the race. The Vuelta's closing day traditionally features the overall winner riding to the finish in a processional opening before the sprinters contest the line.
narratives: Granada finish is a departure from the traditional Madrid closing — first GT in years to end outside the capital.
Short 99km makes for a tactical, attack-friendly final stage rather than a pure parade.
GC battle should be resolved on stage 20, leaving the sprinters to fight the line.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 81st Vuelta a España (August 22 – September 13, 2026) opens with a Monaco Grand Départ — the race's third consecutive foreign start (after Portugal 2024 and Italy 2025) — and finishes with an unusual circuit finale in Granada featuring the Alhambra climb instead of a traditional ceremonial sprint. The 21-stage, 3,275km route packs 58,156m of climbing across 9 flat/hilly stages, 10 medium-mountain or mountain stages, and 2 individual time trials (a 9.6km Monaco prologue and a 32km Jerez ITT). Two rest days fall on August 31 and September 7.
narratives: Monaco Grand Départ: Third consecutive foreign start for the Vuelta — a 9.6km ITT around the Principality opens proceedings before crossing into France and Andorra.
Alhambra finale: The traditional ceremonial closing stage is replaced by a circuit in Granada that climbs the Alhambra five times — the GC could still flip on the final day if margins are tight.
Two ITTs: A 9.6km prologue and a 32km Jerez time trial give the TT specialists meaningful real estate, balancing the climbing-heavy middle weeks.
Climbing-heavy middle: Stages 6–14 visit Andorra, the Pyrenees, the Sierra Nevada area (Aitana, Calar Alto, Sierra de la Pandera) — that's where the GC is built or lost.

Vuelta tradition: True to form, Spanish summit finishes dominate — Aitana, Cerler, Calar Alto, Sierra de la Pandera, Peñas Blancas, and the Cat-Especial Collado del Alguacil (8.3km @ 9.8%) on stage 20.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Stages 4 (Andorra), 7 (Cerler), 9 (Aitana), 12 (Calar Alto), 14 (Sierra de la Pandera), 19 (Peñas Blancas) and especially 20 (Collado del Alguacil at 9.8% avg) are the mountain crux days. The closing Granada / Alhambra circuit on stage 21 is the rare GC-relevant final stage. The Jerez ITT (stage 18) is the second race-of-truth.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1935, the Vuelta a España is the third Grand Tour, traditionally run in late summer as the season's third major test. It has progressively repositioned in the season since the 1990s and now anchors August–September. The 2026 edition is the 81st and continues the modern trend of foreign Grand Départs (Portugal 2024, Italy 2025, Monaco 2026).

keyClimbs.name: Collado del Alguacil (Stage 20)
keyClimbs.notes: Final mountain stage summit — last chance for GC fireworks before Granada.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 95th Bretagne Classic — Ouest-France is a one-day UCI WorldTour race held on Sunday August 30, 2026, starting and finishing in Plouay (Morbihan), Brittany. The course is the classic Plouay circuit: rolling Breton terrain with a punchy 14km finishing loop containing the Côte de Restavy and the closing climb into the village. The race traditionally favours puncheurs and Classics specialists able to handle multiple short climbs over a long day. It is the final WorldTour one-day Classic before the late-summer Spanish Grand Tour build-up.
narratives: Plouay's 95th edition: Organised continuously since 1931 by the Comité des Fêtes (now Plouay Cyclisme Organisation), the race is one of the oldest French one-day classics on the calendar.
Vuelta crossroads: Held the same week the Vuelta a España begins, the race is the final hit-out for Classics riders ahead of the autumn block — start list is a mix of WT regulars and form-seekers.
Plouay circuit: Multiple laps of the 14km finishing circuit (Côte de Restavy, closing climb into Plouay) shape the closing tactics — break-or-be-broken on the late laps.
Breton crowd: One of the most fervently supported one-day races in France — the local Plouay community treats it as a regional festival.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Decisive moves typically come on the final two laps of the Plouay circuit. Reduced-bunch finishes are common; pure bunch sprints are rare given the punchy circuit terrain.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1931 as the Grand Prix de Plouay, the race was renamed Bretagne Classic — Ouest-France in 2015 and joined the UCI WorldTour the same year. Sponsored by the Ouest-France newspaper, the event is the largest cycling fixture in Brittany and a regional cultural institution.

races.name: UEC European Gravel Championships — Men Elite
races.description: UEC European Gravel Championships — continental title.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships take place on Sunday August 30, 2026 in Houffalize, Belgium — the established home of the annual Houffa Gravel event since 2021 and the same organising team behind the 2024 and 2025 UCI Gravel World Championships. The elite men's race covers approximately 141km on a new course featuring a 47km lap completed three times, mixing forest tracks, gravel singletrack, and Ardennes climbs. Top-25% finishers in each age category earn automatic qualification for the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships in Nannup, Australia (October).
narratives: Houffalize hosting: Belgium's Ardennes town has built deep gravel-racing pedigree — UCI Gravel Worlds 2024 + 2025 were held here, and the European Champs return in 2026.
Rainbow-jersey qualifier: Top-25% finishers qualify for the UCI Gravel World Championships in Nannup, Australia later that year — making the Euro champs more than a stand-alone title.
Three-lap format: 47km loop covered three times — punchy Ardennes terrain that rewards riders who can sustain effort across repeated climbs.
Forerunner to the road Worlds: Held a few weeks before the road Worlds in Montréal, the gravel champs increasingly attract crossover talent from the road and CX peloton.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Three-lap format means tactical patience pays — early breaks rarely stick when riders see the same climbs three times. The closing lap typically produces the decisive selection.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The UEC Gravel European Championships were inaugurated in 2022 as the discipline gained UCI recognition. Houffalize, a long-established off-road cycling venue, has hosted the parallel UCI Gravel World Championships in 2024 and 2025; the 2027 Euros will move to Lahti, Finland.

rainbowFavorites: VAN AERT Wout (BEL / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — Belgian home-roads favourite on the Ardennes terrain; gravel is the natural extension of his CX-and-classics palmarès.
VERMEERSCH Florian (BEL) — Belgian gravel specialist with UCI Gravel World Series wins; the Houffalize course is in his backyard.
VAKOC Petr (CZE / Specialized Gravel Programme) — dedicated gravel pro with strong UCI Gravel World Series form; thrives on the Ardennes-style punchy terrain.
VAN DEN BERG Marijn (NED / EF Education - EasyPost) — Dutch all-rounder who has hinted at gravel objectives; suits the lap-format Belgian-Ardennes profile.
darkHorses: HERMANS Quinten (BEL / Alpecin-Premier Tech) — Belgian off-road specialist with CX-and-road versatility on home roads.
VAN BAARLE Dylan (NED / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — Paris-Roubaix winner whose cobbles-and-gravel power matches the Ardennes forest tracks.
OPEN BELGIAN FIELD — UCI Gravel World Series qualifiers from the Belgian gravel scene with home-roads advantage on Houffalize terrain.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 UEC European Gravel Championships elite women's race takes place on Sunday August 30, 2026 in Houffalize, Belgium — alongside the men's event. The women's distance is approximately 94km, covering the same 47km Ardennes lap completed twice. The route mixes forest tracks, gravel singletrack and short, punchy climbs typical of the region. Top-25% finishers in each age category earn automatic qualification for the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships in Nannup, Australia (October).
narratives: Houffalize hosting: Same venue as the men's race — Belgium's Ardennes town has hosted UCI Gravel Worlds 2024 + 2025 and the parallel UEC Euros.
Two-lap format for women: 47km lap covered twice, vs three times for the men — same terrain, shorter distance.
Rainbow-jersey qualifier: Top-25% finishers earn World Championships entry in Nannup, Australia later that year.
Growing crossover: As gravel gains traction, more road and CX women are putting it on their calendar — expect a deeper field than recent editions.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Two-lap format compresses tactics — fewer chances to recover from a bad first lap. The closing kilometres on the second lap typically decide the medals.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The UEC Gravel European Championships were inaugurated in 2022. Houffalize, a long-established off-road cycling venue, has hosted the parallel UCI Gravel World Championships in 2024 and 2025; the 2027 Euros will move to Lahti, Finland.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 Tour de Romandie Féminin runs September 4–6 across the western Swiss canton of Vaud — three stages of typical Swiss racing on flat, mountain, and hilly terrain respectively. Created in 2022 to mark the 75th anniversary of the men's Tour de Romandie, the race has matured into one of the autumn calendar's most demanding WWT stage races. The 2026 edition marks the race's shift to September, away from its previous spring slot in May.
narratives: New September slot: 2026 is the first edition held in September, after several years in the spring — repositioned by organisers to give the race its own clear window in the WWT calendar.
Mountain queen stage on Saturday: Per organiser announcement, stage 2 (Sept 5) is the GC-defining mountain day, with the closing climb settling the race.
Swiss precision: Run by the same organisation as the men's Tour de Romandie — high-quality production, scenic Alpine terrain, and a fanbase that takes the race seriously.
Three-day intensity: With only three stages, each is a quasi-decider. There's no easy day.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Saturday's mountain queen stage will likely settle the GC. The Sunday hilly closer can produce late shifts if margins are tight.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Tour de Romandie Féminin was inaugurated in 2022 alongside the men's race's 75th anniversary. Held by the same organisers as the men's Tour de Romandie, it has grown its profile each year and shifted to September for 2026. Run as a UCI Women's WorldTour fixture from inception.

keyClimbs.name: Vaudoise Alps summit finish
keyClimbs.notes: Queen-stage Alpine summit finish — typical Tour de Romandie Féminin GC decider. Final-day climbing decides the maglia jaune.

gcContenders: Top WWT climbers who target Swiss Alpine stage races — Vollering, Niewiadoma, Longo Borghini profiles fit the terrain.
Swiss home-roads riders use this as a season highlight on familiar climbs.
Visma/SD Worx/FDJ-Suez fight for the GC card on the queen-stage summit.
stageHunters: Sprinters and puncheurs target the flatter opening stages.
Breakaway specialists hunt opportunistic stage wins on the transition days.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Classic Lorient Agglomération — Trophée Ceratizit is a one-day UCI Women's WorldTour race held on Saturday September 5, 2026 in the Morbihan region of Brittany. The course covers Breton terrain in and around the Lorient agglomeration — rolling coastal roads, narrow lanes, and a punchy finishing circuit. Held the week after the GP de Plouay weekend, it forms part of Brittany's late-summer professional cycling block — a final WWT one-day Classic before the autumn schedule winds down.
narratives: Brittany's WWT one-dayer: One of the few late-summer WWT one-day races, slotting between the GP de Plouay weekend and the autumn WT calendar.
Lorient terrain: Coastal Brittany — rolling roads, exposed sections that can produce echelons, and a punchy circuit that suits puncheurs and Classics specialists.
Trophée Ceratizit sponsorship: The race title comes from its sponsoring partnership with Ceratizit, a fitting partner for women's cycling (also long-time Ceratizit-WNT WT team sponsor).
Late-season barometer: With Worlds approaching, GC standings on the WWT and form for the Champs Élysées and beyond are at stake.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The closing-circuit terrain shapes the race — expect a reduced-bunch finish rather than a pure sprint, with late attacks possible on the circuit climbs.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 2023, the Classic Lorient Agglomération - Trophée Ceratizit was added to the UCI Women's WorldTour calendar in 2024. It joined the existing Plouay-area women's racing tradition (alongside the Grand Prix de Plouay féminin) to deepen Brittany's late-summer professional women's slate.

keyClimbs.name: Côtes de Bretagne
keyClimbs.notes: Series of short steep Breton climbs on the closing circuit — the punchy character of GP de Plouay-Lorient terrain.

puncheurs: Top WWT puncheurs and classics specialists historically dominate this Breton one-day — Vos, Kopecky, Vollering profiles all suit the rolling terrain.
Belgian and Dutch classics riders have strong palmarès on Breton terrain similar to Plouay.
Young French puncheurs use this as a home-roads target.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Grand Prix Cycliste de Québec is a UCI WorldTour one-day race held on Friday September 11, 2026 in Québec City. The race uses the iconic Quebec city-centre circuit — short laps featuring the punchy Côte de la Montagne and the Côte de la Potasse climbs, repeated approximately 16 times to give a total distance of about 201km with 2,800m+ of elevation gain. Held the same week as its sister race in Montréal and the lead-up to the 2026 UCI Road World Championships in Montréal — making the pair a key Worlds-form indicator for puncheurs and Classics riders.
narratives: Quebec city circuit: The Côte de la Montagne and Côte de la Potasse climbs, climbed ~16 times, define every edition — punchy enough to deny pure sprinters but short enough to keep the bunch together.

Worlds form indicator: With the 2026 UCI Road World Championships starting in Montréal the following week, the Québec Grand Prix is a final fitness check for Classics specialists and puncheurs targeting rainbow jerseys.
Sister race to Montréal: Same weekend as the GP de Montréal — historically the same start list, with one race in Québec on Friday and one in Montréal on Sunday.
Canadian crowd: One of North America's marquee WorldTour fixtures — large crowds throughout the old-Québec finishing area.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Race typically explodes in the closing 30km as climbers and puncheurs launch attacks on the Côte de la Montagne. Reduced-bunch finishes are the norm.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Grand Prix Cycliste de Québec was first held in 2010 alongside its sister event in Montréal, both quickly joining the UCI WorldTour calendar. They are the only Canadian WorldTour fixtures and bring the world's top professional teams to North America each September.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Montagne
keyClimbs.notes: Short steep cobbled climb in Old Quebec — featured multiple times on the closing finishing circuit.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Potasse
keyClimbs.notes: Sharp punchy climb on the closing laps — secondary launching point.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Salaberry
keyClimbs.notes: Final climbing kicker before the finish line on each lap.

puncheurs: POGAČAR Tadej (SVK / UAE Team Emirates - XRG) — Quebec circuit suits his climber-puncheur profile perfectly; the World Champion targets these races as Worlds prep.
EVENEPOEL Remco (BEL / Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) — Olympic champion with explosive punchy-climber strength.
PIDCOCK Thomas (GBR / Pinarello-Q36.5) — climber-puncheur ideal for the Côte de la Montagne repeats.
PEDERSEN Mads (DEN / Lidl - Trek) — survives the climbs, contests the reduced sprint at the line.
VAUQUELIN Kévin (FRA / INEOS Grenadiers) — French all-rounder with Worlds-prep motivation.
HEALY Ben (IRL / EF Education - EasyPost) — Irish breakaway artist on repeated short steep climbs.

topRiders.name: POGAČAR Tadej
topRiders.notes: Worlds prep on Mont Royal terrain — same circuit as the Worlds road race two weeks later.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Grand Prix Cycliste de Montréal is a UCI WorldTour one-day race held on Sunday September 13, 2026 — sister event to the Québec Grand Prix two days earlier. The race traditionally consists of 18 laps of a 12.3km circuit on Mont Royal, covering approximately 220km with 4,740m of elevation gain. The repeated Côte de la Camillien-Houde and Côte de Polytechnique climbs make it one of the hardest one-day races in the calendar. The 2026 edition runs on essentially the same circuit as the 2026 UCI Road World Championships Elite Men's Road Race, which takes place one week later.
narratives: Worlds dress rehearsal: The 2026 GP de Montréal uses largely the same Mont Royal circuit as the World Championships road race the following week — making this the ultimate Worlds-form test.
4,740m of climbing: Among the highest elevation totals of any one-day race outside Lombardia. The repeated Côte de la Camillien-Houde shapes every edition.
Sister race to Québec: Same weekend as the Québec Grand Prix — historically the same start list, with one race in Québec on Friday and one in Montréal on Sunday.
Worlds debut at the same venue: Montréal hosts the UCI Road World Championships from September 20-27, 2026 — only the second time the Worlds have come to North America.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Decisive moves rarely come before lap 14 or 15 — the climbs gradually shed riders, with the final selection typically on the penultimate lap. Pure climbers and Classics puncheurs both feature.
raceDetails.historicalContext: Founded in 2010 alongside the Québec Grand Prix, the GP de Montréal has been a UCI WorldTour fixture since the series' early years. The 2026 edition takes on added significance as the lead-up race to the UCI Road World Championships, held on essentially the same Montréal circuit.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Camillien-Houde
keyClimbs.notes: Canonical Mont Royal launching climb — short enough to attack from far out, steep enough to thin the elite group. Multiple repeats decide the race.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Polytechnique
keyClimbs.notes: Short steep punch through the Université de Montréal grounds; secondary launching point on every Mont Royal circuit lap.

puncheurs: POGAČAR Tadej (SVK / UAE Team Emirates - XRG) — multi-time GP Montréal winner; the Mont Royal climbs are tailor-made for his climber-puncheur profile.
EVENEPOEL Remco (BEL / Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) — Olympic champion targeting Worlds prep on the same circuit two weeks before the road race.
PIDCOCK Thomas (GBR / Pinarello-Q36.5) — climber-puncheur ideally suited to Camillien-Houde repeats.
PEDERSEN Mads (DEN / Lidl - Trek) — 2019 World Champion who survives the climbs and contests the reduced sprint.
VAN DER POEL Mathieu (NED / Alpecin-Premier Tech) — 2023 World Champion; the Mont Royal climbs are short enough to follow.
HEALY Ben (IRL / EF Education - EasyPost) — Irish breakaway specialist; courses with repeated short steep climbs are his speciality.
SKJELMOSE Mattias (DEN / Lidl - Trek) — Danish puncheur-climber, second card behind Pedersen.

topRiders.name: POGAČAR Tadej
topRiders.notes: Mont Royal climbs match Pogačar's strengths — direct Worlds dress rehearsal.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 UCI Road World Championships Elite Men's Individual Time Trial takes place on Sunday September 20, 2026 in Montréal, Canada — the opening day of a week of competition. The 39.9km TT course winds through Montréal city centre and along the river, with technical urban sections, rolling terrain, and elevation changes that reward power riders who can also handle pacing on undulating courses. Riders compete for the rainbow jersey — the highest individual honour in road cycling for the calendar year.
narratives: Rainbow jersey on the line: The TT World Championship is one of the sport's most prestigious individual titles — the winner wears the rainbow jersey in every TT for a full year.
Montréal venue: Second time the Worlds visit North America (the first time since Hamilton 2003). The TT course uses the same general circuit as the GP de Montréal one week earlier.
39.9km test: Long enough to separate specialists from generalists, technical enough to penalize riders who only ride straight-line power efforts.
Field depth: The Worlds TT consistently draws the strongest specialists from the WT plus dedicated TT teams — expect 60–80 starters across the elite men's start list.
raceDetails.watchNotes: TT specialists typically separate from generalists by the second time check. The closing 10km — with its short urban climbs — has the highest variance and often shapes the final podium.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The UCI Road World Championship Individual Time Trial has been contested since 1994 (a separate event from the Worlds Road Race). It is held annually on the opening weekend of the Road Worlds week. Montréal 2026 is only the second North-American Worlds since 2003 (Hamilton).

rainbowFavorites: GANNA Filippo (ITA / Ineos Grenadiers) — multi-time TT World Champion and the benchmark on a power-and-pacing course like Montréal.
EVENEPOEL Remco (BEL / Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) — reigning Olympic TT champion and the rider to beat on rolling-urban TT courses.
TARLING Joshua (GBR / Ineos Grenadiers) — emerging British power specialist who has closed the gap to Ganna and Evenepoel.
MCNULTY Brandon (USA / UAE Team Emirates - XRG) — versatile TT-and-climbing specialist on home-continent roads.
BISSEGGER Stefan (SUI / Decathlon CMA CGM Team) — pure power TT specialist who thrives on technical urban courses.
darkHorses: VAN AERT Wout (BEL / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — second Belgian card if Evenepoel falters; classics-power profile suits the rolling Montréal terrain.
WÆRENSKJOLD Søren (NOR / Uno-X Mobility) — Norwegian time-trial-strong sprinter capable of medal contention.
FOSS Tobias (NOR / Ineos Grenadiers) — 2022 TT world champion still in the medal mix on the right course.

keySectors.name: Côte de Camillien-Houde (truncated)
keySectors.description: Short section of the Mont Royal climb shared with the road race — modest gradient on the TT course but a tempo-disruptor for power riders.

keySectors.name: Vieux-Montréal cobble-style streets
keySectors.description: Technical urban section through Old Montréal — punishes anyone mishandling cornering on TT bikes.

topRiders.name: GANNA Filippo
topRiders.notes: Reigning multi-time TT World Champion; benchmark on rolling-power courses.

topRiders.name: EVENEPOEL Remco
topRiders.notes: Olympic TT champion targeting his next rainbow on Montréal's rolling course.

topRiders.name: MCNULTY Brandon
topRiders.notes: Versatile TT-and-climbing US rider on home-continent roads.

topRiders.name: VAN AERT Wout
topRiders.notes: Belgium's second TT card; suits rolling Montréal terrain.

topRiders.name: WÆRENSKJOLD Søren
topRiders.notes: Norwegian TT specialist in medal mix.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 UCI Road World Championships Team Time Trial Mixed Relay takes place on Tuesday September 22, 2026 in Montréal — the mid-week showcase of the Worlds programme. Each national team fields three men and three women on the same 40.2km course (riders relay between gender groups), with the combined finishing time decided by the slower rider in each handover. The format places a premium on team execution and balance across both gender squads — strong nations need depth across both their men's and women's TT pools to medal.
narratives: Format that rewards depth: With three men and three women on a 40.2km course, the format favours nations with strong TT specialists across both squads — not just one star rider.

Mid-week showcase: Tuesday is when the Mixed Relay TT typically falls in the Worlds programme — a TV-friendly format that draws strong viewing as the championships build toward the road races at week's end.
Montréal venue: Same 40.2km course as the individual TTs earlier in the week — riders may have already raced their individual events before regrouping for the relay.

Relatively young event: First held at the Worlds in 2019, the Mixed Relay TT is still maturing as a discipline. Nations are still finding the right balance between specialist and rider-rotation strategies.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch the time-check splits as the riders transition between gender groups — large gaps can open if a team has imbalanced TT depth. Splits are normally taken at the women→men handover and at the men→finish handover.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Team Time Trial Mixed Relay was added to the UCI Road World Championships in 2019, replacing the previous trade-team TTT format. It is the only team event at the Worlds where nations (rather than commercial teams) compete. Past winners include Netherlands, Switzerland, Germany, and Italy.

keySectors.name: Montréal city-centre flat-and-rolling sections
keySectors.description: Mixed urban TTT terrain — fast and flat sections with technical turns reward team cohesion and TT-bike handling.

keySectors.name: Mont Royal lower slopes
keySectors.description: Truncated section of the iconic Mont Royal climb — modest gradients but a tempo disruptor for relay teams.

nationalTeams: Italy — historic TTT/Mixed Relay power, with Ganna anchoring the men's leg.
Belgium — Evenepoel anchor + Van Aert + strong women's squad (Kopecky etc.).
Switzerland — perennial Mixed Relay medal candidate with Bissegger/Reusser.
Netherlands — deep across men's and women's rosters.
Australia / USA — strong national TT depth across genders, home-continent advantage for USA.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 UCI Road World Championships Elite Men's Road Race is the climactic Sunday September 27, 2026 finale of the Worlds week in Montréal, Canada. The race starts in Brossard (south of Montréal) and finishes on the iconic Mont Royal circuit — essentially the same closing course as the GP de Montréal two weeks earlier. Riders contest the rainbow jersey on a punishing climbing-heavy circuit featuring repeated ascents of the Côte de la Camillien-Houde and Côte de Polytechnique. This is the third time the Worlds road race has been held in Canada (after Hamilton 2003) and only the second time North America has hosted since the modern era.
narratives: Rainbow jersey: The Elite Men's Road Race is the most coveted single-day title in cycling — winner wears the rainbow jersey in every road race for a full year.
Mont Royal climbing: The repeated Côte de la Camillien-Houde climb is the canonical move-launching point. Punchy climbers dominate this kind of terrain.
Brossard Grand Départ: Starting south of Montréal, the route brings riders up to the city for the punishing finishing circuit — Worlds tradition of long-distance road races (often 250km+).
Third Canadian Worlds: Following Hamilton 2003 and Montréal 1974, the 2026 race marks Montréal's second hosting of the Worlds. The largest sporting event in Montréal since the 1976 Olympics.
GP de Montréal as prelude: The GP de Montréal one-day race two weeks earlier runs on essentially the same finishing circuit — making it a near-perfect form indicator.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The closing 3 laps of the Mont Royal circuit decide every modern Worlds road race held on this style of course. Watch for late attacks on Camillien-Houde or splits forming on the steep ramps of Polytechnique. The race typically explodes inside the final 60km.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1921 as the amateur World Championship and 1927 as the professional event, the UCI Road World Championship Road Race is the sport's oldest individual title. Notable past winners include Eddy Merckx (three rainbow jerseys), Bernard Hinault, Greg LeMond, Peter Sagan (three consecutive), and Mathieu van der Poel. The 2025 edition was held in Kigali, Rwanda — Africa's first Worlds. The 2026 edition returns the championships to North America for the third time.

rainbowFavorites: POGAČAR Tadej (SVK / UAE Team Emirates - XRG) — three-time defending World Champion; Mont Royal's repeated climbs are tailor-made for his climber-puncheur profile.
EVENEPOEL Remco (BEL / Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) — Olympic road race champion; the rainbow jersey is the missing major he chases on punchy terrain.
VAN DER POEL Mathieu (NED / Alpecin-Premier Tech) — 2023 World Champion; the Mont Royal climbs are short enough for him to follow the elite group and unleash his finishing kick.
PIDCOCK Thomas (GBR / Pinarello-Q36.5 Pro Cycling Team) — climber-puncheur ideally suited to the Camillien-Houde repeats; consistent Monument and Worlds podium threat.
PEDERSEN Mads (DEN / Lidl - Trek) — 2019 World Champion who has rebuilt his climbing legs; survives the climbs and out-sprints the reduced bunch.
darkHorses: DEL TORO Isaac (MEX / UAE Team Emirates - XRG) — climber-puncheur with a Mexican passport — solo card with no team obligations.
VINGEGAARD Jonas (DEN / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — Tour de France winner; Worlds course suits him only if the race becomes a war of attrition.
HEALY Ben (IRL / EF Education - EasyPost) — Irish breakaway specialist; courses with repeated short steep climbs are his speciality.
SKJELMOSE Mattias (DEN / Lidl - Trek) — Danish puncheur-climber, second card behind Pedersen on a course that favours both.
ONLEY Oscar (GBR / INEOS Grenadiers) — British second card behind Pidcock; emerging climbing ability for a tactically open finale.
VAUQUELIN Kévin (FRA / INEOS Grenadiers) — French all-rounder/classics rider who has shown big races suit his profile.
MARTINEZ Lenny (FRA / Bahrain - Victorious) — pure climber who can survive Mont Royal repetitions; long-shot rainbow contender.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Camillien-Houde
keyClimbs.notes: The canonical move-launching climb on Mont Royal — short enough to attack from far out, steep enough to thin the elite group. Multiple repeats decide the race.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Polytechnique
keyClimbs.notes: Short steep punch through the Université de Montréal grounds; secondary launching point on every Mont Royal circuit lap.

topRiders.name: POGAČAR Tadej
topRiders.notes: Three-time defending World Champion; Mont Royal climbs match his strengths perfectly.

topRiders.name: EVENEPOEL Remco
topRiders.notes: Olympic road champion chasing the road rainbow jersey.

topRiders.name: VAN DER POEL Mathieu
topRiders.notes: 2023 World Champion; Mont Royal climbs short enough to follow.

topRiders.name: PIDCOCK Thomas
topRiders.notes: Climber-puncheur perfectly suited to Camillien-Houde repeats.

topRiders.name: PEDERSEN Mads
topRiders.notes: 2019 World Champion with rebuilt climbing legs.

topRiders.name: DEL TORO Isaac
topRiders.notes: Climber-puncheur with no national team obligations.

topRiders.name: VINGEGAARD Jonas
topRiders.notes: Tour winner — needs an attritional race to make Mont Royal selective enough.

topRiders.name: HEALY Ben
topRiders.notes: Irish breakaway artist on repeated short climbs.

topRiders.name: SKJELMOSE Mattias

primary.notes: FloBikes Canada deep link to 2026 Il Lombardia.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 120th Il Lombardia — the 'race of the falling leaves' — takes place on Saturday October 10, 2026 as the fifth and final Monument of the season. The 2026 route covers approximately 240km through the spectacular autumn scenery of Lombardy, including the lakes Como and Lecco. The course traverses signature climbs — the Madonna del Ghisallo, Muro di Sormano (when included), Sella di Osigo, and the climactic Civiglio + San Fermo della Battaglia finishing pair when the race finishes in Como. Approximately 4,500m+ of climbing makes Il Lombardia the toughest Monument of the season.
narratives: Last Monument of the season: As the final of cycling's five Monuments, Il Lombardia closes the WorldTour calendar with one of the most dramatic one-day races of the year. Climbers traditionally feature heavily.
Autumn scenery: The race traverses Lake Como and the Lombard foothills as the leaves turn — visually, one of the most iconic days on the calendar.
120th edition: Among the oldest one-day Classics in cycling, Il Lombardia has been continuously contested since 1905 (with WWI interruptions). The 2026 edition is the 120th running.

Climber's race: Unlike the spring Monuments (Roubaix, Flanders, Liège), Lombardia rewards pure climbers — its 4,500m+ of elevation across long ascents like the Ghisallo and the closing pair makes it a peakclimbing test.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Decisive moves usually come on the Civiglio (climbed ~20km from finish) and the final San Fermo della Battaglia. Solo escapes are common — only Lombardia's combination of climbing and descending savvy can produce them. The closing descent into Como has historically been one of the most dangerous in the calendar.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1905, Il Lombardia is one of cycling's five Monuments and the oldest continuously contested one-day Italian race after Milano-Sanremo. Multiple-time winners include Fausto Coppi (five wins), Sean Kelly, and modern-era champions. Tadej Pogačar set a record in 2025 by becoming the first rider to win five consecutive editions of any single Monument. The 2026 route avoids the steep Muro di Sormano descent where Remco Evenepoel crashed in 2020.

keyClimbs.name: Madonna del Ghisallo
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic chapel-topped climb in the Lecco province — Lombardia's spiritual home and traditional mid-stage launching point.

keyClimbs.name: Muro di Sormano
keyClimbs.notes: Notorious 1.9km wall with sustained gradients above 15% and ramps to 25%. Often the final move-launching climb of the race.

keyClimbs.name: Civiglio
keyClimbs.notes: Steep climb above Como — typical late-race selection point with 15km still to ride after the summit.

keyClimbs.name: San Fermo della Battaglia
keyClimbs.notes: Final short kicker before the Como finish — last chance for a solo attacker to gap the chasers.

puncheurs: POGAČAR Tadej (SVK / UAE Team Emirates - XRG) — four-time Lombardia winner; the Lake Como climbs are tailor-made for his climber-puncheur profile.
JORGENSON Matteo (USA / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — Visma's strongest classics-and-Lombardia card with the punchy climber profile.
CHRISTEN Jan (SUI / UAE Team Emirates - XRG) — Pogačar's likely best teammate for the Italian Monument.
SEIXAS Paul (FRA / Decathlon - CMA CGM Team) — emerging French puncheur-climber whose explosive style fits the Sormano/Civiglio gradients.
darkHorses: WIDAR Jarno (BEL / Lotto) — Belgian climber-GC project on terrain that rewards a long-range attack.
Italian Monument specialists with home-roads motivation for the Race of the Falling Leaves.

races.name: UCI Gravel World Championships — Women Elite
races.description: UCI Gravel World Championships — rainbow jersey on the line.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships Elite Women's race takes place on Saturday October 10, 2026 in Nannup, Western Australia — the first time the Gravel Worlds is held outside Europe, and the first time the championship visits the Southern Hemisphere. The women's elite course is one of three official routes unveiled by the UCI in April 2026, shorter than the men's elite race held the following day. Nannup has hosted the SEVEN gravel race (UCI Gravel World Series) since 2018, making it well-established terrain in the gravel ecosystem.
narratives: First Southern Hemisphere Worlds: 2026 marks the first time the UCI Gravel World Championships are held outside Europe — a major step in the discipline's globalisation.
Saturday women's elite: The women's elite race takes Saturday (October 10) while the men race Sunday — same circuit, different distance.
Nannup terrain: Australian forest gravel — faster, more flowing than typical European Ardennes gravel. Expect early breakaways and selective climbing.
Growing crossover: Recent UCI Gravel Worlds have drawn major road and CX names — the women's elite race typically draws an even more diverse start list than the men's.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Faster, flowing Australian gravel rewards riders who can sustain power and pick lines through forest trails. Compared to Ardennes gravel, the breakaway typically forms earlier here.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The UCI Gravel World Championships were inaugurated in 2022 in Veneto, Italy. The 2024 and 2025 editions were held in Houffalize, Belgium; Nannup 2026 is the first non-European edition. The women's elite race has grown in field depth each year as more WT and CX women add gravel to their calendar.

rainbowFavorites: VOLLERING Demi (NED / Lidl - Trek) — climber-puncheur whose endurance suits long off-road racing; gravel transition is becoming a peloton-wide trend.
VOS Marianne (NED / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — all-terrain legend with cyclocross and road titles; gravel is the natural off-road extension.
BRAND Lucinda (NED / Lidl - Trek) — multi-discipline specialist (CX World Champion lineage) who has long mixed gravel into her calendar.
VAN ANROOIJ Shirin (NED / Lidl - Trek) — strong off-road racer with CX background; the WA terrain suits her power-and-handling profile.
darkHorses: KOPECKY Lotte (BEL / SD Worx) — multi-time World Champion (road, track, CX); has hinted at gravel objectives and brings WT-elite engine.
HARVEY Mikayla (NZL) — Oceania rider with home-region advantage in Australia; New Zealand background suits remote off-road racing.
WIEBES Lorena (NED / SD Worx) — sprinter-classics rider, dark horse if course is fast-and-flat enough.
AUSTRALIAN OPEN FIELD — UCI Gravel World Series qualifiers from AUS/NZ region with home-roads advantage on local terrain.

UCI Gravel World Championships — Men Elite
races.description: UCI Gravel World Championships — rainbow jersey on the line.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships Elite Men's race takes place on Sunday October 11, 2026 in Nannup, Western Australia — the first time the Gravel Worlds is held outside Europe, and the first time the championship visits the Southern Hemisphere. The men's elite course is the longest of three official courses unveiled by the UCI in April 2026. Nannup, a small town in the Southern Forests region of WA, has hosted the SEVEN gravel race since 2018, making it familiar terrain for the UCI Gravel World Series ecosystem. Top-25% finishers in each qualifying age category earn rainbow-jersey eligibility through the UCI Gravel World Series qualifier circuit.
narratives: First Southern Hemisphere Worlds: 2026 marks the first time the UCI Gravel World Championships are held outside Europe, raising the discipline's global profile.
Nannup pedigree: Nannup has hosted the SEVEN gravel race (UCI Gravel World Series) since 2018 — local riders have known the terrain for years, but the international peloton arrives fresh.
Three official courses: The UCI unveiled the championship's three courses (Elite Men, Elite Women, Age Group) on April 15, 2026 — a more substantial route plan than recent editions.
Late-season rainbow jersey: As the gravel calendar's pinnacle event, Nannup 2026 closes the gravel season with the most prestigious title in the discipline.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Australian forest gravel is faster and more flowing than European Ardennes-style gravel — expect breakaways that form earlier and stick longer. The race is held alongside the women's elite on Saturday October 10.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The UCI Gravel World Championships were inaugurated in 2022 in Veneto, Italy as the discipline gained UCI recognition. The 2024 and 2025 editions were both held in Houffalize, Belgium. Nannup 2026 is the first non-European edition; the championships return to Europe in subsequent years.

rainbowFavorites: VAN DER POEL Mathieu (NED / Alpecin-Premier Tech) — Gravel World Champion (defending the rainbow jersey on his terms); the Australian terrain and timing favours his style of long-distance off-road racing.
VAN AERT Wout (BEL / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — Belgian off-road powerhouse who has steadily added gravel objectives to his calendar; suits hard-pack Australian fire roads.
VAKOC Petr (CZE / Specialized Gravel Programme) — dedicated gravel professional with strong UCI Gravel World Series form; comes into Nannup with the calendar's most gravel-specific preparation.
VERMEERSCH Florian (BEL) — UCI Gravel World Series regular and previous Gravel Worlds medallist; Belgian classics power translates to gravel.
darkHorses: WURF Cameron (AUS) — home-roads ironman-turned-gravel-rider with deep familiarity of the WA terrain.
MORTON Lachlan (AUS / EF Education - EasyPost) — ultra-endurance Australian known for off-road exploits; home crowd and long distance favour him.
GANNA Filippo (ITA / Ineos Grenadiers) — power TT specialist whose engine could fit long flat gravel sections, though singletrack remains an open question.
VAN BAARLE Dylan (NED / Team Visma | Lease a Bike) — Paris-Roubaix winner with cobbles-to-gravel crossover credentials.
AUSTRALIAN OPEN FIELD — UCI Gravel World Series qualifiers from the Australia/NZ region with home advantage on local terrain.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Tour of Guangxi is the final WorldTour stage race of the 2026 season, held October 13–18 in southern China. Six stages traverse the karst mountains of the Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region, mixing sprint-friendly opener stages, a couple of undulating mid-race days, and a traditional mountain queen-stage finale that historically settles the GC. Stage arrivals fall in the morning European time (afternoon local Chinese time) — making the race a season-closer for fans watching live in Europe.
narratives: Season-closing WT stage race: Tour of Guangxi closes the men's WorldTour stage-race calendar. Riders chasing year-end UCI points use it as their final scoring opportunity.
Karst-mountain finale: The traditional GC-decisive mountain stage in the karst formations near Nongla is the race's signature day — usually stage 4 or 5.
Six-stage format: Three sprint-friendly days, two undulating days, one mountain day, one closing-day variant — a balanced course designed for a mix of sprinters and climbers.
Asian Tour finale: Held alongside the Tour of Chongming Island (women's WT) and Tour of Guangxi Women (one-day) — Asian Tour culmination week of October.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The Nongla / Mashan-area karst-mountain queen stage is the must-watch — typically the only meaningful GC selection. Otherwise, expect sprint stages with bonus-second tactical layers.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 2017 as the WorldTour's first race in China, the Tour of Guangxi has been the closing WorldTour stage race ever since. The race is organised by the same group behind the Tour of Yorkshire and other international stage events. Past editions have featured bunch-sprint days in the city of Nanning and signature climbs in the Nongla limestone region.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Tour of Chongming Island is a three-stage UCI Women's WorldTour race held October 13–15, 2026 on Shanghai's eponymous island in the Yangtze River estuary. The flat terrain of Chongming Island — a Yangtze-delta agricultural area — produces three sprint-friendly stages where crosswinds and bonus seconds matter more than climbing. The race is one of the two final WWT events of the season (alongside the Tour of Guangxi Women's one-day) and a final scoring opportunity for sprinters chasing year-end UCI points.
narratives: Sprinters' race in the WWT calendar: Three flat stages on Chongming Island reward pure sprinters and their lead-out trains — rare in the WWT, which is increasingly climbing-heavy.
Crosswinds and bonus seconds: With elevation negligible, GC margins come down to crosswind echelons and intermediate sprint bonus seconds — tactical layers that reward strong teams.
Season-closing window: As one of the final WWT events of the year, riders chasing year-end points (and teams chasing year-end WT promotion/relegation) come here for final scoring.
Yangtze delta venue: The eco-island setting (Chongming is a protected ecological zone) makes the race visually distinctive from the rest of the WWT calendar.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Each stage is a sprint day on paper — but crosswind splits can dramatically reshape the GC. Watch for echelons forming early on the exposed island roads. The stage-3 final stretch toward Chongming City typically produces the sharpest sprint.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 2010 (men's race) and expanded to a women's-WT race in 2017, the Tour of Chongming Island was cancelled for several years during the COVID-19 pandemic and gradually returned to the calendar. The 2026 edition is part of a steady rebuild of the race's WT status.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Tour of Guangxi Women is a one-day UCI Women's WorldTour race held on Sunday October 18, 2026 in the Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region of southern China — the season's final WWT race. The 2026 edition covers 134.3km on a hillier course than recent years, featuring a 1.4km signature climb at 11.7% average tackled five times in the closing kilometres. The repeated punchy ascent makes this race a puncheur's specialty rather than a sprinter's day.
narratives: Season finale: Final WWT race of 2026. Year-end UCI points, team WT promotion/relegation calculations, and last-chance form for season-ending awards all converge here.
Steeper than recent editions: 2026 route is deliberately hillier — 1.4km @ 11.7% climbed 5× in the closing kilometres replaces the previously sprint-friendly profile.
Border-region setting: The race takes place near the Guangxi-Vietnam border, in the karst-mountain landscape that defines the region.
Same week as men's Tour of Guangxi: The men's six-stage Tour of Guangxi runs October 13-18, with the women's one-day on the closing Sunday.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The 1.4km / 11.7% closing-circuit climb shapes the race — selections form on its first and second laps; the decisive move typically goes on the third or fourth ascent. Pure sprinters can't survive the repeated climbing.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held as a UCI Women's WorldTour fixture in 2017, the Tour of Guangxi Women was on hiatus during the COVID-19 pandemic and returned to the WT calendar in recent years. The 2026 hillier course reflects organisers' goal of making the race a true GC test rather than a procession sprint.
